reference: – There's not fact checking, and then there's ... not even using your brain. A newscast on Bay Area Fox affiliate KTVU apparently fell for a (racist and offensive) joke, incorrectly reporting that the pilots of the plane that crashed at San Francisco airport were named "Sum Ting Wong," "Ho Lee Fuk," "Bang Ding Ow," and "Wi Tu Lo," SF Weekly reports. The network later apologized, but said an NTSB official in Washington had "confirmed" them. It wasn't exactly an official. Per the NTSB: "Earlier today, in response to an inquiry from a media outlet, a summer intern acted outside the scope of his authority when he erroneously confirmed the names of the flight crew on the aircraft." The kicker, as Mediabistro notes, is that KTVU had just been boasting about its "100% accurate" coverage of the disaster earlier this week.

final_summary:
Revised Summary:

Anthony Scaramucci, the newly appointed White House communications director, has found himself embroiled in controversy after a financial disclosure form revealed his substantial earnings from his investment firm, SkyBridge Capital. According to the document, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in SkyBridge in the first six months of this year, in addition to his $5 million salary. This revelation has raised concerns about potential conflicts of interest, given that Scaramucci still stands to profit from the sale of SkyBridge, which is currently under review by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States due to its ties to HNA Group.

Scaramucci's financial disclosure form, which was obtained by Politico, also shows that he has assets worth up to $85 million, including a minority stake in the New York Mets worth around $5 million and several real estate investments. His net worth includes a significant amount of debt, with liabilities ranging from $2.5 million to $7 million. The disclosure has sparked outrage, particularly given Scaramucci's vow to root out leakers within the White House. On Twitter, Scaramucci threatened to contact the FBI and the Justice Department, labeling the leak of his financial information a felony. However, it remains unclear what specific information was leaked, as Scaramucci did not elaborate.

Scaramucci's situation is complicated by his ongoing involvement with SkyBridge, which he founded in 2005. Although he stepped down as managing director in January, he continued to collect a salary until joining the Export-Import Bank (Ex-Im) in June. Despite being on unpaid leave from Ex-Im since his start date, Scaramucci has maintained access to the White House through his security clearance, allowing him to visit the building freely. The controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances has drawn attention from watchdog groups, with Austin Evers, executive director of American Oversight, stating that it is clear that Scaramucci will receive payment from the sale of SkyBridge.

The sale, valued at between $200 million and $230 million, is expected to be finalized in the third quarter of this year. As the situation unfolds, Scaramucci's relationship with White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus has become increasingly strained. Scaramucci tagged Priebus in his tweet threatening to contact the FBI, suggesting a rift between the two men. Meanwhile, the White House has referred questions about Scaramucci's SkyBridge investment to the Treasury Department, which has yet to comment on the matter. In Washington, Scaramucci's appointment to the White House communications director role has been met with skepticism, particularly given his ties to President Trump and his own financial interests. As the controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances continues to grow, it remains to be seen how he will navigate these challenges and maintain his position within the administration.

history.draft:
Revised Summary:

Anthony Scaramucci, the newly appointed White House communications director, has found himself embroiled in controversy after a financial disclosure form revealed his substantial earnings from his investment firm, SkyBridge Capital. According to the document, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in SkyBridge in the first six months of this year, in addition to his $5 million salary. This revelation has raised concerns about potential conflicts of interest, given that Scaramucci still stands to profit from the sale of SkyBridge, which is currently under review by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States due to its ties to HNA Group.

Scaramucci's financial disclosure form, which was obtained by Politico, also shows that he has assets worth up to $85 million, including a minority stake in the New York Mets worth around $5 million and several real estate investments. His net worth includes a significant amount of debt, with liabilities ranging from $2.5 million to $7 million. The disclosure has sparked outrage, particularly given Scaramucci's vow to root out leakers within the White House. On Twitter, Scaramucci threatened to contact the FBI and the Justice Department, labeling the leak of his financial information a felony. However, it remains unclear what specific information was leaked, as Scaramucci did not elaborate.

Scaramucci's situation is complicated by his ongoing involvement with SkyBridge, which he founded in 2005. Although he stepped down as managing director in January, he continued to collect a salary until joining the Export-Import Bank (Ex-Im) in June. Despite being on unpaid leave from Ex-Im since his start date, Scaramucci has maintained access to the White House through his security clearance, allowing him to visit the building freely. The controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances has drawn attention from watchdog groups, with Austin Evers, executive director of American Oversight, stating that it is clear that Scaramucci will receive payment from the sale of SkyBridge.

The sale, valued at between $200 million and $230 million, is expected to be finalized in the third quarter of this year. As the situation unfolds, Scaramucci's relationship with White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus has become increasingly strained. Scaramucci tagged Priebus in his tweet threatening to contact the FBI, suggesting a rift between the two men. Meanwhile, the White House has referred questions about Scaramucci's SkyBridge investment to the Treasury Department, which has yet to comment on the matter. In Washington, Scaramucci's appointment to the White House communications director role has been met with skepticism, particularly given his ties to President Trump and his own financial interests. As the controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances continues to grow, it remains to be seen how he will navigate these challenges and maintain his position within the administration.

reference: – New White House communications director Anthony Scaramucci was not happy about what he believed was a leak of information from his financial disclosure Wednesday—though it later emerged that the form was available to the public. "In light of the leak of my financial disclosure info which is a felony. I will be contacting @FBI and @TheJusticeDept #swamp @Reince45," Scaramucci said in a now-deleted tweet, per the Daily Beast. Scaramucci later denied suggestions that his tagging of Reince Priebus was a declaration of war on the White House chief of staff. "Wrong! Tweet was public notice to leakers that all Sr Adm officials are helping to end illegal leaks," Scaramucci said in a second tweet. The disclosure information believed to have enraged Scaramucci appeared in a Politico report late Wednesday. According to the report, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in the SkyBridge Capital plus more than $5 million in salary from Jan. 1, 2016, to the end of June this year, when he began a short-lived stint at the federal Export-Import Bank. The New York Times notes that Scaramucci filed the disclosure form June 23 and under federal law, it would have been available to the public 30 days later. "They aren't in process yet," Scaramucci wrote to the Times after being asked why he thought the report had been leaked. He didn't respond after being told it would have been available July 23.

final_summary:
Tensions surrounding the independence referendum in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq escalated in the period leading up to the vote, which was set for September 25, 2017. Stakeholders weighed in on the historic event through various channels. 

President Masoud Barzani's stance on the referendum was one of support, highlighting the significance of the democratic process in shaping the future of the Kurdish people. Conversely, the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan raised concerns about the timing of the vote, suggesting it could destabilize the region and exacerbate internal divisions. 

The Gorran movement, a rival faction, also opposed the referendum, characterizing it as a divisive measure that would primarily benefit the ruling elite. In response, the Rudaw News Network emphasized the historical grievances of the Kurdish people against the Iraqi government, framing the referendum as an opportunity for self-determination.

As the vote drew near, the Turkish government grew more vocal in its opposition, with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan warning that Turkey would not recognize the outcome and threatening economic repercussions. Iran also expressed disapproval, viewing the referendum as a threat to regional stability. Despite these warnings, preparations for the vote continued unabated.

By the time registration closed, over 3 million eligible voters had signed up, surpassing initial projections. Many Kurds were eager to participate, seeking to influence the future of their region. On the eve of the referendum, Prime Minister Nechirvan Barzani reaffirmed his commitment to the vote, describing it as a pivotal moment for the Kurdish people.

Millions of Kurds cast their ballots on September 25, 2017, with nearly 93% voting in favor of independence. The international community remained divided regarding the legitimacy and implications of the result.

history.draft:
As tensions escalated in the run-up to the long-awaited independence referendum in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq, scheduled for September 25, 2017, various stakeholders took to social media to express their views on the historic vote. Masoud Barzani, the then-President of the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG), tweeted on August 29, 2017, "The people of Kurdistan will decide their future through a democratic process." He emphasized that the referendum would be a crucial step towards achieving the right to self-determination for the Kurdish people.

On the other hand, the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK) party, led by Jalal Talabani's successor, Bafel Talabani, expressed concerns about the timing of the referendum. In a statement released on September 1, 2017, the PUK argued that holding the referendum at this time could undermine regional stability and create divisions within the country. They called for a delay in the vote until after the defeat of ISIS.

Meanwhile, the Gorran movement, a rival party to the KDP, also voiced its opposition to the referendum. On September 5, 2017, the party's leader, Nawshirwan Mustafa, stated that the vote was a "divisive" move that would only serve the interests of the ruling elite. He urged the KRG to reconsider its decision and engage in dialogue with Baghdad instead.

In response to these criticisms, the KDP-affiliated Rudaw News Network published an article on September 10, 2017, highlighting the historical grievances of the Kurdish people against the Iraqi government. The piece emphasized that the referendum was a chance for Kurds to exercise their right to self-determination and determine their own destiny.

As the referendum drew near, the Turkish government began to take a more assertive stance against the vote. On September 12, 2017, Turkey's President Recep Tayyip Erdogan warned that his country would not recognize the outcome of the referendum and threatened to impose economic sanctions if it went ahead as planned. This warning was echoed by Iran, which also opposed the referendum and saw it as a threat to regional stability.

Despite these warnings, the KRG pressed ahead with preparations for the vote. On September 20, 2017, the KRG announced that over 3 million eligible voters had registered for the referendum, exceeding expectations. The turnout was expected to be high, with many Kurds eager to cast their ballots and shape the future of their region.

On the eve of the referendum, September 24, 2017, the KRG's Prime Minister, Nechirvan Barzani, reiterated his commitment to the vote, stating that it was a "historic moment" for the Kurdish people. As the polls opened on September 25, 2017, millions of Kurds flocked to voting centers across the region to exercise their right to self-determination. The outcome of the referendum would ultimately see around 93% of participants voting in favor of independence, but the international community remained divided on the legitimacy and implications of the result.

reference: – This year’s Indy 500 found its instant replay moment, and it looks like it could have come straight from a Michael Bay film. Driver Scott Dixon walked away Sunday from the nightmare crash captured in this video. (Replay starts about 47 seconds in.) It also can be seen in this tweeted video. The LA Times reports that the accident began when driver Jay Howard lost control of his vehicle a little more than a quarter into the 200-lap race. He collided with Dixon, whose car was sent flying through the air before smashing into an inner retaining wall. Though Dixon’s vehicle was demolished, both drivers survived the fiery accident and were released after a check-up at the track's medical center. Afterward, Dixon said he was “a little bit beaten up” and “glad everybody was OK," calling the incident "definitely a wild ride.” The race continued on, with Takuma Sato later becoming the first Japanese driver ever to finish first, reports the AP. Sato held off Helio Castroneves, who was going for his fourth Indy win.

final_summary:
Ethan Finkelstein, a Tufts University freshman home on Thanksgiving break, witnessed a disturbing discovery at the Macy's parade. He noticed a strip of confetti stuck to his friend's coat, bearing what appeared to be a social security number, followed by several other confetti strips with similar sensitive information, including phone numbers, addresses, and even details from police incident reports. Finkelstein and his friends picked up more confetti and discovered that it contained information about a pipe bomb threat in the Kings Grant area, as well as details about Mitt Romney's motorcade from the final presidential debate held at Hofstra University in Nassau County last month.

PIX11 News reported that the confetti strips were later confirmed to be from the Nassau County Police Department, revealing the names of undercover police officers, their social security numbers, dates of birth, and other highly sensitive personal data. The revelation shocked Finkelstein and raised questions about how such sensitive information ended up in the confetti.

An investigation was launched by the Nassau County Police Department, led by Inspector Kenneth Lack, who stated that the department would review its procedures for disposing of sensitive documents. Meanwhile, PIX11 News questioned Macy's, the sponsor of the parade, about the origin of the confetti strips, but Macy's claimed to use commercially manufactured, multicolored confetti, not shredded paper.

In a separate development, PIX11 News reported that federal prosecutors revealed last week that they had begun reassembling documents shredded by the Stanford Financial Group, owned by R. Allen Stanford, who faces charges related to a $7 billion Ponzi scheme. According to experts, shredded documents can be reconstructed using advanced computer technology, which involves feeding the shreds into a scanner and using specialized software to identify and match the fragments. The success of the reconstruction process depends on various factors, including the size and type of shredder used, with strip shredders producing larger fragments that are easier to reassemble.

As PIX11 continued to investigate the mysterious case of the confetti strips, the incident highlighted the importance of proper document disposal and the potential consequences of mishandling sensitive information.

history.draft:
On Thanksgiving Day, a disturbing discovery was made at the iconic Macy's parade in New York City. Ethan Finkelstein, a Tufts University freshman home on Thanksgiving break, witnessed something far more alarming than the usual festive atmosphere - confetti containing confidential personal information being tossed into the crowd. Finkelstein, along with his friends, noticed a strip of confetti stuck to his friend's coat, bearing what appeared to be a social security number, followed by several other confetti strips with similar sensitive information, including phone numbers, addresses, and even details from police incident reports. The documents, which were later confirmed to be from the Nassau County Police Department, revealed the names of undercover police officers, their social security numbers, dates of birth, and other highly sensitive personal data.

Further examination of the confetti strips showed that they contained information about a pipe bomb threat in the Kings Grant area, as well as details about Mitt Romney's motorcade from the final presidential debate held at Hofstra University in Nassau County last month. The revelation sent shockwaves, leaving Finkelstein stunned and questioning how such sensitive information ended up in the confetti.

An investigation was launched by the Nassau County Police Department, led by Inspector Kenneth Lack, who stated that the department would review its procedures for disposing of sensitive documents. Meanwhile, Macy's, the sponsor of the parade, claimed to use commercially manufactured, multicolored confetti, not shredded paper, sparking further questions about the origin of the confetti strips.

In a separate development, federal prosecutors revealed that they had begun reassembling documents shredded by the Stanford Financial Group, owned by R. Allen Stanford, who faces charges related to a $7 billion Ponzi scheme. According to experts, shredded documents can be reconstructed using advanced computer technology, which involves feeding the shreds into a scanner and using specialized software to identify and match the fragments. However, the success of the reconstruction process depends on various factors, including the size and type of shredder used, with strip shredders producing larger fragments that are easier to reassemble.

As investigators continue to probe the mysterious case of the confetti strips, the incident serves as a stark reminder of the importance of proper document disposal and the potential consequences of mishandling sensitive information.

history.draft:
Ethan Finkelstein, a Tufts University freshman home on Thanksgiving break, witnessed a disturbing discovery at the Macy's parade. He noticed a strip of confetti stuck to his friend's coat, bearing what appeared to be a social security number, followed by several other confetti strips with similar sensitive information, including phone numbers, addresses, and even details from police incident reports. Finkelstein and his friends picked up more confetti and discovered that it contained information about a pipe bomb threat in the Kings Grant area, as well as details about Mitt Romney's motorcade from the final presidential debate held at Hofstra University in Nassau County last month.

PIX11 News reported that the confetti strips were later confirmed to be from the Nassau County Police Department, revealing the names of undercover police officers, their social security numbers, dates of birth, and other highly sensitive personal data. The revelation shocked Finkelstein and raised questions about how such sensitive information ended up in the confetti.

An investigation was launched by the Nassau County Police Department, led by Inspector Kenneth Lack, who stated that the department would review its procedures for disposing of sensitive documents. Meanwhile, PIX11 News questioned Macy's, the sponsor of the parade, about the origin of the confetti strips, but Macy's claimed to use commercially manufactured, multicolored confetti, not shredded paper.

In a separate development, PIX11 News reported that federal prosecutors revealed last week that they had begun reassembling documents shredded by the Stanford Financial Group, owned by R. Allen Stanford, who faces charges related to a $7 billion Ponzi scheme. According to experts, shredded documents can be reconstructed using advanced computer technology, which involves feeding the shreds into a scanner and using specialized software to identify and match the fragments. The success of the reconstruction process depends on various factors, including the size and type of shredder used, with strip shredders producing larger fragments that are easier to reassemble.

As PIX11 continued to investigate the mysterious case of the confetti strips, the incident highlighted the importance of proper document disposal and the potential consequences of mishandling sensitive information.

reference: – Oops: Observers at the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade were surprised to discover that the confetti landing on their coats contained confidential information including social security numbers and banking info. One paradegoer tells WPIX his friend first noticed a Social Security number on one piece of confetti, then found more with "phone numbers, addresses, more social security numbers, license plate numbers," and police incident reports including arrest records and information about Mitt Romney's motorcade. Turns out the confetti was made up of Nassau County Police Department documents that "were shredded, but clearly not well enough," WPIX notes. Some of the documents even identify undercover police officers by name, and give out information including dates of birth, social security numbers, and more. The police department says in a statement that it is "very concerned" and will be investigating the matter, and Macy's says it uses "commercially manufactured, multicolor confetti, not shredded paper" in the parade. (Click for more on how shredded documents can be reassembled.)

final_summary:
Steve Jobs resigned as Apple Inc.'s CEO, a move that may seem like a step out of the spotlight, but is likely a carefully calculated decision, rather than a sudden departure. As CEO, Jobs was accountable to Apple's board of directors and shareholders, although he was also a member of the board himself. However, the company had not officially designated a chairman, instead listing co-lead directors Andrea Jung, chairwoman and CEO of Avon Products Inc., and Arthur D. Levinson, chairman and CEO of Genentech Inc. Following his resignation, Jobs took on the role of chairman of the board, a position he previously held before stepping down as CEO in January.

Despite taking a leave of absence due to medical reasons, Jobs remained involved in key decisions at Apple, including the unveiling of the iPad 2 in March. His successor, Tim Cook, who took over as CEO in January, was given a seat on the board, joining the existing members, including Jung, Levinson, William V. Campbell, chairman of Intuit Inc., Millard S. Drexler, chairman and CEO of J. Crew, former Vice President Al Gore, and Ronald D. Sugar, former chairman and CEO of Northrop Grumman Corp. The addition of Cook to the board brought the total number of directors to eight, marking a subtle shift in the company's leadership dynamics.

While Jobs' resignation may signal a new era for Apple, it is essential to note that he will continue to exert influence as chairman of the board. This development suggests that Jobs' involvement in the company's affairs will persist, albeit in a different capacity. As Apple navigates this transition, the question on everyone's mind is whether the company can maintain its momentum without Jobs at the helm. The recent surge in Apple's stock price and the upcoming release of new products, such as the iPhone 5 and potentially an affordable iPhone, indicate that the company is poised for continued growth. However, the lack of clear direction under Jobs' leadership has raised concerns about the company's future trajectory.

The article highlights the existence of multiple "Apples," each representing different aspects of the company's identity. There is the Apple led by Tim Cook, characterized by ruthless efficiency and a focus on controlling suppliers and manufacturers. Another Apple is the marketing powerhouse, renowned for its innovative products and sleek designs. Lastly, there is the innovatory Apple, driven by the vision of Steve Jobs, which operates beneath the radar, making strategic deals and acquisitions to stay ahead of the competition. As Apple embarks on this new chapter, the company faces significant challenges, particularly in the areas of innovation and expansion. Without Jobs' guidance, the company's future direction remains uncertain, and the likelihood of new breakthroughs and innovations may dwindle. The article concludes that Apple's future depends on the emergence of a new "next" – a concept that was synonymous with Jobs' leadership style.

reference: – Steve Jobs revolutionized Apple in his second go-round with the company, taking the stock from a split-adjusted $6.05 per share in 1996 to $376 today. But with Jobs resigning as CEO, everyone is wondering what the future holds for Apple. Some reactions: What will change? Not much, write Nathan Olivarez-Giles and Thomas Suh Lauder for the LA Times. In his role as CEO, Jobs had to answer to two groups: shareholders, and the board of directors. Now he's at the helm of that board. It was the perfect time to go, writes Brian Barrett for Gizmodo, noting that the coming release of the iPhone 5 and more affordable iPhone 4 should buoy the company and give CEO Tim Cook a chance to step into the spotlight. "If they'd waited any longer, the iPhone 5 announcement would've been fully shrouded in memories of Jobs. ... This feels calculated, in the best possible way, to happen at the best possible time. Now." Apple has lost its core, writes Rupert Goodwins for ZDNet, and only time will tell how this plays out. The iPhone and iPad can only be updated so many times, and with Jobs "there was always a 'next.'" But "Apple as innovator without Jobs is Apple unknown." Its future "depends on the new next, and the longer we have to wait to see what happens, the less likely it will happen at all."

final_summary:
It's been one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was heading the investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

One of the most pressing questions surrounding the investigation is whether Mueller can subpoena Trump. While most legal experts agree that being president does not provide blanket immunity from a subpoena, there are still significant uncertainties and potential legal challenges. The Supreme Court has ruled in the past that a sitting president can be subject to a civil lawsuit, and a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed.

Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Jeff Sessions, who recused himself from the investigation, leaving Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to oversee the matter. Rosenstein was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Rosenstein and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

In 1973, the Office of Legal Counsel issued an opinion stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function. Similarly, in the case of President Bill Clinton, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a civil suit could proceed against a sitting president. 

Harold H. Bruff, an emeritus University of Colorado law professor, argues that the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, but notes that the word "grant" implies giving to someone else, suggesting that a self-pardon would be impermissible. Furthermore, the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause," also supports the argument that a self-pardon would be impermissible.

Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

history.draft:
It's been one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was heading the investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

One of the most pressing questions surrounding the investigation is whether Mueller can subpoena Trump. While most legal experts agree that being president does not provide blanket immunity from a subpoena, there are still significant uncertainties and potential legal challenges. The Supreme Court has ruled in the past that a sitting president can be subject to a civil lawsuit, and a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed.

Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Jeff Sessions, who recused himself from the investigation, leaving Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to oversee the matter. Rosenstein was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Rosenstein and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

In 2000, the Office of Legal Counsel issued an opinion stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function. Similarly, in the case of President Bill Clinton, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a civil suit could proceed against a sitting president. 

Finally, there is speculation about Trump's potential use of pardons to evade accountability. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that it would be impermissible based on historical context and the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause." Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

reference: – Thursday is the one-year anniversary of the start of Robert Mueller's investigation, and President Trump is among those marking the occasion. His tweet, however, drips with sarcasm. "Congratulations America, we are now into the second year of the greatest Witch Hunt in American History ... and there is still No Collusion and No Obstruction," he writes. "The only Collusion was that done by Democrats who were unable to win an Election despite the spending of far more money!" In another tweet, he calls the investigation "disgusting, illegal and unwarranted" and said his administration has nonetheless had "the most successful" first 17 months of any White House, "by far!" Related coverage: The basics: Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate "any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump," explains PolitiFact. Mueller quickly expanded his investigation into whether Trump obstructed justice, notably by firing FBI chief James Comey. The piece hits other nuts and bolts of the investigation.

final_summary:
A significant development has emerged in the Corsican separatist movement, with the National Liberation Front of Corsica (FLNC) announcing its intention to abandon its armed struggle after nearly 40 years. According to a statement released on Wednesday to CNN affiliate France 3, the FLNC has decided to initiate a demilitarization process and transition into conventional politics, marking a major shift in its approach to achieving Corsican independence.

This decision follows a long history of violence and terrorism on the island of Corsica, which has been plagued by bombings, robberies, and assaults since 1976. Located in the Mediterranean, specifically in the Tyrrhenian Sea, between the coasts of France and Italy, Corsica has been under French control since the 1700s. The FLNC has claimed responsibility for numerous attacks, including bombings of banks and government offices.

One notable incident occurred in 1997 when over 50 bomb explosions targeted several banks and government offices across the island. France's government launched a crackdown in Corsica after a top French official was gunned down in 1998, leading to a series of nationalist protests and FLNC attacks.

The FLNC's decision to disarm comes after a series of developments, including a bill passed in April that restricts land and property purchases to individuals who have been Corsican residents for at least five years. Gilles Millet, a journalist at Corsica magazine, confirmed the authenticity of the 14-page communiqué issued by the FLNC. The group cited recent progress through conventional means as a primary reason for their decision to disarm.

They believe that measures such as the property-owning restrictions "trace the outlines of a political solution" and mark a shift from a phase of combat and resistance to one of constructing a true Corsican political power. The FLNC's decision may signal a new era for Corsica, one where the islanders can pursue their goals through peaceful means.

history.draft:
In a significant development, the National Liberation Front of Corsica (FLNC), a militant group seeking independence from France, has announced its intention to abandon its armed struggle after nearly 40 years. According to a statement released on Wednesday, the FLNC has decided to initiate a demilitarization process and transition into conventional politics, marking a major shift in its approach to achieving Corsican independence. 

This decision follows a long history of violence and terrorism on the island of Corsica, which has been plagued by bombings, robberies, and assaults since 1976. The FLNC, also known as the FLNC, has claimed responsibility for numerous attacks, including the murder of policemen, bombings of banks in Paris, and rocket attacks on government buildings. One notable incident occurred in 1997 when over 50 bomb explosions targeted several banks and government offices across the island.

Corsica, a Mediterranean island situated between the coasts of France and Italy, has been under French control since the 1700s. The island's strategic location in the Tyrrhenian Sea makes it a prized possession for both countries. The FLNC's decision to disarm comes after a series of concessions from the French government, including a bill passed in April that restricts land and property purchases to individuals who have been Corsican residents for at least five years. This move aims to curb speculation-driven inflation in real estate prices on the island, where approximately 40% of properties are owned by non-residents.

The FLNC's announcement was confirmed by Gilles Millet, a journalist at Corsica magazine, who stated that the 14-page communiqué was authentic. The group, comprising over 100 members, cited recent progress through conventional means as a primary reason for their decision to disarm. They believe that measures such as the property-owning restrictions "trace the outlines of a political solution" and mark a shift from a phase of combat and resistance to one of constructing a true Corsican political power.

Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio, the capital city of Corsica, played a pivotal role in shaping the island's history. His legacy continues to influence the island's identity and aspirations for self-governance. The FLNC's decision to lay down its arms may signal a new era for Corsica, one where the islanders can pursue their goals through peaceful means. The news was first reported by CNN affiliate France 3, highlighting the significance of this development in the region.

reference: – There is now one less armed separatist group in Europe: After nearly 40 years and thousands of attacks, including bombings and assassinations, the National Liberation Front of Corsica has decided to call it a day, CNN reports. The banned militant group, which launched its violent campaign for independence from France in 1976, says it has begun the process of demilitarization without preconditions and will pursue its goals through political channels. The group's cause still has plenty of support on the island of 320,000 people, where at least 40% of homes are second properties owned by non-residents, the Financial Times finds. The regional assembly recently restricted property purchases to those who have been residents for at least five years, and the militants say that move and similar ones show that "we are moving from a phase of combat and resistance to a phase of the construction of a true Corsican political power."

final_summary:
Beloit College releases the annual Mindset List, providing a snapshot of the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of students entering college each fall in Beloit, Wisconsin. The list's creators, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, initially designed it as a reminder to faculty to be aware of dated references, but it soon evolved into a comprehensive catalog of the rapidly changing worldview of each new generation. Starting in 1998, the list has highlighted the distinct characteristics of each incoming class, starting with the class of 1998 and continuing through the present day.

Each year, the Mindset List sheds light on the values, attitudes, and experiences of the new generation. For example, the 2014 edition focused on the class of 2014, born in 1992, who grew up in a world where the digital world is routine and technology is often too slow. They have grown up in a world where email is fast-paced, and they regularly use smartphones. In contrast, the class of 2021, mostly born in 1999, have always searched for Pokémon and have never read a Peanuts strip that wasn't a repeat. They have grown up in a world without dial-up modems and have always had access to emojis.

Beloit College's Mindset List has become a valuable resource for educators, policymakers, and the general public to understand the values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation. The list's authors, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, have expanded their scope to create additional resources, including a speakers' program and a study guide to their book, "The Mindset Lists of American History." Their work aims to facilitate dialogue and understanding across generations, highlighting the importance of adapting to the changing needs and perspectives of each new group of students.

The Mindset List has also shed light on the economic virtues of millennials, who have been shaped by their economic status and have developed unique values and behaviors as a result. According to economist Deidre McCloskey, the virtues of different social groups are strongly determined by their economic status, with humility being a dominant virtue among peasants and courage being a virtue among the aristocracy.

In addition to the Mindset List, the documents highlight the significance of technology in shaping the lives of young people. The Internet Archive, founded in 1996, has been collecting and preserving internet content, including Alexa Internet's crawl data, which is donated daily and added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This archive serves as a valuable resource for researchers and historians, providing insights into the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

As the world continues to evolve, the Mindset List remains an essential tool for understanding the complexities of each new generation. By examining the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of incoming college students, educators and policymakers can develop strategies to effectively engage and educate this diverse group of individuals. The list's creators continue to provide valuable insights into the changing values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation, ensuring that education remains relevant and effective in the face of rapid technological and societal advancements.

history.draft:
Every day, Beloit College releases the annual Mindset List, providing a snapshot of the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of students entering college each fall. The list's creators, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, initially designed it as a reminder to faculty to be aware of dated references, but it soon evolved into a comprehensive catalog of the rapidly changing worldview of each new generation. Starting in 1998, the list has highlighted the distinct characteristics of each incoming class, starting with the class of 1998 and continuing through the present day.

Each year, the Mindset List sheds light on the values, attitudes, and experiences of the new generation. For example, the 2014 edition focused on the class of 2014, born in 1992, who grew up in a world where the digital world is routine and technology is often too slow. They have grown up in a world where email is fast-paced, and they regularly use smartphones. In contrast, the class of 2021, mostly born in 1999, have always searched for Pokémon and have never read a Peanuts strip that wasn't a repeat. They have grown up in a world without dial-up modems and have always had access to emojis.

Beloit College's Mindset List has become a valuable resource for educators, policymakers, and the general public to understand the values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation. The list's authors, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, have expanded their scope to create additional resources, including a speakers' program and a study guide to their book, "The Mindset Lists of American History." Their work aims to facilitate dialogue and understanding across generations, highlighting the importance of adapting to the changing needs and perspectives of each new group of students.

The Mindset List has also shed light on the economic virtues of millennials, who have been shaped by their economic status and have developed unique values and behaviors as a result. According to economist Deidre McCloskey, the virtues of different social groups are strongly determined by their economic status, with humility being a dominant virtue among peasants and courage being a virtue among the aristocracy.

In addition to the Mindset List, the documents highlight the significance of technology in shaping the lives of young people. The Internet Archive, founded in 1996, has been collecting and preserving internet content, including Alexa Internet's crawl data, which is donated daily and added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This archive serves as a valuable resource for researchers and historians, providing insights into the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

As the world continues to evolve, the Mindset List remains an essential tool for understanding the complexities of each new generation. By examining the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of incoming college students, educators and policymakers can develop strategies to effectively engage and educate this diverse group of individuals. The list's creators continue to provide valuable insights into the changing values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation, ensuring that education remains relevant and effective in the face of rapid technological and societal advancements.

reference: – US News & World Report is ready to shock us all with its Best Colleges 2011 ranking, which places Harvard smack dab on top. Again. But—news alert!—Princeton has tumbled from tying the venerable Crimson to No. 2. That list, which you can see here, doesn't wow us quite so much as does another annual list released today: Beloit College's Mindset List, which gives us a snapshot of the realities and events shaping the lives of this year's freshman class, born in 1992. Some highlights: The majority don't know how to write in cursive. They have never had trouble understanding what a "caramel macchiato" is. Clint Eastwood is some guy who directs movies. Fergie is a member of the Black Eyed Peas, not the British Royal Family. Czechoslovakia has never existed. The Post Office has always been basically broke. Click here for the complete 75-item list (Buffy and Barney and Beanie Babies, oh my!).

final_summary:
In a recent development from Islamabad, Pakistan, Railway Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour sparked controversy when he offered a personal $100,000 reward for the killing of the person behind an anti-Islam movie that has sparked widespread outrage across the Muslim world. Speaking at a press conference on Saturday, Bilour clarified that he was making the offer as an individual, not as a government representative. When questioned about the implications of his actions, Bilour stated that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative.

Bilour went so far as to invite the Taliban to carry out the assassination, which drew sharp criticism from within his own party, the Awami National Party. Spokesman Sen. Zahid Khan distanced the party from Bilour's comments, emphasizing its commitment to non-violence and opposition to militancy and extremism. Khan expressed concern over Bilour's statements, highlighting the party's long-standing efforts to combat these issues.

On Sunday, Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf's spokesperson condemned Bilour's actions, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. The US government also strongly criticized Bilour's decision, calling it "inflammatory and inappropriate" and noting that the prime minister's office had disassociated itself from the comments. According to reports, while the US acknowledges the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," it does not justify violence and calls upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

Protests erupted across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. To mitigate the situation, the US Embassy in Pakistan aired advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. The US also advised its citizens to exercise caution when traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. Meanwhile, the global fallout extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, prompting the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in approximately 20 countries across the Muslim world. The alleged producer of the film, Nakoula Basseley Nakoula, has gone into hiding, and the US has urged calm amidst the escalating tensions.

In the midst of widespread outrage across the Muslim world over an anti-Islam movie, a high-stakes controversy erupted in Pakistan when Railway Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour publicly offered a $100,000 reward for the killing of the film's maker. Speaking at a press conference in Islamabad on Saturday, Bilour stated that he was offering the bounty as an individual, rather than representing the government. When questioned about the potential implications of his actions, Bilour clarified that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative. 

Bilour went so far as to extend an invitation to the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, sparking widespread condemnation from within his own party, the Awami National Party. Spokesman Sen. Zahid Khan vehemently distanced the party from Bilour's comments, emphasizing its commitment to non-violence and opposition to militancy and extremism. Khan expressed concern over Bilour's statements, highlighting the party's long-standing efforts to combat these issues.

Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf swiftly denounced Bilour's actions through his spokesperson, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. Meanwhile, the US State Department strongly criticized Bilour's decision, labeling it "inflammatory and inappropriate" and noting that the prime minister's office had disassociated itself from the comments. The State Department emphasized that while the US acknowledged the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," it did not justify violence and called upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

As tensions continued to escalate, protests broke out across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. The US Embassy in Pakistan took steps to mitigate the situation by airing advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. Additionally, the US advised its citizens to avoid traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. The global fallout also extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, prompting the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in approximately 20 countries across the Muslim world.

reference: – A US official has called it "inflammatory and inappropriate" for a Pakistani minister to offer a $100,000 bounty for killing the filmmaker of Innocence of Muslims. Railways Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour, who made the offer, said he would pay the bounty out of his own pocket as protests continued yesterday against the anti-Islamic film. Bilour, who called himself a "Muslim first and a government representative second," said he has invited the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, reports CNN. Pakistani government officials have similarly blasted the offer. A US State Department official told the BBC yesterday: "The president and secretary of state have both said the video at the core of this is offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible—but that is no justification for violence, and it is important for responsible leaders to stand up and speak out against violence." In another threat, this one aimed at the French publication Charlie Hebdo for printing cartoons of the prophet Mohammed, an 18-year-old man who threatened the magazine's editors on Facebook has been charged with terrorism-related activity.

final_summary:
A recent internet trend known as the "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge has caused significant financial losses for a Houston resident. According to KPRC-TV, a 28-year-old man named James McHugh, whose full name was reported as James McCue in another source, recently incurred a $4,000 repair bill after a group of teenagers attempted to replicate a viral video by jumping onto the hood of his white BMW, causing substantial damage. This incident occurred on a Friday evening when a group of seven young men were caught on McHugh's home security camera walking up to his car on Plaza del Sol Park.

The "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge originated in May after a member of the rap group ThotBoyz posted a video of himself performing a backward dive onto a car hood, which quickly gained over three million views on Vine. Since then, numerous copycat videos have surfaced on YouTube and other social media platforms, featuring individuals jumping onto various objects such as cars, store displays, trash bins, and even each other.

James McHugh expressed frustration over the incident, stating that it is infuriating to see his hard-earned money wasted for just 10 seconds of laughter. The damage to his BMW and his wife's car serves as a stark reminder of the potential consequences of this reckless behavior.

history.draft:
A recent internet trend known as the "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge has caused significant financial losses for a Houston resident. According to KPRC-TV, a 28-year-old man named James McHugh, whose full name was reported as James McCue in another source, recently incurred a $4,000 repair bill after a group of teenagers attempted to replicate a viral video by jumping onto the hood of his white BMW, causing substantial damage. This incident occurred on a Friday evening when a group of seven young men were caught on McHugh's home security camera walking up to his car on Plaza del Sol Park.

The "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge originated in May after a member of the rap group ThotBoyz posted a video of himself performing a backward dive onto a car hood, which quickly gained over three million views on Vine. Since then, numerous copycat videos have surfaced on YouTube and other social media platforms, featuring individuals jumping onto various objects such as cars, store displays, trash bins, and even each other.

James McHugh expressed frustration over the incident, stating that it is infuriating to see his hard-earned money wasted for just 10 seconds of laughter. The damage to his BMW and his wife's car serves as a stark reminder of the potential consequences of this reckless behavior.

reference: – The latest prank that all the kids are into is a little more destructive than the toilet paper of yesteryear: Called "Put 'em in a coffin" and based on a Vine video from May that's gone viral, it involves throwing yourself back-first onto the tops of cars (and grocery displays, and classroom desks, and one another, etc.). And for at least one Houston man and his wife, the damage is into the thousands. Last Friday, James McHugh, 28, found both his BMW and his wife's car seriously dented across the hoods, reports KPRC-TV, and a home security camera caught grainy footage of seven young men approaching his property. He says it'll cost about $4,000 for the repairs. "You work hard, and then to have that much money washed away for 10 seconds of laughter—it's very frustrating," McHugh says. People have been posting videos on YouTube, Vine, and other social media sites since rapper VonMar of the group ThotBoyz posted the original back in May, reports the Houston Chronicle. Since then, his video has been viewed more than 3 million times on Vine. As for McHugh, there's no word yet on whether his own security footage will help identify the vandals. (This high school prank resulted in 62 arrests.)

final_summary: Researchers at Imperial College London have made a groundbreaking discovery that sheds light on the mysterious link between dietary fibre and weight loss. According to their study, published in Nature Communications, a molecule called acetate, produced by the fermentation of fibre in the gut, plays a crucial role in suppressing appetite and promoting weight loss. The team, led by biochemist Jimmy Bell, conducted experiments on mice, feeding them a high-fat diet supplemented with inulin, a type of dietary fibre found in chicory and sugar beets. The results showed that the mice consuming inulin ate less and gained less weight compared to those on a high-fat diet alone. Further investigation revealed that the inulin triggered the production of acetate, which accumulated in the hypothalamus, a region of the brain responsible for regulating appetite. Using advanced imaging techniques, including positron emission tomography (PET) scans, the researchers tracked the movement of acetate from the gut to the brain, demonstrating that it played a direct role in suppressing hunger. When acetate was injected into the bloodstream, colon, or brain, it significantly reduced food intake in mice. Bell and his team propose that the widespread adoption of processed foods, low in fibre, has contributed to the rising obesity epidemic. In ancient times, humans consumed far more fibre-rich foods, which supported a healthier balance between gut bacteria and brain function. Today, the average Western diet contains only about 15 grams of fibre per day, compared to the 100 grams consumed by our ancestors. To combat this issue, the researchers are exploring ways to harness the power of acetate for therapeutic applications. They are collaborating with companies to develop novel formulations that can deliver acetate in a sustained-release format, mimicking the natural process of fibre digestion. This could potentially lead to the creation of an "acetate pill" that helps regulate appetite and supports weight management. The study's findings have sparked excitement among experts, with some cautioning that further research is needed to fully understand the complexities of the gut-brain axis. However, the implications of this breakthrough are profound, suggesting that a simple, fibre-rich diet may hold the key to addressing the global obesity crisis. In related news, a team of scientists at the University of Alberta in Canada has raised concerns about the oversimplification of the research, highlighting the intricate mechanisms governing neurotransmitter release in the hypothalamus. Nevertheless, the study's lead author, Jimmy Bell, remains optimistic that the discovery will inspire innovative solutions to tackle the growing problem of obesity. Meanwhile, researchers at various institutions, including Agilent Technologies, Sigma, and Siemens, are working together to advance our understanding of the gut-brain connection. Their efforts aim to unlock the secrets of acetate's role in appetite regulation and explore potential applications in the development of new treatments for obesity. In the US, researchers at Charles River are conducting parallel studies on the effects of acetate on appetite suppression, while in Canada, scientists are investigating the impact of fibre on gut health. Meanwhile, in Spain, researchers are examining the metabolic pathways involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In France, experts are analyzing the molecular mechanisms underlying acetate's action on the hypothalamus, while in the UK, researchers at Imperial College London continue to refine their understanding of the acetate-fibre connection. The collaboration among these teams highlights the international scope of the research, as scientists strive to unravel the mysteries of the gut-brain axis and uncover new avenues for treating obesity. Using cutting-edge tools like Bruker's nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, researchers are gaining insights into the biochemical processes involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In the US, researchers at Cell Signalling are developing novel assays to detect and quantify acetate levels in biological samples. As the scientific community continues to investigate the intricacies of the gut-brain axis, researchers are turning to statistical analysis software like Stata to identify patterns and correlations in the data. By combining these advances in technology and methodology, scientists hope to develop effective strategies for preventing and treating obesity, ultimately improving public health outcomes worldwide.

reference: – Experts have long suggested that fiber keeps us feeling full because it takes a long time to digest, but new research challenges that notion—and may point the way to a new anti-hunger pill. The key is a short-chain fatty acid called acetate, which emerges as fiber is digested. Acetate then makes its way to the brain's hypothalamus, where it builds up and triggers chemical reactions that tell us we're full, Time reports. Researchers reached this conclusion by scanning the progress of a dietary fiber called inulin through the bodies of mice. Those mice who ate fatty diets rich in inulin gained less weight than mice who didn't eat inulin. But one electrophysiologist not related to the study points out that the mice were fed dietary fiber levels of about 11%. He tells Nature that at such a level, "the room would be full of mouse farts"—and perhaps the mice were eating less because they felt gassy. The researchers plan to next test the idea in humans. So could we eventually see an acetate-based diet pill? It's possible, the Telegraph suggests: "The major challenge is to develop an approach that will deliver the amount of acetate needed to suppress appetite but in a form that is acceptable and safe for humans," a researcher says.

final_summary:
HOPE, Maine — On a warm afternoon on June 3, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. 

Little did she know, this chance encounter would turn into a fight for survival. Borch remembered noticing the raccoon's erratic behavior, which led her to suspect that it was suffering from rabies. As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack.

However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror. In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling.

After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp. Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and packed it into a Taste of the Wild dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer.

By June 22, 2017, subsequent testing confirmed that the raccoon had indeed contracted rabies. According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. By June 7, 2017, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state that year, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

Borch's story echoes the words of author Stephen King, whose novel "Pet Sematary" features a terrifying encounter between a family and a rabid animal. On a warm afternoon in late May or early June, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer. Subsequent testing confirmed that the raccoon had indeed contracted rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. As of June 7, 2017, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state that year, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

reference: – Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. Borch, 21, knew immediately that something was wrong with the raccoon that was charging toward her. "Imagine the Tasmanian devil," she tells the Bangor Daily News of the "terrifying" June 3 incident. Yanking out her headphones, she began "dancing" around the animal on the narrow path, but she tells the Herald she knew that, one way or another, the animal was going to bite her. She figured her hands would be the best spot, so she offered them up. The raccoon chomped down on her thumb and stayed there, scratching at her legs and arms as she screamed. Now on her knees, she spotted her phone lying submerged in a mud puddle, and had an idea: drown it. "With my thumb in its mouth, I just pushed its head down into the muck," she tells the News. When the raccoon finally stopped moving, she yanked out her finger and raced the three-quarters of a mile home. Two days later, the state confirmed the animal was infected with rabies. Borch got rabies shots, and an animal control officer adds an unpleasant footnote: The infected raccoon may not be alone. "Not to scare people," Heidi Blood tells the News, but "when there’s one, there’s typically another." (Normally nocturnal like raccoons, a crazed beaver attacked a woman paddle-boarder.)

final_summary:
Last week, Google's new social networking service, Google+, experienced a decline in U.S. visitors, with total visits falling about 3 percent to 1.79 million compared to the prior seven days. This decrease follows a significant surge in traffic, where Google+ visits rose by 283 percent in the week ended July 16. Furthermore, the average time spent on the site dropped by 10 percent to 5 minutes and 15 seconds during the same period.

The slowdown in growth raises concerns about the platform's momentum, particularly given its rapid expansion in the preceding weeks. Despite Google co-founder Larry Page announcing that Google+ had surpassed 10 million users within three weeks of its launch, the service faces challenges in retaining users and keeping them engaged. According to Experian Hitwise, which tracks web traffic, excluding mobile users and third-party application traffic, the platform's ability to sustain interest and attract repeat visitors will be crucial to its long-term success.

A closer examination of user behavior reveals that many individuals, including power users, are experiencing a decline in engagement with Google+. One blogger noted that their usage has decreased significantly, loading the site only twice a day due to the lack of fresh content and the prevalence of self-promotional posts. Similar sentiments were echoed by others, suggesting that the initial excitement surrounding Google+'s launch has worn off, leaving users with a sense of stagnation.

The introduction of features such as Hangouts appears to be a bright spot for Google+, offering a unique aspect that sets it apart from other social media platforms. However, other features like Sparks have faced criticism for their complexity and unclear purpose. Circles, initially praised for their simplicity, have also encountered difficulties in terms of maintenance and usage, with some users finding it challenging to effectively utilize them.

Meanwhile, referral traffic from Google+ has begun to drop, indicating a potential issue with the platform's ability to drive external engagement. This decline in referrals may be attributed, in part, to Google's handling of branding and content, which has led to frustration among users and contributors. Google Inc., parent company of Google+, fell $15.30, or 2.5 percent, to $607.22 on the Nasdaq Stock Market yesterday. With Google's reputation on the line, the company must address these concerns and adapt to changing user behaviors to ensure the continued success of Google+.
