history.draft:
A new study published today in the journal Climatic Change warns that rising global temperatures due to climate change will significantly impact the aviation industry, making it harder for many aircraft to take off in the coming years. According to the study, conducted by researchers Ethan Coffel and Radley Horton from Columbia University, during the hottest parts of the day, 10 to 30 percent of fully loaded planes may have to remove some fuel, cargo, or passengers, or wait for cooler hours to fly. This is because warmer air expands and becomes less dense, reducing the lift generated by an airplane's wings, making it difficult for them to take off safely.

The study, which analyzed data from 19 major airports worldwide, including those in the US, Europe, Asia, and the Middle East, found that some airports will be more severely impacted than others. New York's LaGuardia Airport, with its short runways, and Dubai International Airport, due to its extremely high temperatures, are among the airports that will face significant challenges. On the other hand, airports like New York's John F. Kennedy International Airport, London's Heathrow, and Paris' Charles de Gaulle are expected to be less affected.

The study estimates that if greenhouse gas emissions continue unabated, fuel capacities and payload weights will have to be reduced by as much as 4 percent on the hottest days for some aircraft. This means that an average 160-seat aircraft may have to remove 12 or 13 passengers to ensure safe takeoff. The researchers also note that some aircraft models, such as the Boeing 737-800, will be more severely impacted than others.

Heatwaves have already caused disruptions in air travel, with recent extreme heat forcing American Airlines to cancel over 40 flights out of Phoenix, and major airlines delaying or canceling dozens of flights out of Las Vegas and Phoenix airports. The study's findings highlight the urgent need for the aviation industry to adapt to the changing climate.

To mitigate the impact of heatwaves on air travel, the researchers suggest modifying aircraft design, expanding runways, or adjusting flight schedules to depart earlier in the day when it's cooler. However, these measures will require significant investment and planning. As the world's climate continues to warm, the aviation industry will need to find ways to adapt to the changing conditions to avoid costly delays and cancellations.

Ethan Coffel, the study's lead author, emphasizes that incorporating climate projections into mid- and long-term plans will be crucial for effective adaptation efforts. Radley Horton, a co-author of the study, notes that the aviation industry needs to start dealing with climate change more aggressively, considering options such as developing engines that produce fewer greenhouse gases and adapting planes to the future climate.

Washington's Reagan National Airport, with its short runways, is also expected to face significant challenges due to heatwaves. The study's findings have significant implications for the aviation industry, which operates on thin profit margins. The researchers warn that the costs of delays and cancellations could disrupt other sectors of the economy as they trickle down. With global temperatures expected to rise by up to 5.5 degrees Fahrenheit by 2100, the aviation industry will need to take proactive steps to mitigate the impact of heatwaves on air travel.

reference: – Remember in June when it got too hot for planes to take off in Phoenix? A new study suggests stories like that will become quite common in the not-too-distant future. Via Reuters, the study published in Climatic Change estimates that rising temperatures could leave up to one-third of planes grounded during heat waves in the coming decades. The problem centers around the wings’ capacity for lift, which is lessened in warmer conditions. By 2100, worldwide average temperatures are expected to rise by 5.4 degrees Fahrenheit. To safely take off, airlines would need to make adjustments during the hottest part of the day, with 10% to 30% of planes affected. Full flights will either stay grounded until conditions cool down or lighten their loads by removing fuel, cargo, or passengers, per a release at EurekAlert. Think 12 to 13 people asked to leave a 160-person passenger plane. "Our results suggest that weight restriction may impose a non-trivial cost," one of the study’s lead authors says. Wired reports that among the airports studied, New York’s LaGuardia and Washington’s Reagan-National fared poorly because of short runways, as did Dubai International Airport, where extreme heat could force reduced loads up to 55% of the time during peak temperatures. Better news: New York's JFK wouldn't be much affected. Delayed departures won't be the only hassle. Scientists say severe turbulence would become more common. “I’ve yet to see a benefit of climate change to aviation,” says atmospheric scientist Paul Williams, who believes it’s high time for the airline industry to prepare and adapt. (An essay at New York provided a decidedly bleak outlook on climate change.)

final_summary:
Lance Armstrong, the former professional cyclist, will sit down for a 90-minute interview with media mogul Oprah Winfrey on January 17, marking his first televised appearance since being stripped of his seven Tour de France titles in October. The interview, described as "no-holds-barred," will be broadcast on the Oprah Winfrey Network and will cover the doping allegations that led to Armstrong's downfall.

According to the head of the United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA), Travis Tygart, Armstrong had offered to donate $150,000 to the organization in 2004, a move deemed a clear conflict of interest. This revelation comes amidst reports that Armstrong is considering publicly admitting to using performance-enhancing drugs and blood transfusions during his cycling career, a move that could potentially lead to his reinstatement under World Anti-Doping Agency rules.

Armstrong's decision to come clean follows years of denials, including his assertion that he passed less than 60 drug tests without failing, despite his former teammates testifying to beating tests or avoiding them altogether. The International Cycling Union, which chose not to appeal USADA's lifetime ban, stripped Armstrong of his titles, and the World Anti-Doping Agency agreed with the sanctions, effectively barring him from competition.

The interview with Winfrey has sparked controversy due to her network's ties to Discovery Communications, which sponsored Armstrong's team in 2005. Critics argue that Winfrey may go easy on Armstrong, pointing to her 2008 interview with Marion Jones, another athlete who admitted to using performance-enhancing drugs. However, Kathy LeMond, wife of Tour de France winner Greg LeMond, has offered to educate Winfrey on the culture of professional cycling ahead of the interview.

In preparation for the interview, journalists and cycling enthusiasts have taken to social media to suggest tough questions for Winfrey to ask Armstrong, including queries about his relationship with his children and his involvement in various doping scandals. Armstrong's attorney, Tim Herman, has declined to comment on reports that Armstrong is considering an admission of guilt, which could potentially lead to his reinstatement.

Armstrong's reputation has been marred by allegations of doping, dating back to 2011 when fellow cyclist Floyd Landis made claims against him. Armstrong subsequently sued USADA to stop the investigation but lost the case, leading to his ban from cycling. His subsequent resignation from the Livestrong Foundation and the stripping of his titles marked a significant fall from grace for the once-beloved athlete.

As Armstrong prepares to face the consequences of his actions, the question remains whether he will finally come clean about his involvement in the doping scandal that has haunted him for years. The interview with Winfrey promises to provide insight into the mind of the man who was once a hero to millions but is now facing the possibility of a lifetime ban from competitive sports.

history.draft:
Lance Armstrong, the former professional cyclist, will sit down for a 90-minute interview with media mogul Oprah Winfrey on January 17, marking his first televised appearance since being stripped of his seven Tour de France titles in October. The interview, described as "no-holds-barred," will be broadcast on the Oprah Winfrey Network and will cover the doping allegations that led to Armstrong's downfall.

According to the head of the United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA), Travis Tygart, Armstrong had offered to donate $150,000 to the organization in 2004, a move deemed a clear conflict of interest. This revelation comes amidst reports that Armstrong is considering publicly admitting to using performance-enhancing drugs and blood transfusions during his cycling career, a move that could potentially lead to his reinstatement under World Anti-Doping Agency rules.

Armstrong's decision to come clean follows years of denials, including his assertion that he passed less than 60 drug tests without failing, despite his former teammates testifying to beating tests or avoiding them altogether. The International Cycling Union, which chose not to appeal USADA's lifetime ban, stripped Armstrong of his titles, and the World Anti-Doping Agency agreed with the sanctions, effectively barring him from competition.

The interview with Winfrey has sparked controversy due to her network's ties to Discovery Communications, which sponsored Armstrong's team in 2005. Critics argue that Winfrey may go easy on Armstrong, pointing to her 2008 interview with Marion Jones, another athlete who admitted to using performance-enhancing drugs. However, Kathy LeMond, wife of Tour de France winner Greg LeMond, has offered to educate Winfrey on the culture of professional cycling ahead of the interview.

In preparation for the interview, journalists and cycling enthusiasts have taken to social media to suggest tough questions for Winfrey to ask Armstrong, including queries about his relationship with his children and his involvement in various doping scandals. Armstrong's attorney, Tim Herman, has declined to comment on reports that Armstrong is considering an admission of guilt, which could potentially lead to his reinstatement.

Armstrong's reputation has been marred by allegations of doping, dating back to 2011 when fellow cyclist Floyd Landis made claims against him. Armstrong subsequently sued USADA to stop the investigation but lost the case, leading to his ban from cycling. His subsequent resignation from the Livestrong Foundation and the stripping of his titles marked a significant fall from grace for the once-beloved athlete.

As Armstrong prepares to face the consequences of his actions, the question remains whether he will finally come clean about his involvement in the doping scandal that has haunted him for years. The interview with Winfrey promises to provide insight into the mind of the man who was once a hero to millions but is now facing the possibility of a lifetime ban from competitive sports.

reference: – Lance Armstrong is rumored to be ready to confess to drug use—and it could happen on Oprah's show. The disgraced cyclist will give a "no-holds-barred" interview to Oprah Winfrey that will air on her network next week, CNN reports. In his first formal interview since he was banned from the sport and stripped of his titles for doping, Armstrong will "address the alleged doping scandal, years of accusations of cheating, and charges of lying about the use of performance-enhancing drugs throughout his storied cycling career," the network says in a statement. In a 60 Minutes Sports interview to be aired tonight, the chief of the US Anti-Doping Agency says an Armstrong representative offered to donate around $250,000 to the agency, reports the New York Times. Travis Tygart, who accuses Armstrong of trying to intimidate former teammates, says the 2004 offer represented a clear conflict of interest. "We had no hesitation in rejecting that offer," he says. Tygart says he received death threats during his organization's investigation of Armstrong.

final_summary:
In a dramatic turn of events, the NYPD, along with Mayor Bill de Blasio and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, held a press conference on Wednesday to announce the identity of the suspect responsible for the brutal stabbing of two young children in the elevator of a NYCHA building in East New York. The suspect, identified as 27-year-old Daniel St. Hubert, was subsequently arrested in Queens, bringing relief to the community and the families of the victims, as reported by the New York Daily News.

According to authorities, St. Hubert, a 5'9" and 210-pound black male, had a history of violence, having served time for assault and attempted murder. He was released on parole on May 23, but failed to meet with his parole officer earlier that day. The suspect had also been charged with assaulting a police officer and a correction officer, which contributed to his current parole status.

St. Hubert was described as "armed and dangerous" by NYPD Chief of Detectives Robert Boyce, who emphasized that the public should not approach him. The suspect's description was circulated through various media outlets, including a tweet by Tina Moore of the New York Daily News featuring a photo of his distinctive tattoo of a satanic goat's head with horns, etched under the word "Aries."

The investigation began on Sunday, when Prince Joshua "P.J." Avitto, 6, and Mikayla Capers, 7, were stabbed multiple times in the elevator of the Boulevard Houses on Schenk Avenue in East New York. Unfortunately, P.J. passed away due to his injuries, while Mikayla remains in critical condition at New York-Presbyterian Hospital Columbia. Forensic evidence linked St. Hubert to the crime scene.

As the investigation unfolded, police released St. Hubert's name and photos, urging the public to come forward with any information. The NYPD also launched a task force to track down the suspect, who had a $69,000 price on his head. The Western Beef supermarket chain contributed an additional $50,000 to the reward money, further incentivizing the public to assist in the search.

Community leaders, including Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, praised the NYPD for their efforts in apprehending the suspect, saying, "We can breathe a lot better now that the person who we believe committed the heinous crime of stabbing two of our young people is now behind bars." The NYPD's swift action brought closure to the families of the victims, particularly P.J.'s mother, Aricka McClinton, who expressed gratitude to the police and thanked them for catching the perpetrator, saying, "My baby can rest in peace."

The case highlights concerns about safety in public housing developments, with Mayor de Blasio acknowledging the need for increased security measures. Commissioner Bratton announced plans to deploy additional officers to NYCHA buildings, citing the upcoming graduation of new NYPD recruits in July. The incident also raises awareness about the importance of being vigilant and aware of one's surroundings, especially for young people, as emphasized by Rochelle Copeland, who lost her 18-year-old daughter to a similar stabbing in the same area on Friday.

reference: – Police in New York City arrested a 27-year-old man tonight accused of fatally stabbing a 6-year-old boy and injuring his 7-year-old playmate over the weekend, reports Gothamist. Daniel St. Hubert is in custody, the arrest made soon after police publicly identified him as a suspect. As the Daily News explains, the break in the case came when investigators matched DNA found on a knife left at the scene with the state's criminal database. Hubert has a long criminal record for offenses including assault and had been paroled on May 23, reports the New York Times. Police haven't called him a suspect in another fatal stabbing that occurred just two nights earlier, but the investigation is continuing. The same brand of knife—a model discontinued 10 years ago—was left behind in the slaying of 18-year-old Tanaya Copeland, and the man seen fleeing that scene had the same general description as Hubert. The 6-year-old boy who was killed, PJ Avitto, had gotten into an elevator with friend Mikayla Capers in their Brooklyn housing complex before being attacked. Mikayla remains in critical condition.

final_summary:
A seismic shift occurred in Tunisia, a North African country, where a wave of protests swept through the streets, culminating in the ousting of President Zine El Abidine Ben Ali, who fled to Saudi Arabia on Friday. This event marked a significant turning point in the region, where authoritarian regimes had long been the norm. However, the protests were not a sudden phenomenon; they were the culmination of decades of simmering discontent, fueled by economic woes, corruption, and a sense of disillusionment with the status quo.

Rising food prices, unemployment, and a lack of political freedom were among the key issues that resonated deeply with the Tunisian people. These grievances were echoed by protesters in other countries, such as Egypt, where chants against President Hosni Mubarak reflected the Tunisian cry of "down, down with Hosni Mubarak!" The international community watched closely, with Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton issuing a statement criticizing Arab leadership and the region's stagnant politics.

Al Jazeera broadcast live coverage of the protests, highlighting the slogan "the street creates change." Social media platforms like Facebook played a crucial role in mobilizing the protests, with hashtags like #Tunisia trending worldwide. Mohamed Bouazizi, a 26-year-old university graduate, immolated himself in a city square in December, sparking widespread outrage and galvanizing the protests. His act of defiance echoed the frustrations of many Tunisians, who felt that their voices were ignored by the authorities.

As the protests gained momentum, the Tunisian government struggled to maintain control. On Saturday, the country's constitutional court ruled that Ben Ali's departure was permanent, paving the way for a new government. Fouad Mebazaa, the president of the lower house of parliament, was sworn in as the new chief of state, promising to hold elections within 60 days. However, the transition was marred by violence, with reports of looting, arson, and clashes between protesters and security forces.

The situation in Tunisia served as a stark reminder of the fragility of authoritarian regimes in the Arab world, particularly in countries like Iraq, where the US-led invasion in 2003 created a power vacuum that allowed extremist groups to flourish. As the dust settled, it became clear that the protests had tapped into deeper currents of discontent, fueled by economic woes, corruption, and a sense of disillusionment with the status quo. The fall of Ben Ali sent shockwaves throughout the region, raising hopes that other autocratic leaders might soon follow suit.

In the aftermath of Ben Ali's departure, the Tunisian government faced numerous challenges, including restoring order, addressing the humanitarian crisis, and navigating the complex web of alliances and rivalries within the country. The international community watched closely, with France announcing the launch of investigations into suspicious money transfers between Tunisia and France. As the situation continued to unfold, it remained to be seen whether the new government would be able to consolidate power and usher in a new era of democratic reform.

The legacy of Ben Ali's rule hung heavy over the country, with many Tunisians expressing fears about the potential for revenge attacks by loyalists. The Interior Ministry, once a bastion of Ben Ali's power, was now a focal point of contention, with reports emerging of security forces loyal to the former regime attempting to discredit the protesters and sow chaos. As the situation continued to evolve, one thing was clear: the people of Tunisia had spoken, and their voices would not be silenced.

history.draft:
In the tumultuous landscape of the Arab world, a seismic shift occurred in Tunisia, a North African country, where a wave of protests swept through the streets, culminating in the ousting of President Zine El Abidine Ben Ali, who fled to Saudi Arabia on Friday. This pivotal moment marked a significant turning point in the region, where authoritarian regimes had long been the norm. The protests, however, were not a sudden phenomenon; they were the culmination of decades of simmering discontent, fueled by economic woes, corruption, and a sense of disillusionment with the status quo.

The protests began in response to rising food prices, unemployment, and a lack of political freedom, issues that resonated deeply with the Tunisian people. As the situation unfolded, the international community watched closely, with Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton issuing a scathing critique of Arab leadership and the region's stagnant politics. Meanwhile, Al Jazeera broadcast live coverage of the protests, dubbing them "the street creates change." Social media platforms like Facebook played a crucial role in mobilizing the protests, with hashtags like #Tunisia trending worldwide.

At the epicenter of the protests was Mohamed Bouazizi, a 26-year-old university graduate who immolated himself in a city square in December, sparking widespread outrage and galvanizing the protests. His act of defiance echoed the frustrations of many Tunisians, who felt that their voices were ignored by the authorities. In Egypt, similar sentiments were brewing, with protesters chanting slogans against Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, echoing the Tunisian cry of "down, down with Hosni Mubarak!"

As the protests gained momentum, the Tunisian government struggled to maintain control. On Saturday, the country's constitutional court ruled that Ben Ali's departure was permanent, paving the way for a new government. Fouad Mebazaa, the president of the lower house of parliament, was sworn in as the new chief of state, promising to hold elections within 60 days. However, the transition was marred by violence, with reports of looting, arson, and clashes between protesters and security forces.

The situation in Tunisia served as a stark reminder of the fragility of authoritarian regimes in the Arab world, particularly in countries like Iraq, where the US-led invasion in 2003 had left a power vacuum that allowed extremist groups to flourish. As the dust settled, it became clear that the protests had tapped into deeper currents of discontent, fueled by economic woes, corruption, and a sense of disillusionment with the status quo. The fall of Ben Ali sent shockwaves throughout the region, raising hopes that other autocratic leaders might soon follow suit.

In the aftermath of Ben Ali's departure, the Tunisian government faced numerous challenges, including restoring order, addressing the humanitarian crisis, and navigating the complex web of alliances and rivalries within the country. The international community watched closely, with France announcing the launch of investigations into suspicious money transfers between Tunisia and France. As the situation continued to unfold, it remained to be seen whether the new government would be able to consolidate power and usher in a new era of democratic reform.

Meanwhile, the legacy of Ben Ali's rule hung heavy over the country, with many Tunisians expressing fears about the potential for revenge attacks by loyalists. The Interior Ministry, once a bastion of Ben Ali's power, was now a focal point of contention, with reports emerging of security forces loyal to the former regime attempting to discredit the protesters and sow chaos. As the situation continued to evolve, one thing was clear: the people of Tunisia had spoken, and their voices would not be silenced.

reference: – Tunisia got a new interim president today—parliamentary leader Fouad Mebazaa—who is technically the third leader in 24 hours, notes the Los Angeles Times. He takes over from the previous interim president, who briefly held power when longtime leader El Abidine Ben Ali fled the country in the face of mass protests over rising prices, joblessness, corruption, repression, you name it. The storyline continues to shape up as one that could set a historical precedent—an Arab populace rising up against its leader. Anthony Shadid, New York Times: "The most enthusiastic suggested it was the Arab world’s Gdansk, the birthplace of Solidarity in Poland, which heralded the end to Communist rule in Eastern Europe. That seemed premature, particularly because the contours of the government emerging in Tunisia were still unclear." Shadi Hamid, Huffington Post: "No one should underestimate what happened yesterday in Tunisia. If the revolution succeeds, it may very well prove to be one of the most important moments in recent Arab history. It will alter the calculus not only for Arab regimes—who are watching very, very nervously—but for Western powers that have long oriented their Middle East policy around seemingly stable, autocratic governments." Click for the latest as chaos continues on the streets.

final_summary:
Following the devastating terrorist attacks in Paris, the international hacking collective Anonymous has escalated its campaign against the Islamic State (IS), declaring "war" on the militant group and vowing to dismantle its online presence. This marked a significant escalation of Anonymous' efforts, which began in earnest after the Charlie Hebdo attacks in January, when the group launched its initial campaign against IS, dubbed #OpISIS, with the aim of exposing and destroying websites, social media accounts, and email addresses associated with the group.

The group, known for its anonymous nature and decentralized structure, has been actively working to identify and expose IS supporters on social media platforms, particularly Twitter, where it has claimed to have taken down over 3,800 pro-IS accounts since the Paris attacks. This latest move is part of its new #OpParis campaign, which aims to further disrupt IS' online activities. As part of its new campaign, Anonymous has released a list of over 1,000 Twitter accounts that it claims IS supporters use to spread propaganda.

Experts caution that Anonymous' efforts may not be as effective as they seem. Joe Gallop, head of the hacktivism intelligence practice at iSight Partners, notes that IS supporters can easily re-establish new accounts and that the group's use of encrypted communication methods makes it difficult to track their online activities. Moreover, Michael Smith, principal and chief operating officer at Kronos Advisory, warns that uncoordinated efforts by groups like Anonymous can disrupt law enforcement investigations and inadvertently benefit the enemy.

Ghost Security Group, a subgroup of hacktivists that has worked closely with Anonymous, has also been actively tracking IS online activities. The group has handed over valuable information to government officials, which has helped disrupt planned attacks in Tunisia. Smith, who serves as an intermediary between Ghost Security Group and the government, believes that outside support is crucial in combating IS' online presence, but emphasizes the need for coordination with law enforcement agencies to ensure that efforts do not inadvertently aid the enemy.

Ghost Security Group, formerly known as GhostSec, was one of the two hacktivist groups that worked with Anonymous to expose the Twitter accounts of ISIS supporters following the Charlie Hebdo attacks earlier this year. The group has since shed its outlaw image and is now working to provide valuable information to government officials to combat IS' online presence.

In a separate development, the French authorities conducted a raid in the Saint-Denis neighborhood of Paris, where three suspected terrorists were believed to be hiding. The operation resulted in gunfire exchanges between the suspects and police, but the outcome remains unclear. Meanwhile, the Islamic State has responded to Anonymous' declaration of war with a statement on its Telegram channel, urging its supporters to take precautions against cyber attacks. The group advised its followers to avoid suspicious links, change their locations using workaround technology, and rename their email addresses to avoid detection.

The Islamic State militant group (ISIS) released a statement on Monday responding to Anonymous’s declaration of “total war,” calling the hacker group “idiots” and offering guidance to pro-ISIS supporters to protect against cyber attacks. Anonymous threatened that it would launch its “biggest operation ever” against ISIS in response to the wave of shooting and suicide bomb attacks in Paris that left at least 129 people dead.

Anonymous has targeted ISIS for a number of months following the attacks on the Paris offices of satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo and its hack of U.S. CENTCOM's Twitter account. The group has uncovered the Twitter accounts of ISIS members and hacked a number of the group's sites. The hacktivist group has dismantled at least 149 of ISIS’s affiliated websites, flagged approximately 101,000 Twitter accounts and nearly 6,000 propaganda videos, according to U.S. magazine Foreign Policy estimates. Additionally, Anonymous and Ghost Security Group have also targeted Al Qaeda, another terrorist organization, by exposing the Twitter accounts of ISIS supporters following the Charlie Hebdo attacks earlier this year.

history.draft:
In the aftermath of the devastating terrorist attacks in Paris, the international hacking collective Anonymous has escalated its campaign against the Islamic State (IS), declaring "war" on the militant group and vowing to dismantle its online presence. The group, known for its anonymous nature and decentralized structure, has been actively working to identify and expose IS supporters on social media platforms, particularly Twitter, where it has claimed to have taken down over 3,800 pro-IS accounts since the Paris attacks.

This latest move marks a significant escalation of Anonymous' efforts, which began in earnest after the Charlie Hebdo attacks in January. The group's initial campaign, dubbed #OpISIS, aimed to expose and destroy websites, social media accounts, and email addresses associated with IS. Since then, Anonymous has claimed credit for shutting down several websites and exposing emails, private networks, internet addresses, and over 9,000 Twitter accounts allegedly used by IS activists.

As part of its new #OpParis campaign, Anonymous has released a list of over 1,000 Twitter accounts that it claims IS supporters use to spread propaganda. The group has also vowed to launch its "biggest operation ever" against IS, promising to use its cyber skills to "unite humanity" and warning terrorists to "expect massive cyber attacks."

However, experts caution that Anonymous' efforts may not be as effective as they seem. Joe Gallop, head of the hacktivism intelligence practice at iSight Partners, notes that IS supporters can easily re-establish new accounts and that the group's use of encrypted communication methods makes it difficult to track their online activities. Moreover, Michael Smith, principal and chief operating officer at Kronos Advisory, warns that uncoordinated efforts by groups like Anonymous can disrupt law enforcement investigations and inadvertently benefit the enemy.

Meanwhile, the Islamic State has responded to Anonymous' declaration of war with a statement on its Telegram channel, urging its supporters to take precautions against cyber attacks. The group advised its followers to avoid suspicious links, change their locations using workaround technology, and rename their email addresses to avoid detection.

In a separate development, the French authorities conducted a raid in the Saint-Denis neighborhood of Paris, where three suspected terrorists were believed to be hiding. The operation resulted in gunfire exchanges between the suspects and police, but the outcome remains unclear.

Ghost Security Group, a subgroup of hacktivists that has worked closely with Anonymous, has also been actively tracking IS online activities. The group has handed over valuable information to government officials, which has helped disrupt planned attacks in Tunisia. Smith, who serves as an intermediary between Ghost Security Group and the government, believes that outside support is crucial in combating IS' online presence, but emphasizes the need for coordination with law enforcement agencies to ensure that efforts do not inadvertently aid the enemy.

As the conflict between Anonymous and IS continues to unfold, the global community watches with bated breath, wondering whether the hacktivist group's efforts will ultimately make a meaningful difference in the fight against terrorism.

The Islamic State militant group (ISIS) released a statement on Monday responding to Anonymous’s declaration of “total war,” calling the hacker group “idiots” and offering guidance to pro-ISIS supporters to protect against cyber attacks. Anonymous threatened that it would launch its “biggest operation ever” against ISIS in response to the wave of shooting and suicide bomb attacks in Paris that left at least 129 people dead.

Anonymous has targeted ISIS for a number of months following the attacks on the Paris offices of satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo and its hack of U.S. CENTCOM's Twitter account. The group has uncovered the Twitter accounts of ISIS members and hacked a number of the group's sites. The hacktivist group has dismantled at least 149 of ISIS’s affiliated websites, flagged approximately 101,000 Twitter accounts and nearly 6,000 propaganda videos, according to U.S. magazine Foreign Policy estimates. Additionally, Anonymous and Ghost Security Group have also targeted Al Qaeda, another terrorist organization, by exposing the Twitter accounts of ISIS supporters following the Charlie Hebdo attacks earlier this year.

reference: – Anonymous is following through with its promise to target ISIS in a cyber war after the Paris terror attacks. Operating under the hashtags #opISIS and #opParis, the group has begun leaking the personal details of alleged ISIS recruiters, reports the Independent. Anonymous also has compiled lists of social media accounts linked to extremists in an attempt to have them removed, and at least one post reveals the physical address of a suspected recruiter in Europe. Another list, spotted by Tech Insider, includes more than 80 names, though the site notes that Anonymous has wrongly identified extremists before. The group is also working to take down ISIS-affiliated websites using denial-of-service attacks to overload servers. ISIS, however, seems unperturbed. "All they can do is hacking (sic) Twitter accounts, emails etc.," the terrorist group said in a statement Monday, calling Anonymous a bunch of "idiots." Still, ISIS shared tips with supporters about how to avoid cyberattacks, including altering email addresses, changing a computer's shared location, and ignoring suspicious links, per Newsweek. While Anonymous' attacks could hinder communications between ISIS supporters, analysts tell the Christian Science Monitor that they're unlikely to do much good. "What they are doing is more of simple harassment," one says. "In the end it is not going to help law enforcement to prevent an actual physical attack like what happened in Paris." (ISIS has a 24-hour help desk for jihadis having tech trouble.)

final_summary:
Researchers led by Paul Bierman, a geomorphologist at the University of Vermont, published a groundbreaking study in the journal Science on April 17. The study revealed a 2.7-million-year-old remnant of a lush, prehistoric landscape buried beneath the icy expanse of Greenland's highest point, Summit. 

Pollen and plant DNA found in the seafloor off Greenland's coast suggest that the island was once home to a tundra and patchy forests, similar to the current Arctic landscape. The study indicates that this verdant landscape remained exposed for approximately 200,000 to 1 million years before being entombed under the weight of ice. 

Bierman and his team extracted the ancient soil from beneath the Greenland Ice Sheet during the GISP2 deep-drilling project in 1993. By employing a geochemical technique called beryllium-10 dating, the researchers dated the age of the soil and unlocked its secrets. High levels of beryllium-10 in the soil, comparable to those found in long-lived Arctic landscapes, confirmed that the soil had indeed been exposed to the elements for millions of years.

The discovery has significant implications for our understanding of the Earth's climate history. Specifically, the fact that the Greenland Ice Sheet has persisted for so long, even during periods of extreme warming, suggests that it has never fully melted, despite experiencing a natural warming event 130,000 years ago. 

The study reveals that the ice sheet's persistence is due to its unique characteristics. Unlike warm-based glaciers, which erode their underlying terrain, the Greenland Ice Sheet is frozen to its bedrock, allowing it to deform like glass or warm plastic above a stationary base. This phenomenon, known as a cold-based glacier, has protected the ancient landscape beneath, preserving it for millions of years.

The researchers' findings have sparked renewed interest in the stability of the Greenland ice sheet, particularly in relation to its potential impact on sea-level rise caused by human-induced climate change. As Bierman noted, "human activity may spell the end of it." The preservation of the ancient landscape beneath the ice sheet serves as a poignant reminder of the delicate balance between the Earth's climate systems and the consequences of human actions.

The discovery of the 2.7-million-year-old soil has far-reaching implications for the field of glaciology and our understanding of the Earth's climate history. As Bierman stated, "the bottom of an ice sheet can preserve some really old landscapes." The team's research has opened doors to new avenues of inquiry, including the search for ancient landscapes in Antarctica, where the researchers hope to uncover even more secrets about the planet's distant past.

reference: – The soil under the highest point of the Greenland Ice Sheet, scientists have learned, is 2.7 million years old, LiveScience reports. In other words, the silt buried under thousands of feet of ice "has been preserved from beyond the dawn of humankind," says Paul Bierman, who determined the soil's age through a geochemical dating process. The findings also suggest Greenland wasn't always as frosty as it is today: Instead, it was a tundra for between 200,000 and one million years before it was encased in ice. It was once home to bits of forest, the scientists found using offshore plant DNA. In other words, as the International Business Times has it, "Greenland really was green." The Greenland Ice Sheet that's there now, however, has been remarkably durable: The findings imply that it made it through a strong period of warming some 130,000 years ago. "It's unlikely the ice sheet has disappeared for significant periods of time in the last three million years, at least at this one pinprick on this big island," Bierman says. "Now human activity may spell the end of it." (What else lies under Greenland's ice? A canyon that dwarfs the Grand Canyon, an aquifer holding 100 billion tons of water, and two huge lakes.)

final_summary:
Tamerlan Tsarnaev, the older brother involved in the Boston Marathon bombings, lay unclaimed at the Boston medical examiner's office for nearly two weeks following his death on April 19. However, his body was eventually transferred to a funeral home in Worcester, Massachusetts, after his uncle, Ruslan Tsarni, arranged for the transfer. Tsarni had previously spoken out against the alleged bombings, condemning the perpetrators as "losers."

Tsarnaev's death certificate lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," with the manner of death classified as homicide. The document indicates that Tsarnaev was shot by police and run over by a car during a confrontation with law enforcement.

The funeral home, owned by Peter Stefan, is working to arrange a burial for Tsarnaev, despite facing resistance from several cemeteries in Massachusetts and neighboring states. Stefan expressed frustration with the situation, stating that everyone deserves to be buried, regardless of the circumstances surrounding their death. He noted that his funeral home has experience handling Muslim services, including washing and preparing the body for burial.

Several cemeteries have declined to accept Tsarnaev's body due to concerns about potential backlash from the public. Stefan acknowledged these fears, saying that some people might be upset if Tsarnaev were buried near their loved ones. To address these concerns, Stefan's funeral home will maintain a police detail outside the premises throughout the weekend.

Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the younger brother, is currently being held at a federal Bureau of Prisons medical facility in Devens, Massachusetts, where he faces charges related to the use of a weapon of mass destruction. He is receiving treatment for gunshot wounds sustained during a shootout with police on April 19, two days after the bombings occurred.

An independent government review is underway to examine whether intelligence and law enforcement agencies could have done more to investigate Tamerlan Tsarnaev prior to the attack. President Barack Obama has defended U.S. efforts, stating that preventing attacks by self-radicalized individuals is extremely challenging.

Tsarnaev's official cause of death will be listed as gunshot wounds and blunt trauma, according to a law enforcement source. As the process of arranging a burial continues, Tsarnaev's widow, Katherine Russell, remains under scrutiny, with her lawyer claiming she had no knowledge of her husband's plans to bomb the Boston Marathon.

Residents of North Attleboro, Massachusetts, protested the presence of Tsarnaev's body in their town two weeks ago. The hearse carrying his body was initially taken to a funeral home near the Rhode Island state line before being moved to Stefan's funeral home in Worcester.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of covering for the bombing suspects, including Azamat Tazhayakov and Dias Kadyrbayev, who removed items from Tsarnaev's dorm room after the bombings. The FBI is examining a laptop seized from Kadyrbayev, which is believed to contain evidence related to the bombings.

The Boston area continues to recover from the aftermath of the bombings, with 12 people still hospitalized with injuries. Gun rights advocates, such as the National Rifle Association, have pointed to the Boston Marathon bombings as an example of why citizens should be allowed to carry firearms for self-defense.

history.draft:
Tamerlan Tsarnaev, the older brother involved in the Boston Marathon bombings, lay unclaimed at the Boston medical examiner's office for nearly two weeks following his death on April 19. His body was eventually taken into custody by a funeral home, Graham Putnam & Mahoney Funeral Parlors in Worcester, Massachusetts, after his uncle, Ruslan Tsarni, a Maryland resident, arranged for the transfer. Tsarni had previously condemned the alleged bombings, calling the perpetrators "losers."

Tsarnaev's death certificate, obtained by CNN, lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," with the manner of death classified as homicide. The document indicates that Tsarnaev was shot by police and run over by a car during a confrontation with law enforcement.

The funeral home, owned by Peter Stefan, is working to arrange a burial for Tsarnaev, despite facing resistance from several cemeteries in Massachusetts and neighboring states. Stefan expressed frustration with the situation, stating that everyone deserves to be buried, regardless of the circumstances surrounding their death. He is receiving treatment for gunshot wounds sustained during a shootout with police.

Meanwhile, an independent government review is underway to examine whether intelligence and law enforcement agencies could have done more to investigate Tamerlan Tsarnaev prior to the attack. President Barack Obama defended U.S. efforts, stating that preventing attacks by self-radicalized individuals is extremely challenging.

On Friday, a law enforcement source revealed that Tsarnaev's official cause of death would be listed as gunshot wounds and blunt trauma. As the process of arranging a burial continues, Tsarnaev's widow, Katherine Russell, remains under scrutiny, with her lawyer claiming she had no knowledge of her husband's plans to bomb the Boston Marathon.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of covering for the bombing suspects, including Azamat Tazhayakov and Dias Kadyrbayev, who removed items from Tsarnaev's dorm room after the bombings. Gun rights advocates, such as National Rifle Association Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre, have pointed to the Boston Marathon bombings as an example of why citizens should be allowed to carry firearms for self-defense.

Two weeks ago, on Saturday, residents of North Attleboro, Massachusetts, took to the streets to protest the presence of Tsarnaev's body in their town. The hearse carrying his body was initially taken to a funeral home near the Rhode Island state line before being moved to Stefan's funeral home in Worcester.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of covering for the bombing suspects, including Azamat Tazhayakov and Dias Kadyrbayev, who removed items from Tsarnaev's dorm room after the bombings. The FBI is examining a laptop seized from Kadyrbayev, which is believed to contain evidence related to the bombings.

In addition, the Boston area continues to recover from the aftermath of the bombings, with 12 people still hospitalized with injuries. Gun rights advocates, such as National Rifle Association Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre, have pointed to the Boston Marathon bombings as an example of why citizens should be allowed to carry firearms for self-defense.

history.draft:
Tamerlan Tsarnaev, the older brother involved in the Boston Marathon bombings, lay unclaimed at the Boston medical examiner's office for nearly two weeks following his death on April 19. However, his body was eventually transferred to a funeral home in Worcester, Massachusetts, after his uncle, Ruslan Tsarni, arranged for the transfer. Tsarni had previously spoken out against the alleged bombings, condemning the perpetrators as "losers."

Tsarnaev's death certificate lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," with the manner of death classified as homicide. The document indicates that Tsarnaev was shot by police and run over by a car during a confrontation with law enforcement.

The funeral home, owned by Peter Stefan, is working to arrange a burial for Tsarnaev, despite facing resistance from several cemeteries in Massachusetts and neighboring states. Stefan expressed frustration with the situation, stating that everyone deserves to be buried, regardless of the circumstances surrounding their death. He noted that his funeral home has experience handling Muslim services, including washing and preparing the body for burial.

Several cemeteries have declined to accept Tsarnaev's body due to concerns about potential backlash from the public. Stefan acknowledged these fears, saying that some people might be upset if Tsarnaev were buried near their loved ones. To address these concerns, Stefan's funeral home will maintain a police detail outside the premises throughout the weekend.

Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the younger brother, is currently being held at a federal Bureau of Prisons medical facility in Devens, Massachusetts, where he faces charges related to the use of a weapon of mass destruction. He is receiving treatment for gunshot wounds sustained during a shootout with police on April 19, two days after the bombings occurred.

An independent government review is underway to examine whether intelligence and law enforcement agencies could have done more to investigate Tamerlan Tsarnaev prior to the attack. President Barack Obama has defended U.S. efforts, stating that preventing attacks by self-radicalized individuals is extremely challenging.

Tsarnaev's official cause of death will be listed as gunshot wounds and blunt trauma, according to a law enforcement source. As the process of arranging a burial continues, Tsarnaev's widow, Katherine Russell, remains under scrutiny, with her lawyer claiming she had no knowledge of her husband's plans to bomb the Boston Marathon.

Residents of North Attleboro, Massachusetts, protested the presence of Tsarnaev's body in their town two weeks ago. The hearse carrying his body was initially taken to a funeral home near the Rhode Island state line before being moved to Stefan's funeral home in Worcester.

Three friends of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev have been accused of covering for the bombing suspects, including Azamat Tazhayakov and Dias Kadyrbayev, who removed items from Tsarnaev's dorm room after the bombings. The FBI is examining a laptop seized from Kadyrbayev, which is believed to contain evidence related to the bombings.

The Boston area continues to recover from the aftermath of the bombings, with 12 people still hospitalized with injuries. Gun rights advocates, such as the National Rifle Association, have pointed to the Boston Marathon bombings as an example of why citizens should be allowed to carry firearms for self-defense.

reference: – The death certificate of Tamerlan Tsarnaev apparently will hold no surprises. While Massachusetts officials have yet to release it publicly, the owner of the Worcester funeral home with his body said it lists the cause of death as "gunshot wounds of torso and extremities" and "blunt trauma to head and torso," reports CNN. That gibes with what authorities have previously said: Tamerlan was hit by police bullets and run over by Dzhokhar as the younger brother fled their shootout in a stolen SUV. The certificate lists the time of death as 1:35am on April 19, reports AP. The funeral home, meanwhile, is still striking out in finding a cemetery willing to take the body. "There’s a fear there,” owner Peter Stefan of the Graham Putnam and Mahoney funeral parlor tells WBZ. "Will people want to go to the cemetery, or will people be upset that somebody who’s a terrorist will be buried next to their Uncle Freddy.”

final_summary:
An 80-year-old woman took her own life at the historic Berkeley City Club in Berkeley, California, prompting the evacuation of hotel guests and residents. The incident occurred on the third floor of the six-story landmark building, where the woman used a toxic chemical, sodium azide, in her room. 

According to Berkeley Police, the incident began around 1:56 p.m. when emergency services received a call reporting "a person down." Responders discovered a potential suicide involving hazardous materials, leading to a coordinated effort to safely remove the substance. Berkeley Police, Berkeley Fire, and the University of California Police Department collaborated in the response.

Hazardous materials crews and bomb squad technicians were dispatched to the scene, and Durant Avenue between Telegraph and Shattuck avenues was temporarily closed to ensure public safety. Although the exact number of evacuees was unknown, there were no reported injuries as of Tuesday evening.

Berkeley Police Officer Ethell Wilson stated that the area was secured, and the Berkeley City Club issued a statement assuring its members that none were involved or harmed in the incident. However, the relationship between the deceased woman and the club remained unclear. 

The Oakland Tribune reported that the woman left a note warning authorities of the hazardous situation. Sodium azide, a colorless salt commonly found in car airbag systems, becomes acutely toxic when mixed with water. 

Berkeley City Club, designed by Julia Morgan, is a hotel and private social club featuring amenities such as an indoor swimming pool, restaurant, hair salon, and meeting spaces. Berkeleyside reported on the incident, citing multiple sources including the original story by Kristin J. Bender.

history.draft:
On Tuesday, a tragic event unfolded at the historic Berkeley City Club in Berkeley, California, resulting in the evacuation of hotel guests and residents. A woman in her 80s, later identified by the Oakland Tribune as having taken her own life, used a toxic chemical, sodium azide, in her room on the third floor of the six-story landmark building. 

According to Berkeley Police Sergeant Joe Okies, the incident occurred around 1:56 p.m. when emergency services received a call reporting "a person down." Upon arrival, responders discovered a potential suicide involving hazardous materials, prompting a coordinated effort to safely remove the substance. Berkeley Police, Berkeley Fire, and the University of California Police Department collaborated in the response.

Berkeley Police Officer Ethell Wilson stated that hazardous materials crews and bomb squad technicians were dispatched to the scene, and Durant Avenue between Telegraph and Shattuck avenues was temporarily closed to ensure public safety. Although the exact number of evacuees was unknown, there were no reported injuries as of Tuesday evening.

As the investigation progressed, the Berkeley City Club issued a statement assuring its members that none were involved or harmed in the incident. However, the relationship between the deceased woman and the club remained unclear. The Berkeley Fire Department's initial response led to a handover to law enforcement, with Berkeley Police assuming control of the situation.

Deputy Fire Chief Avery Webb explained that the presence of the hazardous material necessitated a law enforcement response. The Berkeley City Club, designed by Julia Morgan, is a hotel and private social club featuring amenities such as an indoor swimming pool, restaurant, hair salon, and meeting spaces.

Berkeleyside, a local news outlet, provided updates on the developing story, which was also covered by the Oakland Tribune. Journalist Kristin J. Bender reported on the incident, highlighting the use of sodium azide, a colorless salt commonly found in car airbag systems, which becomes acutely toxic when mixed with water.

reference: – The landmark Berkeley City Club was evacuated yesterday after an 80-year-old resident used a toxic chemical to kill herself in her room. Police in the California city say Hazmat teams and bomb squad technicians were called in after a note was found outside the woman's third-story apartment warning of the danger, the Oakland Tribune reports. Police say a "known quantity of what appeared to be a hazardous material has been removed" and steps are being taken to make the building—which houses both hotel guests and residents—safe, reports Berkeleyside. The woman is believed to have used sodium azide, a colorless salt used as the gas-forming component in car airbag systems. It becomes extremely toxic when mixed with water.

final_summary:
In the United States, the relations between the White House and the press corps covering it have reached a critical point under the Trump administration. To address this tension, comedian Samantha Bee is organizing a gala dinner, "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," set to take place on April 29, 2017, at the Willard Hotel in Washington, D.C., coinciding with the annual White House Correspondents' Association dinner. The event aims to provide a platform for comedians to tackle the Trump administration without restraint, addressing the strained dynamics between the White House and the press.

Bee, host of the TBS satirical-comedy program "Full Frontal," revealed that the concept emerged in November 2016 after the presidential election, when she and her team discussed whether the traditional dinner would continue under a Trump presidency. They decided to create an alternative event, allowing them to tackle topics and roast the President in a manner they deemed fitting. Bee emphasized that their initiative is not intended to supplant the WHCA dinner but rather serve as a backup plan in case the traditional event does not materialize.

The WHCA dinner, which has been held annually since 1983, typically features high-profile celebrities and comedians, such as Seth Meyers and Stephen Colbert, who have used these platforms to critique the administration. However, the current atmosphere surrounding the event has become increasingly awkward, with journalists and celebrities struggling to navigate the delicate balance between humor and offense. In contrast, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" promises to provide a space for comedians to tackle the Trump administration without hesitation.

The event will also aim to raise awareness about the challenges faced by journalists, with all proceeds going to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Bee expressed her concerns about the press being under attack under the Trump administration, highlighting the need for a platform where unsung journalists can be recognized and celebrated. The evening will feature a diverse range of performers, although specific details remain undisclosed.

In recent times, late-night hosts have adapted to changing viewer habits by incorporating innovative approaches to engage their audiences. Shows like "Full Frontal" have experimented with social media, publishing humorous content on platforms like Medium and sharing behind-the-scenes insights via Facebook Live. Bee's decision to organize the alternative dinner reflects this trend, as she seeks to connect with her audience and maintain relevance in a rapidly evolving media landscape.

Donald Trump's strained relationship with the press has led to speculation about whether he will participate or cancel the event altogether. As the date of the WHCA dinner draws near, the fate of the traditional event remains uncertain. Meanwhile, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" stands as a testament to the resilience of satire and the importance of free speech in the face of adversity. By providing a platform for comedians to tackle the Trump administration, Bee hopes to inject some much-needed levity into the polarized discourse and remind Americans of the value of a robust and independent press.

history.draft:
In the United States, the relations between the White House and the press corps covering it have reached a critical point under the Trump administration. To address this tension, comedian Samantha Bee is organizing a gala dinner, "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," set to take place on April 29, 2017, at the Willard Hotel in Washington, D.C., coinciding with the annual White House Correspondents' Association dinner. The event aims to provide a platform for comedians to tackle the Trump administration without restraint, addressing the strained dynamics between the White House and the press.

Bee, host of the TBS satirical-comedy program "Full Frontal," revealed that the concept emerged in November 2016 after the presidential election, when she and her team discussed whether the traditional dinner would continue under a Trump presidency. They decided to create an alternative event, allowing them to tackle topics and roast the President in a manner they deemed fitting. Bee emphasized that their initiative is not intended to supplant the WHCA dinner but rather serve as a backup plan in case the traditional event does not materialize.

The WHCA dinner, which has been held annually since 1983, typically features high-profile celebrities and comedians, such as Seth Meyers and Stephen Colbert, who have used these platforms to critique the administration. However, the current atmosphere surrounding the event has become increasingly awkward, with journalists and celebrities struggling to navigate the delicate balance between humor and offense. In contrast, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" promises to provide a space for comedians to tackle the Trump administration without hesitation.

The event will also aim to raise awareness about the challenges faced by journalists, with all proceeds going to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Bee expressed her concerns about the press being under attack under the Trump administration, highlighting the need for a platform where unsung journalists can be recognized and celebrated. The evening will feature a diverse range of performers, although specific details remain undisclosed.

In recent times, late-night hosts have adapted to changing viewer habits by incorporating innovative approaches to engage their audiences. Shows like "Full Frontal" have experimented with social media, publishing humorous content on platforms like Medium and sharing behind-the-scenes insights via Facebook Live. Bee's decision to organize the alternative dinner reflects this trend, as she seeks to connect with her audience and maintain relevance in a rapidly evolving media landscape.

Donald Trump's strained relationship with the press has led to speculation about whether he will participate or cancel the event altogether. As the date of the WHCA dinner draws near, the fate of the traditional event remains uncertain. Meanwhile, Bee's "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner" stands as a testament to the resilience of satire and the importance of free speech in the face of adversity. By providing a platform for comedians to tackle the Trump administration, Bee hopes to inject some much-needed levity into the polarized discourse and remind Americans of the value of a robust and independent press.

reference: – If Donald Trump chooses to roast journalists, celebrities, and politicians at the annual White House Correspondents' Dinner in Washington, DC, this year, it'll be with the knowledge that he's being brutally mocked not too far away. Samantha Bee of TBS' Full Frontal says she'll host celebrities, journalists, and comedians at a Trump roast at the Willard Hotel in Washington on April 29. Bee tells the New York Times that the goal of the event, dubbed "Not the White House Correspondents' Dinner," is not to "supersede" the official dinner scheduled for the same night, but simply to "ensure that we get to properly roast the president." She promises "plenty of surprises," per Variety. "If you're not careful you just might learn something. Specifically, you'll learn how screwed we'd be without a free press," she adds. It's not yet clear when or how the event will be broadcast, or even who will attend, but Bee says proceeds will go to the Committee to Protect Journalists. Whether the official Correspondents' dinner will happen is also unclear given Trump's tumultuous relationship with the press, his history with the dinner, and "his oft-demonstrated thin skin," reports the Times. The Independent notes the dinner has been an annual occurrence since 1983.

final_summary:
Italian authorities have made a significant breakthrough in cracking a cryptic code linked to the notorious 'Ndrangheta mafia organization, according to the BBC. The coded text, discovered during an investigation into a high-profile murder in Rome in January last year, is believed to be part of an oath-taking process used by the 'Ndrangheta to initiate new members into their ranks, specifically within the "San Luca" clan. According to Renato Cortese, head of the police rapid response team in Rome, the document reveals the formula that aspiring members must recite to become part of the 'Ndrangheta. The coded message, inscribed on three sheets of note paper alongside firearms and ammunition, was deciphered after two dedicated police officers with a passion for puzzles spent weeks unraveling its secrets, as reported by the BBC's Rome correspondent Alan Johnston. 

The translation is described as being rich in symbolism, providing insight into the 'Ndrangheta's secretive nature. The document's discovery highlights the organization's continued influence and adaptability, particularly given its involvement in large-scale business ventures and its status as one of Europe's most feared and influential crime syndicates. The 'Ndrangheta, rooted in Calabria, a region in southern Italy, has surpassed Sicily's Cosa Nostra as Italy's most powerful mafia, according to recent assessments. The network operates across Europe and has established connections with Colombian drug cartels, cementing its position as Europe's largest cocaine smuggler. This finding underscores the complexity of the 'Ndrangheta's operations and the challenges faced by law enforcement agencies in combating this formidable force.

In a significant breakthrough, Italian authorities claim to have cracked a cryptic code hidden within a mysterious document linked to the notorious 'Ndrangheta mafia organization. Discovered during an investigation into a high-profile murder in Rome in January last year, the coded text is believed to be part of an ancient oath-taking ritual used by the 'Ndrangheta to initiate new members into their ranks. According to Renato Cortese, head of the police rapid response team in Rome, the document reveals the formula that aspiring members must recite to become part of the 'Ndrangheta, specifically within the "San Luca" clan.

The coded message, inscribed on three sheets of note paper alongside firearms and ammunition, was deciphered after two dedicated police officers with a passion for puzzles spent weeks unraveling its secrets. Described as a mix of Arabic, Cyrillic, and Chinese-type script, the translation is said to be rich in symbolism, shedding light on the mystical aura surrounding the induction of new recruits into the 'Ndrangheta fold. This enigmatic aspect of the mafia's inner workings has long fascinated investigators, who see it as a testament to the organization's enduring commitment to tradition and secrecy.

As one of Europe's most feared and influential crime syndicates, the 'Ndrangheta has expanded its reach beyond Italy's borders, establishing connections with Colombian drug cartels and solidifying its position as the continent's largest cocaine smuggler. With its roots firmly planted in Calabria, a region in southern Italy, the 'Ndrangheta has surpassed Sicily's Cosa Nostra as Italy's most powerful mafia, according to recent assessments. The discovery of this coded document serves as a poignant reminder of the 'Ndrangheta's continued influence and adaptability, even as it navigates the complexities of modern organized crime.

According to BBC Rome correspondent Alan Johnston, the finding highlights the 'Ndrangheta's ability to balance its involvement in large-scale business ventures with adherence to traditional practices, including the use of arcane codes and rituals. As the Italian authorities continue to grapple with the ever-evolving nature of organized crime, the deciphered text offers a rare glimpse into the inner workings of the 'Ndrangheta, underscoring the need for sustained efforts to combat this formidable force.

reference: – "For the baptism, five people are needed—no more and no less," reads the coded instructions. But this "baptism" isn't the usual sort: It's for new members of the 'Ndrangheta crime syndicate in Italy, reports the Telegraph. Police discovered the instructions during a murder investigation, and two officers who like to solve puzzles got busy deciphering the text, which resembles ancient Egyptian and Greek. The big break came when they figured out the letter C, and the rest fell into place. It's oh-so-dramatic stuff: The initiation must take place in a "sacred, sacrosanct, and secure" location, and the newbie must cut his skin with a knife in order to swear a blood oath—"an eternal allegiance to the honored society.” The BBC reports that the 'Ndrangheta is thought to be the most powerful mafia in Italy, with a specialty in cocaine smuggling. "Finding such a document shows that even if they are projected towards big businesses and are a criminal group with a global presence, they still use archaic systems," says a police official in Rome. (The syndicate apparently isn't a fan of the new pope.)

final_summary:
Jamie Merrett, a tetraplegic man from Devizes in Wiltshire, was left severely brain-damaged after a nurse accidentally switched off his life-supporting ventilator in January 2009. The incident occurred shortly after Merrett had installed a bedside camera in his home due to concerns over the quality of care he was receiving. The footage, obtained by the BBC through a confidential report by Wiltshire social services, showed nurse Violetta Aylward turning off the ventilator, leaving Merrett without oxygen for 21 minutes before paramedics were finally able to restart the machine.

Merrett, who had been paralyzed from the neck down since a road accident in 2002, used a voice-activated computer to communicate and possessed a relatively high level of cognitive function prior to the incident. However, his sister Karren Reynolds reported that his condition significantly deteriorated after the accident, with his level of understanding dropping to that of a young child.

A confidential report by Wiltshire social services revealed that the agency supplying Aylward, Ambition 24Hours, was aware of its responsibility to provide nurses trained in the use of ventilators but lacked adequate systems to verify the qualifications of its staff. The Nursing and Midwifery Council suspended Aylward pending an investigation into the incident, citing that a nurse should work within their level of competence and possess the necessary skills to perform their duties.

The NHS Wiltshire Primary Care Trust acknowledged the error and stated that it would take steps to prevent similar incidents in the future. The trust also implemented additional measures to ensure the safety of patients under its care. As the incident remains the subject of ongoing litigation, the trust cannot comment further on the matter. Merrett's family is considering taking legal action against those responsible for the incident, with his solicitor describing it as one of the most severe cases of nursing negligence he had encountered.

final_summary:
On Monday, during a Rose Garden press conference at the White House, US President Donald Trump held a gathering to discuss the recently agreed-upon trade deal between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico. In this setting, Trump called upon Cecilia Vega, a reporter from ABC News, to pose a question. Before she received the microphone, Trump made a comment suggesting Vega was surprised or shocked that he had chosen her to speak next. Vega responded, stating she wasn't in a state of shock, to which Trump retorted, "She's shocked that I picked her." That's okay. I know you're not thanking; you never do. 

The initial transcript of the event, released by the White House several hours later, incorrectly quoted Trump's words, changing them to "I know you're not thanking. You never do." On Tuesday, the White House issued a correction to the transcript, accurately reflecting Trump's actual comments. Vega continued to pursue her line of questioning, asking about Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh, but Trump initially declined to address the topic, limiting himself to discussing trade-related matters. He later returned to Vega, allowing her to ask her question regarding Kavanaugh. The exchange garnered significant attention online, with Vega expressing her perspective on the situation, stating that a news conference allows reporters to ask any question they deem relevant under the First Amendment.

history.draft:
On Monday, during a Rose Garden press conference at the White House, US President Donald Trump held a gathering to discuss the recently agreed-upon trade deal between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico. In this setting, Trump called upon Cecilia Vega, a reporter from ABC News, to pose a question. Before she received the microphone, Trump made a comment suggesting Vega was surprised or shocked that he had chosen her to speak next. Vega responded, stating she wasn't in a state of shock, to which Trump retorted, "She's shocked that I picked her." That's okay. I know you're not thanking; you never do. 

The initial transcript of the event, released by the White House several hours later, incorrectly quoted Trump's words, changing them to "I know you're not thanking. You never do." On Tuesday, the White House issued a correction to the transcript, accurately reflecting Trump's actual comments. Vega continued to pursue her line of questioning, asking about Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh, but Trump initially declined to address the topic, limiting himself to discussing trade-related matters. He later returned to Vega, allowing her to ask her question regarding Kavanaugh. The exchange garnered significant attention online, with Vega expressing her perspective on the situation, stating that a news conference allows reporters to ask any question they deem relevant under the First Amendment.

reference: – President Trump made headlines Monday when he delivered a slam to ABC News journalist Cecilia Vega. Then the White House kept the story alive with a controversial official transcript—one that toned down Trump's remark—before fixing it after an outcry. You can watch the original back and forth for yourself in this video. Trump selects Vega to ask the first question at a news conference, then jokes that she is shocked at that. "I'm not, thank you, Mr. President," Vega responded. Trump, apparently mishearing her, then says, "I know you're not thinking. You never do." However, Politico reports that the first version of the White House transcript, out later Monday, quotes Trump as saying: "I know you're not thanking. You never do." The transcript was spotted by Voice of America journalist Steve Herman, who tweeted the pertinent section and said he wasn't buying it. "I was sitting just behind her in the Rose Garden and we all clearly heard him say: "I know you're not thinking. You never do.” As critics accused the White House of whitewashing the exchange, a revised transcript appeared Tuesday morning, reflecting that Trump did indeed say "thinking," reports NBC News.

final_summary:
Windi C. Thomas, a 44-year-old resident of Erie, has pleaded guilty to third-degree murder in connection with the fatal attack on her boyfriend, 44-year-old Keeno Butler, at their shared east Erie residence in March. As part of her plea agreement with prosecutors, Thomas agreed to a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in state prison. This reduced sentence cuts two to four years from the maximum sentence of 20 to 40 years for third-degree murder. 

The incident occurred on March 18 when Thomas allegedly attacked Butler, causing blunt force trauma to his neck and thoracic area, leading to his death. According to investigators, Thomas claimed that she used a folding knife to cut Butler's hand and struck him in the head with a table leg during a confrontation. Additionally, she stated that she placed her body on top of Butler, resulting in her weight compressing his head, exacerbating the injuries he sustained.

Erie County Judge William R. Cunningham accepted Thomas' plea on Monday, allowing Assistant Erie County District Attorney Jeremy Lightner to drop charges of aggravated assault, reckless endangerment, and possession of the instrument of a crime. Thomas' court-appointed lawyer, Mark Del Duca, described the outcome as a "good result" considering the circumstances of the case, stating that it was a fair compromise for both parties involved.

However, the victim's sister, Sandra Butler, expressed disappointment with the proposed sentence, advocating for a harsher punishment. She believes that Thomas should serve more time, citing that her brother's life was invaluable. Butler's family continues to struggle with the loss, with Sandra revealing that her mother still calls out for her deceased brother every night.

Thomas will remain incarcerated in the Erie County Prison without bond until her scheduled sentencing hearing on December 21.
