reference: – A 300-pound woman in Pennsylvania has pleaded guilty to murdering her boyfriend by stabbing him, hitting in the head with a table leg, and finally crushing the 120-pound man with her own weight. Windi Thomas, 44, pleaded guilty to third-degree murder in the March 18 death of Keeno Butler in exchange for a recommended sentence of 18 to 36 years in state prison, the Erie Times-News reports. Police say Thomas admitted crushing Butler after a fight at their Erie residence. She told them she positioned herself so that some of her weight would be on his head. The death was ruled a homicide "caused by respiratory insufficiency secondary to blunt force trauma to the neck and thoracic compression, exacerbated by blunt force trauma to the head," court papers state. Sandra Butler, the victim's sister, says Thomas should have gotten life. "My mother, she calls for him every night," she says. Thomas, who had been scheduled to go on trial next week, will be sentenced Dec. 21, the AP reports. (A Fitbit led to this man's arrest after he claimed an obese intruder killed his wife.)

final_summary:
Comedy Central confirmed on Tuesday that Jon Stewart, the host of "The Daily Show," will be leaving the anchor's chair after nearly 17 years. However, the exact date of his departure remains uncertain, with Stewart indicating it may occur in September, December, or July, depending on the final details being worked out. Stewart, 52, announced his decision to depart during the show's taping earlier that day, stating that he felt it was time for someone else to have the opportunity he had.

Stewart began hosting "The Daily Show" in January 1999, succeeding Craig Kilborn, and under his leadership, the show has won 20 prime-time Emmys and become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans. During his tenure, Stewart emerged as a leading political satirist and one of the toughest interviewers in the news media, earning him widespread recognition and acclaim. Throughout his career, Stewart has directed several films, including "Rosewater," a drama about the detention of Iranian-Canadian journalist Maziar Bahari during Iran's 2009 election protests. He has also appeared in various other projects, such as "The Larry Sanders Show" and "Crossfire," where he famously sparred with host Tucker Carlson on the alleged malign impact of cable news on American political discourse.

As a result of Stewart's departure, Comedy Central released a statement praising his contributions to the network, citing his unique voice and vision, which have made "The Daily Show" an unparalleled platform for political comedy. Michele Ganeless, the president of Comedy Central, stated that Stewart has been at the heart of the network, championing and nurturing the best talent in the industry, and that the show will endure for years to come. Specifically, Ganeless noted that Stewart will remain at the helm of "The Daily Show" until later this year, as confirmed by Comedy Central's official statement on Twitter.

Stewart's exit marks the latest change in the Comedy Central late-night lineup, following the departure of Stephen Colbert, who hosted "The Colbert Report" before taking over for David Letterman on CBS. Another notable departure was John Oliver, who left to host the breakout hit "Last Week Tonight" on HBO. Despite these changes, Comedy Central remains confident in the show's enduring legacy, with Ganeless emphasizing that "The Daily Show" will continue to thrive beyond Stewart's departure.

In a heartfelt address to his audience, Stewart expressed gratitude for the experience and acknowledged that the show didn't deserve a restless host. He praised the talented individuals he has worked with, describing them as "creative, collaborative, and kind." Stewart's emotional farewell marked the end of an era for "The Daily Show," but his impact on American politics and satire will undoubtedly be remembered for years to come. Getty Images captured the moment when Stewart announced his departure to his audience, who were visibly moved by the news. Every day, Stewart has brought his unique perspective and wit to the show, making it a staple of American television. Last year, Stewart took a break from the show to direct his film "Rosewater," which premiered to critical acclaim. In a statement, Michele Ganeless, the president of Comedy Central, praised Stewart's dedication to the network, saying that he will always be a part of the Comedy Central family. Stewart's departure comes as no surprise, given his comments to NPR's Terry Gross in November, where he hinted at the possibility of leaving the show.

In a heartfelt address to his audience, Stewart expressed gratitude for the experience and acknowledged that the show didn't deserve a restless host. He praised the talented individuals he has worked with, describing them as "creative, collaborative, and kind." Stewart's emotional farewell marked the end of an era for "The Daily Show," but his impact on American politics and satire will undoubtedly be remembered for years to come. Getty Images captured the moment when Stewart announced his departure to his audience, who were visibly moved by the news. Every day, Stewart has brought his unique perspective and wit to the show, making it a staple of American television. Last year, Stewart took a break from the show to direct his film "Rosewater," which premiered to critical acclaim. In a statement, Michele Ganeless, the president of Comedy Central, praised Stewart's dedication to the network, saying that he will always be a part of the Comedy Central family. Stewart's departure comes as no surprise, given his comments to NPR's Terry Gross in November, where he hinted at the possibility of leaving the show.

reference: – Jon Stewart is retiring from the Daily Show later this year. He reportedly revealed the big news during the taping of tonight's episode, reports the AV Club. In a statement to Politico, a Comedy Central exec confirms that Stewart will step down at a still-unspecified date after more than 15 years. "Through his unique voice and vision, the Daily Show has become a cultural touchstone for millions of fans and an unparalleled platform for political comedy that will endure for years to come," says network chief Michele Ganeless, which seems to suggest that the show will go on with a new host. This is one of those rare cases when a network's official statement is right in line with what most others are writing: "To a younger generation of viewers who stopped watching the news, his late-night 'fake' news show—which skewered politicians and the media—made him a trusted messenger to millions," writes Emily Yahr at the Washington Post. Business Insider: The show is a "cultural force." NPR: A "hugely influential figure in American politics." New York Times: A "nightly home for sharp-edged political satire and up-to-the-moment commentary on the news." (Stewart, of course, is a movie director now, too.)

final_summary:
On February 14, 2011, Simon Maloy reported on the backlash faced by conservative provocateur James O'Keefe for a proposed prank targeting CNN reporter Abbie Boudreau. According to O'Keefe, the plan involved luring Boudreau onto a boat, where he would pretend to seduce her in a satirical attempt to counter her reporting methods. However, the details of the plan, as outlined in a document obtained by CNN, raised concerns about the potential for coercion and exploitation.

O'Keefe claimed that the document was a draft and not intended to be carried out as written, citing that he often receives numerous ideas and schemes, some of which he may approve of in principle but not in their entirety. He stated that he liked the basic concept of turning the tables on Boudreau but was repulsed by the explicit language and symbolism used in the document, which was drafted by his associate Ben Wetmore, someone he referred to as his mentor.

However, evidence suggests that O'Keefe's account may be disputed. His accomplice, Izzy Santa, believed that the plan involved creeping Boudreau out, implying that the intention was not to engage in a harmless satirical stunt but rather to create an uncomfortable situation. Furthermore, the document described a scenario involving a boat with strawberries and champagne, which seems inconsistent with O'Keefe's claim that the plan was to be conducted in a conservative setting.

Meanwhile, conservative media outlets, including Andrew Breitbart's BigGovernment.com, had been targeting Michelle Obama with sexist attacks and mocking cartoons, with one cartoon depicting her demanding "lard." Rush Limbaugh, a prominent conservative figure, had previously targeted Michelle Obama with sexist remarks, labeling her "Michelle, My Butt."

In response to these criticisms, O'Keefe defended himself, accusing the liberal media of being biased and claiming that his actions were misunderstood. He pointed to his past successes, including the downfall of ACORN, a community organization that had faced criticism for its handling of government funds. However, his defense was met with skepticism, particularly given his association with Wetmore, who had a history of creating provocative content.

This controversy unfolded amidst a broader debate about government intervention in American life, with the Obama administration's efforts to address obesity and other health issues facing opposition from conservative critics.

reference: – Andrew Breitbart, fresh off the news of Shirley Sherrod's lawsuit against him, has gotten himself embroiled in another controversy. His Big Government website lashed out at Michelle Obama's anti-obesity campaign ... by basically calling her fat. A cartoon strip on the website shows an overweight Obama eating a huge pile of hamburgers while talking to her big-eared husband about the campaign, Salon reports. The punch line—"Shut up and pass the bacon!"—was originally "Shut up and pass the lard!" Media Matters notes. Click to see the cartoon.

final_summary:
Hurricane Isaac made landfall in Louisiana on August 29, 2012, causing widespread destruction and chaos along the Gulf Coast. The storm brought powerful winds of up to 80 mph and heavy rainfall, resulting in flooding and power outages across the region. Over 817,000 customers were left without power, with the majority of outages occurring in Louisiana. 

In New Orleans, Mayor Mitch Landrieu imposed a dusk-to-dawn curfew, warning residents to stay indoors due to hazardous conditions. The city's levees, strengthened after Hurricane Katrina in 2005, held up relatively well, but surrounding areas experienced severe flooding. In Plaquemines Parish, a rural levee was overtopped, causing water to pour into the area and stranding dozens of residents in attics and on rooftops. Parish President Billy Nungesser estimated that as many as 800 homes may have suffered significant water damage in the parish alone.

Rescue efforts were underway, with the National Guard launching a large-scale rescue operation to reach stranded residents. However, the slow-moving storm posed challenges for rescue teams, with Isaac's winds and rain continuing to hamper efforts. The National Weather Service issued a tornado warning for southern Mississippi, including the cities of Long Beach and Gulfport, as the storm crawled inland. Governor Bobby Jindal activated the National Guard to assist with rescue efforts and declared a state of emergency.

The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) deployed four UH-60 Blackhawk helicopters and two SH-60 Seahawk helicopters to aid in search and rescue operations. The National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the storm continued to move inland, the National Hurricane Center (NHC) reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph.

The storm's slow pace allowed it to dump heavy rainfall on the region, causing flash flooding and landslides. The NHC warned of isolated tornadoes and rough surf along the coast, advising residents to exercise caution. In Mississippi, the storm caused significant disruptions, with over 4,000 homes without power and 2,132 evacuees housed in 31 shelters across the state. The city of Pensacola, Florida, reported that it was back to normal operations, with city hall, libraries, and garbage collection functioning as usual.

As the storm continued to wreak havoc, officials scrambled to respond to the crisis. The Louisiana Attorney General reminded sex offenders that evacuating due to the storm did not exempt them from notifying law enforcement of their whereabouts. The National Weather Service issued a flash flood warning for several Louisiana cities, including New Orleans, and a tornado warning for Hancock and Harrison counties in Mississippi. The storm's slow pace and large size worsened its effects, making it seem like a stronger storm than its Category 1 classification suggested.

The NHC warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the storm continued to move inland, the NHC reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph. In the aftermath of the storm, officials began assessing the damage and coordinating relief efforts. The National Guard continued to assist with rescue operations, and utility companies worked to restore power to affected areas.

The storm's impact was felt across the region, with thousands of residents displaced and countless homes damaged or destroyed. CNN affiliate WWL reported that the parish's levees were designed to withstand a storm surge of up to 12 feet, but Isaac's storm surge exceeded that amount, causing significant damage. In Baton Rouge, CNN affiliate WBRZ reported that the city's residents were preparing for the worst, with many stocking up on supplies and boarding up windows.

On Tuesday, CNN affiliate WGNO reported that the National Weather Service had issued a tornado warning for several parishes in Louisiana, including St. Tammany, Tangipahoa, and Washington. The storm's slow pace and large size made it difficult to predict exactly where the storm would make landfall, but officials were bracing for the worst. In Alabama, CNN affiliate WALA reported that the state's residents were preparing for the storm, with many evacuating to higher ground.

As the storm continued to move inland, the National Hurricane Center warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the sun rose on August 29, 2012, the full extent of the devastation became clear, leaving a trail of destruction in its wake. The storm's impact was felt across the region, with thousands of residents displaced and countless homes damaged or destroyed. In the aftermath of the storm, officials began assessing the damage and coordinating relief efforts. The National Guard continued to assist with rescue operations, and utility companies worked to restore power to affected areas.

history.draft:
Hurricane Isaac made landfall in Louisiana on August 29, 2012, causing widespread destruction and chaos along the Gulf Coast. The storm brought powerful winds of up to 80 mph and heavy rainfall, resulting in flooding and power outages across the region. Over 817,000 customers were left without power, with the majority of outages occurring in Louisiana. 

In New Orleans, Mayor Mitch Landrieu imposed a dusk-to-dawn curfew, warning residents to stay indoors due to hazardous conditions. The city's levees, strengthened after Hurricane Katrina in 2005, held up relatively well, but surrounding areas experienced severe flooding. In Plaquemines Parish, a rural levee was overtopped, causing water to pour into the area and stranding dozens of residents in attics and on rooftops. Parish President Billy Nungesser estimated that as many as 800 homes may have suffered significant water damage in the parish alone.

Rescue efforts were underway, with the National Guard launching a large-scale rescue operation to reach stranded residents. However, the slow-moving storm posed challenges for rescue teams, with Isaac's winds and rain continuing to hamper efforts. The National Weather Service issued a tornado warning for southern Mississippi, including the cities of Long Beach and Gulfport, as the storm crawled inland. Governor Bobby Jindal activated the National Guard to assist with rescue efforts and declared a state of emergency.

The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) deployed four UH-60 Blackhawk helicopters and two SH-60 Seahawk helicopters to aid in search and rescue operations. The National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the storm continued to move inland, the National Hurricane Center (NHC) reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph.

The storm's slow pace allowed it to dump heavy rainfall on the region, causing flash flooding and landslides. The NHC warned of isolated tornadoes and rough surf along the coast, advising residents to exercise caution. In Mississippi, the storm caused significant disruptions, with over 4,000 homes without power and 2,132 evacuees housed in 31 shelters across the state. The city of Pensacola, Florida, reported that it was back to normal operations, with city hall, libraries, and garbage collection functioning as usual.

As the storm continued to wreak havoc, officials scrambled to respond to the crisis. The Louisiana Attorney General reminded sex offenders that evacuating due to the storm did not exempt them from notifying law enforcement of their whereabouts. The National Weather Service issued a flash flood warning for several Louisiana cities, including New Orleans, and a tornado warning for Hancock and Harrison counties in Mississippi. The storm's slow pace and large size worsened its effects, making it seem like a stronger storm than its Category 1 classification suggested.

The NHC warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the storm continued to move inland, the NHC reported that Isaac's maximum sustained winds had decreased to 60 mph. In the aftermath of the storm, officials began assessing the damage and coordinating relief efforts. The National Guard continued to assist with rescue operations, and utility companies worked to restore power to affected areas.

The storm's impact was felt across the region, with thousands of residents displaced and countless homes damaged or destroyed. CNN affiliate WWL reported that the parish's levees were designed to withstand a storm surge of up to 12 feet, but Isaac's storm surge exceeded that amount, causing significant damage. In Baton Rouge, CNN affiliate WBRZ reported that the city's residents were preparing for the worst, with many stocking up on supplies and boarding up windows.

On Tuesday, CNN affiliate WGNO reported that the National Weather Service had issued a tornado warning for several parishes in Louisiana, including St. Tammany, Tangipahoa, and Washington. The storm's slow pace and large size made it difficult to predict exactly where the storm would make landfall, but officials were bracing for the worst. In Alabama, CNN affiliate WALA reported that the state's residents were preparing for the storm, with many evacuating to higher ground.

As the storm continued to move inland, the National Hurricane Center warned of potentially catastrophic flooding, with totals reaching up to 20 inches in some areas. As the sun rose on August 29, 2012, the full extent of the devastation became clear, leaving a trail of destruction in its wake. The storm's impact was felt across the region, with thousands of residents displaced and countless homes damaged or destroyed. In the aftermath of the storm, officials began assessing the damage and coordinating relief efforts. The National Guard continued to assist with rescue operations, and utility companies worked to restore power to affected areas.

reference: – Today is the 7th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, and for residents of Plaquemines Parish, it's deja vu all over again, according to the parish president. Billy Nungesser tells NPR that the parish, located some 95 miles from New Orleans, has already seen damage that rivals, and could even surpass, that heaped upon it by Katrina. With the levee overrun, Nungesser says parts of the parish that had escaped unscathed in previous hurricanes now sit under five feet of water. "I don't know who's calling this a Category 1, but this is no Category 1," Nungesser said. "My house has more damage than it did during Katrina." More on the situation in Plaquemines, and beyond: The parish's levees range from 8.5 feet to 12 feet in height. The Army Corps of Engineers estimates the storm surge hit 12.5 feet, but have yet to confirm a breach. This implies the levees, which are not part of the federal levee system, failed, reports the Times-Picayune. While the director of Plaquemines Parish's emergency preparedness says winds may have hit 110mph, CNN reports that Isaac has weakened a bit since, with maximum sustained winds of 75mph (the hurricane threshold is 74mph). As of about 10:30am ET, Isaac's center was located about 50 miles south-southwest of New Orleans. The AP reports that 500,000 are without power in the areas around New Orleans. In Southern Mississippi, a tornado warning has been issued.

final_summary:
According to Apple's recent announcement, the highly anticipated white iPhone 4 will become available starting tomorrow. As stated by Philip Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of Worldwide Product Marketing, "The white iPhone 4 has finally arrived and it's beautiful." The device will be sold in 16- and 32-gigabyte versions for $199 and $299, respectively, with a two-year wireless contract through Apple, Verizon, AT&T, and other authorized resellers. This marks the culmination of months of anticipation, with initial rumors suggesting a possible launch as early as this spring, specifically by the end of April, with some reports pointing towards a launch date of April 27.

As Apple addresses concerns over the collection and storage of location data on iOS devices, the company clarifies that it does not collect or store the location of customers' iPhones, nor has it ever done so. However, Apple acknowledges that users may have been misinformed due to a lack of understanding about how the device provides fast and accurate location information. The company identifies several bugs that contributed to the confusion, including the storage of a crowd-sourced database of Wi-Fi hotspots and cell towers surrounding the device's location, which can be hundreds of miles away. Apple emphasizes that it cannot identify the source of this data, and that the stored cache is not the actual location of the device, but rather a compilation of locations of nearby Wi-Fi hotspots and cell towers.

To address these concerns, the company plans to release a free iOS software update within the next few weeks that will reduce the size of the database, encrypt the data, and delete it when Location Services are turned off by the user. Additionally, another bug that caused the iPhone to continue updating its location data even after Location Services were disabled will be fixed. These measures follow a class-action lawsuit filed against Apple, alleging invasion of privacy and computer fraud, as well as investigations by French, German, Italian, and South Korean regulators into the alleged location collection feature on Apple devices. Apple's response to these concerns includes a comprehensive Q&A session addressing the issue, which can be found online.

These developments come amidst a class-action lawsuit filed against Apple, alleging invasion of privacy and computer fraud, as well as investigations by French, German, Italian, and South Korean regulators into the alleged location collection feature on Apple devices. reference: – Apple finally responded to the accusations that it was tracking iPhone users’ locations today, saying it “has never done so and has no plans to ever do so.” In a statement, which you can read on TechCrunch, the company explained that it was actually keeping a log not of the phone’s location, but of the location of WiFi hotspots and cell towers, “some of which may be located more than one hundred miles away from your iPhone.” The data is used to more quickly triangulate users’ position when they use location-based services, and is sent to Apple anonymously. But the company did admit to some “bugs” in the software—like that it keeps collecting data even after you’ve turned Location Services off, and that it stored years of data, instead of the week's worth it needs—and promised an iOS update to fix them. And, just to add some positive news to the cycle, it also announced that the rumored white iPhone will be available tomorrow, Time reports.

final_summary:
On June 8, Anthony Bourdain, the renowned American chef, author, and television host, was found dead in his hotel room in Kaysersberg, France, where he had been working on an upcoming episode of his CNN series "Parts Unknown" with his close friend, French-born chef Eric Ripert. Bourdain, 61, had been open about his struggles with substance abuse in the past, including a history of heroin and cocaine addiction, but had maintained that he had remained sober since entering rehab in the early 1990s. However, a recent toxicology report revealed that at the time of his death, Bourdain had no narcotics in his system, although a small amount of a non-narcotic medicine was detected in a therapeutic dose, along with a trace of alcohol.

Bourdain's death was confirmed as a suicide by hanging, with French authorities stating that there was no evidence of foul play or violence involved. This conclusion was further supported by a medical examination conducted by French prosecutor Christian de Rocquigny de Fayel, who stated that nothing suggested the involvement of a third person. The investigation also found that Bourdain had been in good health prior to his death, with no underlying medical conditions that could have contributed to his decision to take his own life.

In the aftermath of his death, tributes poured in from around the world, with friends, colleagues, and fans expressing their shock and sadness at the loss of the charismatic and adventurous chef. Among those paying tribute was his girlfriend, Asia Argento, an Italian actress who had been dating Bourdain for over a year. Argento, 42, shared a heartfelt message on Instagram, saying that Bourdain had given all of himself in everything he did and that his bravery and generosity had inspired countless people.

Bourdain's relationship with Argento had been well-documented, particularly in the wake of her high-profile allegations against film producer Harvey Weinstein, whom she accused of rape. Bourdain had been a vocal supporter of Argento, praising her courage and fearlessness in speaking out against Weinstein and others in Hollywood who had enabled his behavior. Just before his death, Bourdain had expressed his pride in Argento's speech at the Cannes Film Festival, calling it an "incredibly powerful moment."

As news of Bourdain's death spread, fans and fellow celebrities took to social media to pay their respects, sharing memories and stories of the chef's adventures and exploits. In Paris, where Bourdain had spent much of his career, fans gathered outside the restaurant where he had worked, leaving flowers and messages of condolence. CNN, where Bourdain had hosted several popular shows, released a statement mourning the loss of its beloved colleague and friend. As the news sank in, fans and admirers began to reflect on the impact that Bourdain had had on their lives, from his irreverent travel shows to his bestselling books and cookbooks.

Just two weeks after Bourdain's death, on June 22, Argento posted a poignant message on Instagram, sharing a photo of herself and Bourdain smiling together in front of a serene blue body of water. "Two weeks without you," she wrote, her words a testament to the enduring bond between the couple and the profound impact that Bourdain's death had left on those who loved him.

history.draft:
On June 8, Anthony Bourdain, the renowned American chef, author, and television host, was found dead in his hotel room in Kaysersberg, France, where he had been working on an upcoming episode of his CNN series "Parts Unknown" with his close friend, French-born chef Eric Ripert. Bourdain, 61, had been open about his struggles with substance abuse in the past, including a history of heroin and cocaine addiction, but had maintained that he had remained sober since entering rehab in the early 1990s. However, a recent toxicology report revealed that at the time of his death, Bourdain had no narcotics in his system, although a small amount of a non-narcotic medicine was detected in a therapeutic dose, along with a trace of alcohol.

Bourdain's death was confirmed as a suicide by hanging, with French authorities stating that there was no evidence of foul play or violence involved. This conclusion was further supported by a medical examination. The investigation also found that Bourdain had been in good health prior to his death, with no underlying medical conditions that could have contributed to his decision to take his own life.

In the aftermath of his death, tributes poured in from around the world, with friends, colleagues, and fans expressing their shock and sadness at the loss of the charismatic and adventurous chef. Among those paying tribute was his girlfriend, Asia Argento, an Italian actress who had been dating Bourdain for over a year. Argento, 42, shared a heartfelt message on Instagram, saying that Bourdain had given all of himself in everything he did and that his bravery and generosity had inspired countless people.

Bourdain's relationship with Argento had been well-documented, particularly in the wake of her high-profile allegations against film producer Harvey Weinstein, whom she accused of rape. Bourdain had been a vocal supporter of Argento, praising her courage and fearlessness in speaking out against Weinstein and others in Hollywood who had enabled his behavior. Just before his death, Bourdain had expressed his pride in Argento's speech at the Cannes Film Festival, calling it an "incredibly powerful moment."

As news of Bourdain's death spread, fans and fellow celebrities took to social media to pay their respects, sharing memories and stories of the chef's adventures and exploits. In Paris, where Bourdain had spent much of his career, fans gathered outside the restaurant where he had worked, leaving flowers and messages of condolence. CNN, where Bourdain had hosted several popular shows, released a statement mourning the loss of its beloved colleague and friend. As the news sank in, fans and admirers began to reflect on the impact that Bourdain had had on their lives, from his irreverent travel shows to his bestselling books and cookbooks.

Just two weeks later, on June 22, Argento posted a poignant message on Instagram, sharing a photo of herself and Bourdain smiling together in front of a serene blue body of water. "Two weeks without you," she wrote, her words a testament to the enduring bond between the couple and the profound impact that Bourdain's death had left on those who loved him.

(Rose McGowan wants people to stop blaming Argento for his suicide.)

final_summary:
In the digital age, a unique canine asset has emerged as a crucial tool in the fight against cybercrime – a 2-year-old black Labrador named Bear. Trained to sniff out electronic data devices, Bear is one of only five dogs in the nation with this specialized skillset, and his impressive track record includes playing a pivotal role in the high-profile arrest of former Subway pitchman Jared Fogle on child-porn charges. Fogle, who pleaded guilty to possessing X-rated images of minors and paying to have sex with teenage girls, was investigated by authorities in Indiana several weeks prior to his guilty plea.

During the search of Fogle's residence, Bear's keen sense of smell proved invaluable, detecting a thumb drive that human investigators had missed. This remarkable feat showcased the effectiveness of Bear's training, which involves getting him accustomed to the scent of electronic devices and rewarding him with treats when he indicates their presence. Bear's handler, Todd Jordan, has been working with the talented canine for a year and has witnessed firsthand its exceptional abilities.

Jordan demonstrated Bear's skills by hiding various electronic devices in a room and instructing him to "seek" them out; within mere minutes, Bear located a memory card, a flash drive, and an iPad. This impressive display of Bear's capabilities has earned him recognition from prosecutors, including Steven DeBrota, who led the Fogle case and praised Bear as "a key part of the team." Bear's contributions extend beyond the Fogle case, having participated in four other investigations, including the recent arrest of Olympics gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp.

His services will soon be utilized by the Seattle Police Department, where he will aid in investigating internet crimes. As Bear prepares to relocate to Seattle, his current owner, Todd Jordan, expressed mixed emotions, acknowledging that while it will be difficult to part with the dog, he takes pride in knowing that Bear's work will continue to make a significant impact. Jordan's expertise extends beyond Bear, as he trains additional canines to join the ranks of electronic-sniffing dogs. After the Fogle raid, Seattle PD reached out to Jordan, and Bear is now headed west. Bear is moving to Seattle this month, marking the fifth case he has worked on. Jordan is currently training two more dogs to sniff out electronics.

reference: – There are only five dogs in America trained to sniff out electronic devices, and one of them played a major role in Jared Fogle's downfall. Bear, a 2-year-old black Labrador, was able to sniff out a thumb drive that human investigators missed during a search of the former Subway spokesman's home last month, reports NBC News, which describes Bear as a "porn-sniffing dog." He can detect tiny memory cards easily and was trained with methods associated with "any other K-9 training, like with the narcotics or explosives or anything," his trainer tells WISH-TV. "You get the dog used to the odor and reward them as they indicate on it." The lead prosecutor on the Fogle case tells NBC that Bear is a "key part of the team," though he doubted the pooch's abilities at first. "I thought I was being punked, but it does work," he says. The evidence, along with that gathered by an FBI informant, helped secure Fogle's guilty plea on child-porn and underage sex charges. Bear has been a busy dog: NBC reports that he also played a role in the arrest of Olympic gymnastics coach Marvin Sharp. Bear's trainer, a deputy fire chief, sells dogs to law enforcement agencies when their training is completed, and Bear will soon be on his way to Seattle, where the police department contacted the trainer after hearing about the Fogle case. (Fogle's charitable foundation appears to have been a sham.)

final_summary:
A Northern California couple from Roseville is grieving the loss of their 11-year-old daughter, Paige Warner, who passed away after suffering a severe allergic reaction during a family Thanksgiving trip to Hawaii. Paige, who had pre-existing food allergies and sensitivities to environmental conditions, had been excitedly looking forward to visiting the islands with her family, including her parents, Karin Carpenter and Hank, and her younger sister, Violet.

On Thanksgiving Day, the family arrived in Honolulu and spent the evening at a friend's home in Hawaii Kai for a holiday meal. Despite being unable to eat much of the food due to her allergies, Paige seemed fine initially, enjoying time with her new friends and getting into the pool with Violet. However, shortly after diving into the water, Paige began experiencing severe symptoms, complaining of a burning sensation in her nose and difficulty breathing. Her mother, Karin Carpenter, quickly realized something was seriously wrong and called 911.

An EMT who was a guest at the gathering administered Benadryl to Paige, but she struggled to take it. The ambulance arrived within seven minutes, and paramedics administered epinephrine and Benadryl en route to Kapiolani Children's Hospital. Despite efforts to revive her, Paige suffered cardiac arrest and required over 20 minutes of CPR before her heart was restarted. She was placed on life support and remained in a coma, undergoing various medical treatments, including a cooling blanket to reduce her body temperature.

Paige's family remained by her side, praying, playing music, and reading messages from friends to her. Her biological father, Josh, flew in to visit her, although he was unable to stay until the end due to feelings of guilt for neglecting his responsibilities towards Paige in the past. An MRI on December 1 revealed significant damage to Paige's brain, with the exception of the brain stem and the area responsible for memory. Further testing confirmed that Paige's brain had suffered irreparable damage, leading to her passing at 9:25 a.m. on December 4.

A GoFundMe page was set up to support the family during this difficult time, raising funds to help them cope with the loss of their beloved daughter. As the family mourns Paige's loss, her younger sister Violet has found solace in helping her parents cope, insisting on decorating the house for Christmas, a tradition Paige loved dearly.

history.draft:
A Northern California couple is grieving the loss of their 11-year-old daughter, Paige Warner, who passed away after suffering a severe allergic reaction during a family Thanksgiving trip to Hawaii. Paige, who had pre-existing food allergies and sensitivities to environmental conditions, had been excitedly looking forward to visiting the islands with her family, including her parents, Karin Carpenter and Hank, and her younger sister, Violet. 

On Thanksgiving Day, the family arrived in Honolulu and spent the evening at a friend's home in Hawaii Kai for a holiday meal. Despite being unable to eat much of the food due to her allergies, Paige seemed fine initially, enjoying time with her new friends and getting into the pool with Violet. However, shortly after diving into the water, Paige began experiencing severe symptoms, complaining of a burning sensation in her nose and difficulty breathing. Her mother, Karin Carpenter, quickly realized something was seriously wrong and called 911.

An EMT who was a guest at the gathering administered Benadryl to Paige, but she struggled to take it. The ambulance arrived within seven minutes, and paramedics administered epinephrine and Benadryl en route to Kapiolani Children's Hospital. Despite efforts to revive her, Paige suffered cardiac arrest and required over 20 minutes of CPR before her heart was restarted. She was placed on life support and remained in a coma, undergoing various medical treatments, including a cooling blanket to reduce her body temperature.

KTXL reported that Paige's family remained by her side, praying, playing music, and reading messages from friends to her. Her biological father, Josh, flew in to visit her, although he was unable to stay until the end due to feelings of guilt for neglecting his responsibilities towards Paige in the past. A report from KTLA sister station KTXL stated that an MRI on December 1 revealed significant damage to Paige's brain, with the exception of the brain stem and the area responsible for memory. Unfortunately, further testing confirmed that Paige's brain had suffered irreparable damage, leading to her passing at 9:25 a.m. on December 4.

In response to the tragedy, a GoFundMe page raised over $28,000 to support the family during this difficult time. As the family mourns Paige's loss, her younger sister Violet has found solace in helping her parents cope, insisting on decorating the house for Christmas, a tradition Paige loved dearly.

history.draft:
A Northern California couple from Roseville is grieving the loss of their 11-year-old daughter, Paige Warner, who passed away after suffering a severe allergic reaction during a family Thanksgiving trip to Hawaii. Paige, who had pre-existing food allergies and sensitivities to environmental conditions, had been excitedly looking forward to visiting the islands with her family, including her parents, Karin Carpenter and Hank, and her younger sister, Violet.

On Thanksgiving Day, the family arrived in Honolulu and spent the evening at a friend's home in Hawaii Kai for a holiday meal. Despite being unable to eat much of the food due to her allergies, Paige seemed fine initially, enjoying time with her new friends and getting into the pool with Violet. However, shortly after diving into the water, Paige began experiencing severe symptoms, complaining of a burning sensation in her nose and difficulty breathing. Her mother, Karin Carpenter, quickly realized something was seriously wrong and called 911.

An EMT who was a guest at the gathering administered Benadryl to Paige, but she struggled to take it. The ambulance arrived within seven minutes, and paramedics administered epinephrine and Benadryl en route to Kapiolani Children's Hospital. Despite efforts to revive her, Paige suffered cardiac arrest and required over 20 minutes of CPR before her heart was restarted. She was placed on life support and remained in a coma, undergoing various medical treatments, including a cooling blanket to reduce her body temperature.

Paige's family remained by her side, praying, playing music, and reading messages from friends to her. Her biological father, Josh, flew in to visit her, although he was unable to stay until the end due to feelings of guilt for neglecting his responsibilities towards Paige in the past. An MRI on December 1 revealed significant damage to Paige's brain, with the exception of the brain stem and the area responsible for memory. Further testing confirmed that Paige's brain had suffered irreparable damage, leading to her passing at 9:25 a.m. on December 4.

A GoFundMe page was set up to support the family during this difficult time, raising funds to help them cope with the loss of their beloved daughter. As the family mourns Paige's loss, her younger sister Violet has found solace in helping her parents cope, insisting on decorating the house for Christmas, a tradition Paige loved dearly.

reference: – On Thanksgiving Day, Paige Warner and her family flew to Honolulu for the 11-year-old's first trip to Hawaii. She "was so excited," recounts mom Karin Carpenter, who explains that after a holiday dinner of white rice and soy sauce—Paige had "extensive" food allergies—her daughter got into the pool with her sister. Fewer than 15 minutes later, she was out of the pool, complaining of a stinging nose. Her condition quickly snowballed into something much more serious, involving difficulty breathing, and she was taken to the ER. On Dec. 4, after a week in a coma, the girl from Roseville, Calif., was taken off life support; it's still unclear what triggered the reaction, reports KTLA. As Carpenter writes on a GoFundMe page, she speculates about possible contributing factors: the plane, entering an environment full of allergens that were new to her, the area's large feral cat population, pesticide runoff in the pool from an adjacent golf course, or a bronchial spasm caused by the pool's temperature. The family is "devastated," but sister Violet is bringing healing, insisting that the family hang its Christmas decorations because Paige "loves Christmas." The GoFundMe campaign has raised more than $30,000 to date. The most heart-tugging line on the page, from Carpenter's description of the events immediately after Paige got out of the pool: "She looked into my eyes and screamed, and said, 'Mom, I'm dying. I know I am because I can see it in your eyes.'" (A teen's allergic reaction recently caused her to "burn from the inside out.")

final_summary:
In a shocking case of child abuse, Jorge Barahona, a father of four adopted children, including 10-year-old twins Victor and Nubia, was found alongside his son in a red pickup truck on Interstate 95 in West Palm Beach, Florida. Nubia's lifeless body was covered in acid and wrapped in a plastic bag in the back of the vehicle. 

According to a probable-cause affidavit filed by the West Palm Beach Police Department, a roadside assistance ranger with the Florida Department of Transportation discovered the truck parked on the side of I-95 on Monday, with Victor inside, suffering from respiratory distress and soaked in an unknown chemical. Barahona was found on the ground outside the truck, and a hazardous materials crew was called in to clean up the scene.

Victor, who was also severely injured, was rushed to the hospital with severe burns to his abdomen, upper thighs, and buttocks, as well as prior injuries including a fractured clavicle, a fractured arm, scarring to his buttocks and lower abdomen, and ligature marks on both wrists. A probable-cause affidavit revealed that Barahona had initially claimed he was distraught over Nubia's death and had given Victor a handful of sleeping pills, telling him to take them so he could sleep. However, police say his story doesn't add up, as Victor was covered in a different chemical, whose composition had yet to be determined, and there was no gasoline on his body.

Mark Riordan, a spokesperson for the Florida Department of Children and Families, stated that the agency had previously investigated the couple on three separate occasions, although the cases were closed without finding any evidence of abuse or neglect. A recent investigation was launched after a teacher reported concerns about the twins being bound by their hands and feet and made to stand in the bathtub. 

Miami's Child Protection Team, led by Dr. Walter Lambert, reported that Victor was receiving intensive treatment for his injuries, which included being placed in a hyperbaric chamber due to his severe burns. The team also noted that Victor's kidneys may be failing, and he was experiencing difficulty urinating.

Alessandra, a 6-year-old relative who had stayed with the Barahonas after school, had previously witnessed the twins being locked in a bathroom with a shower curtain taped to the walls and being forced to stand in garbage bins. She had confided in a therapist, prompting a short-lived child welfare investigation. The case highlights the need for vigilance in protecting vulnerable children from abuse and ensuring that those responsible are held accountable for their actions.

history.draft:
In a shocking turn of events, a disturbing case of child abuse came to light in West Palm Beach, Florida, leaving a trail of horror and tragedy in its wake. Jorge Barahona, a 53-year-old father of four adopted children, including 10-year-old twins Victor and Nubia, was found alongside his son in a red pickup truck on Interstate 95, with Nubia's lifeless body covered in acid and wrapped in a plastic bag in the back of the vehicle.

According to eyewitnesses, Barahona had attempted to take his own life by pouring gasoline over his head, but changed his mind when he realized his son, Victor, was beside him. Victor, who was also severely injured, was rushed to the hospital with severe burns to his abdomen, upper thighs, and buttocks, as well as prior injuries including a fractured clavicle, a fractured arm, scarring to his buttocks and lower abdomen, and ligature marks on both wrists.

A probable-cause affidavit filed by the West Palm Beach Police Department revealed that Barahona had initially claimed he was distraught over Nubia's death and had given Victor a handful of sleeping pills, telling him to take them so he could sleep. However, police say his story doesn't add up, as Victor was covered in a different chemical, whose composition had yet to be determined, and there was no gasoline on his body.

The investigation into the case began earlier this month when a Florida Department of Transportation employee discovered the truck parked on the side of I-95, with Victor inside, suffering from respiratory distress and soaked in an unknown chemical. Barahona was found on the ground outside the truck, and a hazardous materials crew was called in to clean up the scene.

As the investigation unfolded, it emerged that Barahona and his wife, Carmen, had a history of allegations of child abuse. The Florida Department of Children and Families (DCF) had previously investigated the couple on three separate occasions, although the cases were closed without finding any evidence of abuse or neglect. However, a recent investigation was launched after a teacher reported concerns about the twins being bound by their hands and feet and made to stand in the bathtub.

On Wednesday, a child welfare judge ordered that the remaining two children in the home, a 7-year-old girl and an 11-year-old boy, be placed in foster care. The DCF's Mark Riordan confirmed that all evidence indicated Nubia was deceased, while Victor was clinging to life in the burn unit at Jackson Memorial Hospital in Miami.

Alessandra, a 6-year-old relative who had stayed with the Barahonas after school, had previously witnessed the twins being locked in a bathroom with a shower curtain taped to the walls and being forced to stand in garbage bins. She had confided in a therapist, prompting a short-lived child welfare investigation. The case highlights the need for vigilance in protecting vulnerable children from abuse and ensuring that those responsible are held accountable for their actions.

reference: – It's one of the more horrific allegations of a child abuse to come along for some time: Florida police found the corpse of a 10-year-old girl, covered in acid, in the back of her adopted father's truck. Her twin brother was in the front seat, nearly overcome by toxic fumes, and is recovering in intensive care from previous injuries including a broken arm and collar bone. Their adopted father, 53-year-old Jorge Barahona, was found outside the truck and has been charged with aggravated child abuse for now, reports CNN. The story coming together suggests the twins were adopted by Jorge and his wife and subjected to systemic abuse, including being bound by their hands and feet and forced to stay in the bathroom, reports the Miami Herald. State authorities had two previous reports of something amiss, including one on Feb. 10, but took no action against the parents. A heart-wrenching detail from the Herald: An investigation finally began when the couple's biological granddaughter told her therapist she wanted to break open her piggy bank to help the twins escape. Click for more.

final_summary:
A high-profile terrorism case unfolding in Chicago has raised questions about the government's motives behind withholding information about the origins of the evidence used to charge Adel Daoud, a 19-year-old man accused of attempting to detonate a car bomb outside a downtown bar. The case has drawn attention to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), specifically Section 702, a provision that allows the U.S. government to intercept foreign targets' emails and phone calls without an individual warrant. 

According to a motion filed by Daoud's lawyers, Thomas Durkin and Joshua Herman, the government is deliberately avoiding disclosure of whether the evidence used to charge Daoud was obtained through Section 702, fearing a potential constitutional challenge to the law. The lawyers argue that if the government acknowledges using evidence derived from Section 702, Daoud would have standing to challenge the law's constitutionality. However, the government has refused to confirm or deny the use of Section 702, instead claiming that the evidence was obtained through a traditional FISA court order four days before a sweeping government surveillance law was set to expire in 2008, Sen. Dianne Feinstein, the chairman of the Senate's Intelligence Committee, took to the Senate floor to tout the law's value by listing some of the terrorist attacks it had helped thwart, including a plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar that fall.

Daoud's lawyers contend that the government is being disingenuous, pointing out that Senator Feinstein's statement suggests that the law was indeed used in the case. The FISA Amendments Act, which governs the U.S. government's surveillance program, has come under intense scrutiny since Edward Snowden revealed details about the scope of the program earlier this month. Critics argue that Section 702's breadth and lack of individual warrants raise significant constitutional concerns. The law targets non-U.S. persons reasonably believed to be located outside the U.S., without requiring a warrant for interception.

Daoud's case began when he came under FBI scrutiny after receiving an email in February 2012 regarding his registration with an online jihadi forum overseas. In May 2012, Daoud sent himself a link for nine issues of Inspire magazine, an online publication put out by al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula, an al-Qaeda affiliate. Two FBI undercover employees posing as jihadists contacted Daoud online in response to material he had posted. Between July and September, an undercover FBI agent met Daoud in Chicago and helped him plan the attack. He was arrested in September after attempting to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar. Daoud has pleaded not guilty and is set to go on trial in February.

Lawyers for Daoud argue that the government is trying to have it both ways – boasting about the effectiveness of the surveillance program when convenient, while hiding behind state secrets when it suits their interests. The case highlights the tension between national security concerns and individual rights, as well as the need for transparency and accountability in the use of surveillance powers. U.S. District Judge Sharon Johnson Coleman has set a trial date for February.

The case has drawn attention to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), specifically Section 702, a provision that allows the National Security Agency (NSA) to intercept foreign targets' emails and phone calls without an individual warrant.

According to a motion filed by Daoud's lawyers, Thomas Durkin and Joshua Herman, the government is deliberately avoiding disclosure of whether the evidence used to charge Daoud was obtained through Section 702, fearing a potential constitutional challenge to the law. The lawyers argue that if the government acknowledges using evidence derived from Section 702, Daoud would have standing to challenge the law's constitutionality. However, the government has refused to confirm or deny the use of Section 702, instead claiming that the evidence was obtained through a traditional FISA court order.

Senator Dianne Feinstein, chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, previously cited the alleged plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar as an example of the effectiveness of the FISA Amendments Act, which was passed in 2008. However, the government is now refusing to say whether this specific case involved the use of Section 702. Daoud's lawyers contend that the government is being disingenuous, pointing out that Senator Feinstein's statement suggests that the law was indeed used in the case.

The FISA Amendments Act, which governs the NSA's surveillance program, has come under intense scrutiny since Edward Snowden's revelations about the scope of the program. Critics argue that Section 702's breadth and lack of individual warrants raise significant constitutional concerns. The law targets non-US persons reasonably believed to be located outside the US, without requiring a warrant for interception.

Daoud's case began when he came under FBI scrutiny after posting online messages about killing Americans. FBI analysts posed as terrorists and exchanged messages with him, helping him plan an attack. He was arrested in September after attempting to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar. Daoud has pleaded not guilty and is set to go on trial in February.

Lawyers for Daoud argue that the government is trying to have it both ways – boasting about the effectiveness of the surveillance program when convenient, while hiding behind state secrets when it suits their interests. The case highlights the tension between national security concerns and individual rights, as well as the need for transparency and accountability in the use of surveillance powers.

history.draft:
A high-profile terrorism case unfolding in Chicago has raised questions about the government's motives behind withholding information about the origins of the evidence used to charge Adel Daoud, a 19-year-old man accused of attempting to detonate a car bomb outside a downtown bar. The case has drawn attention to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), specifically Section 702, a provision that allows the U.S. government to intercept foreign targets' emails and phone calls without an individual warrant. 

According to a motion filed by Daoud's lawyers, Thomas Durkin and Joshua Herman, the government is deliberately avoiding disclosure of whether the evidence used to charge Daoud was obtained through Section 702, fearing a potential constitutional challenge to the law. The lawyers argue that if the government acknowledges using evidence derived from Section 702, Daoud would have standing to challenge the law's constitutionality. However, the government has refused to confirm or deny the use of Section 702, instead claiming that the evidence was obtained through a traditional FISA court order four days before a sweeping government surveillance law was set to expire in 2008, Sen. Dianne Feinstein, the chairman of the Senate's Intelligence Committee, took to the Senate floor to tout the law's value by listing some of the terrorist attacks it had helped thwart, including a plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar that fall.

Daoud's lawyers contend that the government is being disingenuous, pointing out that Senator Feinstein's statement suggests that the law was indeed used in the case. The FISA Amendments Act, which governs the U.S. government's surveillance program, has come under intense scrutiny since Edward Snowden revealed details about the scope of the program earlier this month. Critics argue that Section 702's breadth and lack of individual warrants raise significant constitutional concerns. The law targets non-U.S. persons reasonably believed to be located outside the U.S., without requiring a warrant for interception.

Daoud's case began when he came under FBI scrutiny after receiving an email in February 2012 regarding his registration with an online jihadi forum overseas. In May 2012, Daoud sent himself a link for nine issues of Inspire magazine, an online publication put out by al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula, an al-Qaeda affiliate. Two FBI undercover employees posing as jihadists contacted Daoud online in response to material he had posted. Between July and September, an undercover FBI agent met Daoud in Chicago and helped him plan the attack. He was arrested in September after attempting to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar. Daoud has pleaded not guilty and is set to go on trial in February.

Lawyers for Daoud argue that the government is trying to have it both ways – boasting about the effectiveness of the surveillance program when convenient, while hiding behind state secrets when it suits their interests. The case highlights the tension between national security concerns and individual rights, as well as the need for transparency and accountability in the use of surveillance powers. U.S. District Judge Sharon Johnson Coleman has set a trial date for February.

reference: – Adel Daoud is a Chicago 19-year-old accused by the feds of trying to detonate a bomb outside a Chicago bar in 2012. His case, however, could serve as the first constitutional challenge to the government's sweeping surveillance techniques, reports the Washington Post. In a court filing yesterday, attorneys for the terror suspect demanded to know whether federal authorities used their expanded powers under a 2008 amendment to the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act to nab him. “Whenever it is good for the government to brag about its success, it speaks loudly and publicly,” they wrote, as per the Chicago Tribune. "When a criminal defendant’s constitutional rights are at stake, however, it quickly and unequivocally clams up under the guise of State Secrets.” Federal prosecutors say they're not obligated to say whether they used the law, and a judge will have the final say, reports WLS-TV. So did they use it? Here's a hint: Last year, when the Senate was debating whether to renew the 2008 law—it's formally known as the FISA Amendments Act—Dianne Feinstein made her case in favor by asserting that it had helped stop "a plot to bomb a downtown Chicago bar." If the judge rules that the government must acknowledge it used the FAA, Daoud's attorney's would then be able to challenge its constitutionality.

final_summary:
On Wednesday morning, Mayor Dario Nardella of Florence announced an initiative aimed at discouraging tourists from eating and drinking on the steps of the city's churches. As of lunchtime, city authorities began implementing the plan, which involves pouring water over the church steps to make them unwelcoming for picnickers. According to Nardella, the goal is to encourage tourists to visit nearby cafes and restaurants, which offer outdoor seating and tables, rather than treating the churches as dining areas. 

The first locations targeted by the initiative were the areas surrounding the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Santo Spirito church. Nardella stated that the city would assess the effectiveness of the measure before deciding whether to extend it to other areas, such as pavements. However, some locals expressed concerns that the move may deter tourists from visiting the city or be perceived as elitist, particularly towards those who prefer to eat takeaway food instead of dining at expensive restaurants.

Tourists outside the Santa Croce basilica. Photo: Alberto Pizzoli/AFP

On social media, users criticized the decision, with some labeling it as "orrorre" (horror), a term used by local resident @Stefy_1987 on Twitter on May 31, 2017. Others pointed out that the water-based solution might not be effective in the sweltering heat of the Italian climate, suggesting that picnickers might not mind getting wet. In response to these criticisms, Nardella clarified that the city would not employ powerful water cannons to target tourists but rather focus on cleaning the church steps and pavements during lunchtime.

In fact, Nardella explicitly stated that the city was "in no way against tourists, but against those who do not respect Florence." He emphasized that the city values tourists but aims to address the disrespect shown towards its cultural heritage, citing instances of visitors leaving trash behind and disregarding the historical significance of the sites. This initiative is part of a broader effort to tackle the issues associated with mass tourism in Florence, which attracts approximately 12 million visitors annually.

Since becoming mayor in 2014, Nardella has implemented various measures to mitigate the negative impacts of tourism, including regulating restaurants in the historic center to ensure they serve at least 70 percent locally sourced products and blocking applications for fast-food chains like McDonald's to operate in sensitive areas, such as the Piazza del Duomo. The city's actions follow similar initiatives taken by other Italian cities, including Rome, which has imposed fines on individuals caught eating on the Spanish Steps. In contrast, Florence opted for a more gentle approach, choosing to use water to deter visitors from congregating on church steps. The city's goal is to preserve its rich cultural heritage while still welcoming tourists.

reference: – The mayor of Florence says some tourists have no "decorum" these days—something he hopes to fix with a water hose. Mayor Dario Nardella says a small number of the Italian city's 12 million annual tourists—particularly day-trippers from cruise ships—have taken to "camping out" on church steps at lunchtime. They "sit down on church steps, eat their food, and leave rubbish strewn on them," with no respect for "our cultural heritage," Nardella tells the Guardian. He says city officials thought about handing out fines to such tourists, but they decided instead to get creative. The plan: Use water hoses to soak church steps. That way, if tourists sit down, "they'll get wet," Nardella said in announcing the strategy Wednesday. Shortly after the announcement, the steps of both the Basilica of Santa Croce and the Chiesa di Santo Spirito were doused. In the future, this will be a daily occurrence, reports the Telegraph. "Instead of imposing fines, we thought this measure was more elegant," Nardella tells the Guardian, adding other areas might eventually get a wash as well. While some social media users joked that a free shower might be nice in the heat of summer, others have accused city officials of being "elitist" in targeting tourists who choose not to eat in Florence's restaurants, per the Local. Nardella counters that the city has plenty of outdoor seating for tourists. Churches, however, are no place for a picnic, he says. (Barcelona has an issue with its tourists, too.)

final_summary:
A recent study published in the journal Psychological Science investigated the relationship between emotions and color perception. Researchers Christopher A. Thorstenson and Adam D. Pazda, supervised by Andrew J. Elliot, designed and conducted the study to examine whether emotions can influence color perception. Building on the existing knowledge that emotions can affect low-level visual processes, which are fundamental to higher-order vision, the study aimed to provide further insight into this relationship.

Previous research has demonstrated that emotions can influence color perception, and the current study sought to expand upon this understanding. The study involved two experiments, with the first experiment consisting of 129 college students who were randomly assigned to watch either a sad film clip, an amusing clip, or a neutral screen saver. Specifically, the sad clip featured a poignant scene from Disney's The Lion King, while the amusing clip consisted of a stand-up comedy routine. The neutral condition involved watching a blank desktop screensaver. After viewing the clips, participants were tasked with identifying desaturated colors, specifically red, yellow, green, and blue patches that had been muted to gray.

The results of the first experiment revealed that participants who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing or neutral clips performed accurately. Notably, the impairment in color perception was specific to the blue-yellow axis, suggesting that sadness may have a unique impact on the processing of these colors. This finding is consistent with previous research indicating that dopamine, a neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, plays a crucial role in color perception.

To confirm the results, the researchers conducted a second experiment, which yielded similar findings. Participants who watched the sad clip were less accurate in perceiving color along the blue-yellow axis compared to those who watched the neutral clip. However, their performance was equivalent to the neutral group along the red-green axis. The researchers speculate that dopamine deficiency might influence perception of blue-yellow colors, given that past research has shown that perception of these colors is affected in clinical disorders involving dopamine.

The study's findings have significant implications for our understanding of how emotions shape our subjective experience of the world. According to Christopher A. Thorstenson, lead author of the study, "Our results show that mood and emotion can affect how we see the world around us." The study's conclusions are supported by previous research demonstrating that exposure to certain colors can influence cognitive performance, facial attractiveness, and food intake. Furthermore, the study contributes to the growing body of research exploring the complex interplay between emotions, cognition, and perception.

The study's significance extends beyond the realm of basic science, offering insights into the intricate relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception. By shedding light on the complex interactions between the brain's neural networks, the study provides a deeper understanding of how our experiences shape our perception of reality. As Thorstenson notes, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us." The study was conducted at the University of Rochester in New York, and the findings have sparked interest in the field of psychology, with some researchers arguing that the evidence for top-down effects is still limited.

The study's results have also been discussed in various media outlets, including Time Health, which highlighted the potential link between emotions and color perception. According to Thorstenson, "Data are beginning to emerge supporting the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them." The study's findings have implications for our understanding of the human experience, and the researchers hope to continue exploring the complex relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception in future studies.

In related research, a study published in the Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance found that exposure to the color red can impair cognitive performance, while another study published in the journal Appetite discovered that exposure to certain colors can influence food intake. These findings suggest that colors can have a profound impact on our behavior and cognition, and the study by Thorstenson and colleagues adds to this growing body of research.

The study's lead author, Christopher A. Thorstenson, is a psychologist at the University of Rochester, and the study was published in the journal Psychological Science. The study's findings have been widely discussed in the scientific community, with some researchers arguing that the results support the idea that emotions can influence our perception of reality. According to Thorstenson, "The possibility of top-down effects on perception is tremendous, and our study contributes to this ongoing debate."

The study's results have also been compared to the classic story of Dorothy in the Wizard of Oz, where she finds herself in a world that is not as it appears. Similarly, the study suggests that our perception of reality is not solely determined by objective stimuli but is also influenced by our internal emotional state. As Thorstenson notes, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us." The study's findings have significant implications for our understanding of the human experience, and the researchers hope to continue exploring the complex relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception in future studies.

history.draft:
A groundbreaking study published in the esteemed journal Psychological Science has shed new light on the intricate relationship between human emotions and color perception. Led by researcher Christopher Thorstenson, a psychologist at the University of Rochester in New York, the study aimed to investigate whether emotions can influence color perception. According to Thorstenson, "Color is such an important part of our experience," and the research sought to uncover the underlying mechanisms behind the common observation that people's moods can alter their perception of colors.

In the study, participants were randomly assigned to watch either a sad film clip, an amusing clip, or a neutral screen saver. The sad clip featured a poignant scene from Disney's The Lion King, where Mufasa's death evokes intense sorrow, while the amusing clip consisted of a stand-up comedy routine. The neutral condition involved watching a blank desktop screensaver. After viewing the clips, participants were tasked with identifying desaturated colors, specifically red, yellow, green, and blue patches that had been muted to gray.

The results revealed a striking correlation between emotional state and color perception. Participants who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing or neutral clips performed accurately. Notably, the impairment in color perception was specific to the blue-yellow axis, suggesting that sadness may have a unique impact on the processing of these colors. This finding is consistent with previous research indicating that dopamine, a neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, plays a crucial role in color perception.

Thorstenson's study builds upon the idea that emotions can influence low-level visual processes, which are essential for higher-order vision. The researchers propose that the connection between emotions and color perception may be rooted in the brain's neural pathways, where dopamine transmission is critical for processing visual information. The study's findings have implications for our understanding of how emotions shape our subjective experience of the world.

Interestingly, the study's results echo the notion that our perception of reality is not solely determined by objective stimuli but is also influenced by our internal emotional state. This concept, known as the "top-down" effect, suggests that higher-level cognition can modulate perception, rather than merely reflecting it. While some researchers argue that the evidence for top-down effects is still limited, Thorstenson's study contributes to the growing body of research exploring the complex interplay between emotions, cognition, and perception.

The study's lead author, Christopher Thorstenson, emphasized that the findings have broader implications for our understanding of the human experience. He stated, "Our results show that mood and emotion can affect how we see the world around us." The study's conclusions are supported by previous research demonstrating that exposure to certain colors can influence cognitive performance, facial attractiveness, and food intake. As Thorstenson noted, "Data are beginning to emerge supporting the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them."

The study's significance extends beyond the realm of basic science, offering insights into the intricate relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception. By shedding light on the complex interactions between the brain's neural networks, the study provides a deeper understanding of how our experiences shape our perception of reality. As Thorstenson aptly put it, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us."

Researchers C. A. Thorstenson and A. D. Pazda led a study published in the journal Psychological Science to investigate the relationship between emotions and color perception. The study aimed to explore whether emotions can influence color perception, building on the idea that emotions can affect low-level visual processes, which are essential for higher-order vision. 

Past research has suggested that emotions can influence color perception, and the current study aimed to provide further insight into this relationship. The study involved two experiments, conducted by C. A. Thorstenson and A. D. Pazda, with the supervision of A. J. Elliot. In the first experiment, 129 college students were randomly assigned to watch either a sad film clip, an amusing clip, or a neutral screen saver. The sad clip featured a poignant scene from Disney's The Lion King, while the amusing clip consisted of a stand-up comedy routine. The neutral condition involved watching a blank desktop screensaver. After viewing the clips, participants were tasked with identifying desaturated colors, specifically red, yellow, green, and blue patches that had been muted to gray.

The results of the first experiment revealed that participants who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing or neutral clips performed accurately. Notably, the impairment in color perception was specific to the blue-yellow axis, suggesting that sadness may have a unique impact on the processing of these colors. This finding is consistent with previous research indicating that dopamine, a neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, plays a crucial role in color perception.

To confirm the results, the researchers conducted a second experiment, which yielded similar findings. Participants who watched the sad clip were less accurate in perceiving color along the blue-yellow axis compared to those who watched the neutral clip. However, their performance was equivalent to the neutral group along the red-green axis. The researchers speculate that dopamine deficiency might influence perception of blue-yellow colors, given that past research has shown that perception of these colors is affected in clinical disorders involving dopamine.

The study's findings have implications for our understanding of how emotions shape our subjective experience of the world. The results suggest that our perception of reality is not solely determined by objective stimuli but is also influenced by our internal emotional state. This concept, known as the "top-down" effect, proposes that higher-level cognition can modulate perception, rather than merely reflecting it. As Thorstenson aptly put it, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us."
