reference: – Faced with a 12-year-old girl hanging by an arm from an 80-foot-high bridge, threatening to end her young life, a rookie cop started talking, reports CBS Los Angeles. Asking the girl if he could come closer, Officer Chris Perez says the child "was just crying and holding on;" she told Perez she had fled a group home in LA, and that she needed help. "It was just back-and-forth banter with me and her," Perez says. "'Can I come closer to you? Can I talk you closer? Can I pull you over the ledge so we can talk in a safer environment?' It was that back-to-back conversation with her that I felt was going so smoothly that I felt comfortable enough to lean over and get her." Per CBS, Perez "leaned over the railing, grabbed her in a bear hug, and helped her over the railing to safety." Police officials credit Perez's "calm, professional actions" with saving the girl, while Perez himself seems appalled that someone so young could find herself so desperate. "I can’t relate, that, oh my Lord, this young individual, this juvenile, is on the other side [on the bridge] what could possibly be going wrong in your life to make you want to do something like this?" But after the rescue, "she was sad and just started breaking down even more," Perez tells CBS News. The girl, who wanted to reconnect with an aunt, her only family, was put in police custody.

final_summary:
According to a recent survey conducted by ForbesWoman and the National Endowment for Financial Education (NEFE), nearly one-third of Americans who share their finances with their spouses admit to lying to them about money, while another third claim to have been deceived - specifically, 31%. This staggering statistic comes from an online poll conducted by Harris Interactive from December 17 to 21 among 2,019 U.S. adults. The survey reveals that financial infidelity is a widespread issue affecting many relationships, with 67% of couples impacted by financial dishonesty reporting that it led to an argument.

The survey found that the majority of financial dishonesty involves hiding small transactions such as cash, minor purchases, and bills, but a significant number of individuals also conceal major purchases, maintain secret bank accounts, and falsify information about their debt or earnings. NEFE's Chief Executive, Ted Beck, emphasizes that "a third of the population admits to not being honest with their spouse," which can lead to severe consequences, including arguments, reduced trust, and even divorce. Specifically, 16% of those affected by financial infidelity reported that it led to a divorce, while 11% stated it resulted in a separation.

Psychotherapist Tina Tessina notes that financial deceit can be just as damaging as other forms of infidelity, causing a total loss of trust, feelings of betrayal, and relationship destruction. Seattle resident Theo Pauline Nestor experienced this firsthand when she discovered her husband's secret gambling habit and accumulated debt, which ultimately led to their divorce after 13 years of marriage.

The survey found that over half of financial cheaters hide cash (58%) or minor purchases (54%), with 30% admitting to concealing bills, 16% hiding major purchases, 15% maintaining secret bank accounts, 11% lying about their debts, and 11% misrepresenting their earnings. Matrimonial and family law attorney Nancy Chemtob, who specializes in cases involving financial infidelity, observes that having more wealth does not necessarily mean more honesty, as individuals with higher incomes often engage in more complex schemes to deceive their partners.

Chemtob cites examples of clients who have been victims of financial infidelity, including a woman who fabricated her entire financial history during courtship and another who lied about receiving child support. Family therapist Carleton Kendrick attributes financial dishonesty to various motivations, such as pragmatism, control, guilt, and fear, and notes that societal norms and upbringing can influence individual attitudes towards money in relationships.

Interestingly, the survey reveals that men and women are equally likely to engage in financial infidelity, although women are more likely to report discovering their partner's deceit. However, men tend to perceive their partners as hiding minor purchases more frequently than women, whereas women are more likely to accuse their partners of lying about their earnings and debt. NEFE's Beck recommends discussing finances openly and honestly, ideally before marriage, and suggests using tax season as a non-threatening opportunity to address financial concerns.
anchors: The National Endowment For Financial Education

history.draft:
According to a recent survey conducted by ForbesWoman and the National Endowment for Financial Education (NEFE), nearly one-third of Americans who share their finances with their spouses admit to lying to them about money, while another third claim to have been deceived - specifically, 31%. This staggering statistic comes from an online poll conducted by Harris Interactive from December 17 to 21 among 2,019 U.S. adults. The survey reveals that financial infidelity is a widespread issue affecting many relationships, with 67% of couples impacted by financial dishonesty reporting that it led to an argument.

The survey found that the majority of financial dishonesty involves hiding small transactions such as cash, minor purchases, and bills, but a significant number of individuals also conceal major purchases, maintain secret bank accounts, and falsify information about their debt or earnings. NEFE's Chief Executive, Ted Beck, emphasizes that "a third of the population admits to not being honest with their spouse," which can lead to severe consequences, including arguments, reduced trust, and even divorce. Specifically, 16% of those affected by financial infidelity reported that it led to a divorce, while 11% stated it resulted in a separation.

Psychotherapist Tina Tessina notes that financial deceit can be just as damaging as other forms of infidelity, causing a total loss of trust, feelings of betrayal, and relationship destruction. Seattle resident Theo Pauline Nestor experienced this firsthand when she discovered her husband's secret gambling habit and accumulated debt, which ultimately led to their divorce after 13 years of marriage.

The survey found that over half of financial cheaters hide cash (58%) or minor purchases (54%), with 30% admitting to concealing bills, 16% hiding major purchases, 15% maintaining secret bank accounts, 11% lying about their debts, and 11% misrepresenting their earnings. Matrimonial and family law attorney Nancy Chemtob, who specializes in cases involving financial infidelity, observes that having more wealth does not necessarily mean more honesty, as individuals with higher incomes often engage in more complex schemes to deceive their partners.

Chemtob cites examples of clients who have been victims of financial infidelity, including a woman who fabricated her entire financial history during courtship and another who lied about receiving child support. Family therapist Carleton Kendrick attributes financial dishonesty to various motivations, such as pragmatism, control, guilt, and fear, and notes that societal norms and upbringing can influence individual attitudes towards money in relationships.

Interestingly, the survey reveals that men and women are equally likely to engage in financial infidelity, although women are more likely to report discovering their partner's deceit. However, men tend to perceive their partners as hiding minor purchases more frequently than women, whereas women are more likely to accuse their partners of lying about their earnings and debt. NEFE's Beck recommends discussing finances openly and honestly, ideally before marriage, and suggests using tax season as a non-threatening opportunity to address financial concerns.

reference: – Infidelity isn't always the stuff of illicit affairs. A new survey shows that 31% of Americans who combine their finances with a spouse have lied about money to said spouse. "Financial infidelity may be the new normal," writes Jenna Goudreau at her Forbes blog. The most common misdeeds were hiding cash (58%), hiding minor purchases (54%), and hiding a bill (30%). But a decent number admitted to hiding major purchases (16%), keeping a secret bank account (15%), and lying about debt or earnings (11%). “Betrayal regarding money can be just as painful and damaging as other kinds of cheating," says a psychotherapist. It, too, can lead to "total loss of trust, feelings of betrayal and destruction of the relationship." For those feeling a little suspicious, Forbes has a list of 10 red flags (defensiveness, insistence on handling bills alone, etc.) to look for.

final_summary:
It's become a familiar ritual: "Click here if you have read and agree to the Terms of Service." How many times in your life have you hovered over that little ticky box without bothering to click the TOS link first? Or scrolled straight to the bottom of a pop-up window with thousands of words of legalese in it, just to continue installation? If your answer is anything other than "all the times," you are likely in a very small minority.

A new study from researchers at two universities confirms what most of us already anecdotally know: nobody's actually reading the fine print, even if they should. According to a recent study, a staggering 98% of people fail to read the fine print when signing up for online services. The study involved creating a fictitious social networking site called NameDrop, whose terms of service and privacy policy were modeled after those of LinkedIn. However, the researchers added several "gotcha" clauses that would normally raise red flags, including one stating that users would have to share their data with the NSA and other security agencies in the United States and abroad, potentially impacting their eligibility for various aspects of life, and another requiring users to assign their firstborn child to the site, with the agreement becoming enforceable until the year 2050 if the user did not yet have children - reminiscent of the infamous deal made with Rumpelstiltskin.

The study found that out of 543 participants, only 10-11 noticed the existence of these clauses, while the rest either skipped the fine print altogether or quickly scanned it without fully understanding its contents. On average, participants who chose to read the terms of service spent just 51 seconds doing so, while those who read the privacy policy averaged 73 seconds. These speeds are far below the estimated time it would take to thoroughly comprehend the lengthy documents, which consisted of approximately 8000 and 4000 words, respectively.

This phenomenon is not unique to younger generations; a similar experiment conducted in the UK in 2014 found the same results, where users unknowingly signed away their firstborn in exchange for access to a free WiFi hotspot. In 2010, a UK-based retailer discovered that customers were willing to sign over their rights without hesitation. Other studies have shown that fewer than one in five internet users actually reads the terms of service, and even those who do often don't bother to process the information.

The study's findings highlight the importance of critically evaluating online agreements before clicking "accept." As long as companies disclose their intentions, they can essentially do whatever they want with users' accounts and personal information. The researchers attribute this lack of attention to the complexity and length of the policies, which are often poorly formatted and filled with legalese. While there are tools available to aid comprehension, there is still a long way to go in making online policies more accessible and understandable. A study published on Ars Technica on April 2, 2016, last revised on August 18, 2018, and originally written on June 1, 2018, further emphasizes the issue, suggesting that information overload is a significant negative predictor of reading terms of service upon signup.

reference: – If Facebook or Apple ever turns truly evil, we might be in big trouble, apparently. A study published last week found that almost nobody reads online terms of service agreements—and even fewer understand them. Ars Technica reports 543 college students signed up for a fake social networking site called NameDrop, believing they were helping with a "pre-launch evaluation." They were actually agreeing to hand over their first-born child to NameDrop—enforceable through 2050—as well as have their data and information shared with the NSA, foreign security agencies, and all manner of third parties, according to Consumerist. But, of course, only 2% of participants realized they were agreeing to any of that. The study found 399 participants didn't read any of the 12,000 words comprising NameDrop's terms of service and privacy policy. The other 144 spent an average of 73 seconds reading the privacy policy and 51 seconds reading the terms of service. Based on average reading speed, it should have taken 30 minutes and 16 minutes respectively. In the end, 98% of participants didn't notice any of the onerous clauses in NameDrops's terms. The study concluded that people "view policies as nuisance" and that "I agree to these terms and conditions" is the "biggest lie on the internet." (Amazon will void its terms of service if the zombies come.)

final_summary:
In a tense standoff over the U.S. debt ceiling, Speaker John Boehner has reiterated his stance that President Barack Obama must accept entitlement cuts without tax increases in exchange for a debt-ceiling increase. This hardline approach comes ahead of crucial talks at the White House between Obama and congressional leaders aimed at averting a U.S. default. Boehner's comments follow Obama's praise for the Speaker's willingness to explore a "grand bargain" involving significant spending reductions on popular programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and Social Security in return for as much as $1 trillion in new revenue. However, Boehner abruptly ended their one-on-one discussions on Saturday after facing criticism from conservative lawmakers and activists, including House Majority Leader Eric Cantor.

Boehner maintained that the disagreement between him and Obama lies in policy, stating that the president wants to raise taxes on job creators while he believes the focus should be on reducing entitlements. He emphasized that the House cannot pass a bill increasing taxes on job creators and instead proposed a debt-limit increase tied to spending cuts exceeding the hike in the debt limit, along with measures to restrain future spending. Additionally, Boehner advocated for the passage of a balanced-budget amendment, which will be debated in the House next week.

Boehner disputed claims that he had discussed tax increases during his private talks with Obama, asserting that his goal was to simplify and lower tax rates, which would stimulate economic growth and generate additional revenue for the federal government. However, opposition from Cantor and Senate Republicans led Boehner to retreat on such an agreement. In a statement, Boehner acknowledged that he shares Obama's concern about raising the U.S. borrowing limit to prevent a government default but remains adamant that House Republicans will not support any deal involving tax hikes.

Boehner expressed his preference for significant tax reform, suggesting that increased revenues could be achieved without raising taxes. He criticized the White House for its perceived lack of commitment to reforming major social benefit programs, echoing his earlier assertion that Obama insists on raising taxes. The House cannot pass a bill that raises taxes on job creators. The government faces a looming deadline to reach a deal that combines spending cuts and a debt-ceiling increase.

In a tense standoff over the U.S. debt ceiling, Speaker John Boehner has reiterated his stance that President Barack Obama must accept entitlement cuts without tax increases in exchange for a debt-ceiling increase. This hardline approach comes ahead of crucial talks at the White House between Obama and congressional leaders aimed at averting a U.S. default. Boehner's comments follow Obama's praise for the Speaker's willingness to explore a "grand bargain" involving significant spending reductions on popular programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and Social Security in return for as much as $1 trillion in new revenue. However, Boehner abruptly ended their one-on-one discussions on Saturday after facing criticism from conservative lawmakers and activists, including House Majority Leader Eric Cantor.

Boehner maintained that the disagreement between him and Obama lies in policy, stating that the president wants to raise taxes on job creators while he believes the focus should be on reducing entitlements. He emphasized that the House cannot pass a bill increasing taxes on job creators and instead proposed a debt-limit increase tied to spending cuts exceeding the hike in the debt limit, along with measures to restrain future spending. Additionally, Boehner advocated for the passage of a balanced-budget amendment, which will be debated in the House next week.

Boehner disputed claims that he had discussed tax increases during his private talks with Obama, asserting that his goal was to simplify and lower tax rates, which would stimulate economic growth and generate additional revenue for the federal government. However, opposition from Cantor and Senate Republicans led Boehner to retreat on such an agreement. In a statement, Boehner acknowledged that he shares Obama's concern about raising the U.S. borrowing limit to prevent a government default but remains adamant that House Republicans will not support any deal involving tax hikes.

Boehner expressed his preference for significant tax reform, suggesting that increased revenues could be achieved without raising taxes. He criticized the White House for its perceived lack of commitment to reforming major social benefit programs, echoing his earlier assertion that Obama insists on raising taxes. The House cannot pass a bill that raises taxes on job creators. The government faces a looming deadline to reach a deal that combines spending cuts and a debt-ceiling increase.

reference: – John Boehner stuck to his guns in a press conference today, offering precious little hope that a debt ceiling deal was anywhere close to getting done. He thanked Barack Obama for praising his "good-faith effort," but said it mattered little. “Our disagreements are not personal,” he said, according to Politico. “They never have been. The gulf between the two parties now is about policy.” “This boil downs to two things: The president insists on raising taxes, and they’re just not serious enough [about] fundamental entitlement reform,” Boehner said. “It takes two to tango, and they’re just not there yet.” Boehner said he did fundamentally agree that the debt limit needed to rise, the AP reports. But he said the bill could only pass the House if it included an even larger amount of spending cuts.

final_summary:
In the wake of the surprise Season 3 premiere of Rick and Morty, fans have been clamoring for the revival of McDonald's Szechuan dipping sauce, which was released in 1998 to promote the Disney movie Mulan in the U.S. This nostalgia-fueled frenzy has led to a thriving market for rare and discontinued food products, with enthusiasts willing to pay exorbitant prices for a taste of their childhood. A prime example of this phenomenon is the sale of a 20-year-old packet of the coveted sauce on eBay for a staggering $14,700, a price tag that reflects the intense desire among fans to recapture their childhood memories.

Brian Florence, the curator of Sodafinder.com, a website dedicated to cataloging and selling hard-to-find soft drinks, has capitalized on this trend. Based in the United States, Florence travels extensively throughout the country to hunt down unique regional varieties of soda, often using his findings to fuel his online business. His collection includes over 400 12-packs of canned soft drinks, featuring rare flavors such as Wink, Buffalo Rock Ginger Ale, and Coca-Cola made with real sugar, which he sells to customers who are willing to pay premium prices for a taste of their youth.

Patrick Meynders, an Amazon seller from Alberta, Canada, has also tapped into this market, specializing in discontinued snacks like Dunkaroos, which he imports from Europe to meet the demands of his customers. Similarly, Josh Nichols, an eBay vendor from Texas, has built a business around selling Dr. Pepper, particularly the Dublin Dr. Pepper variant made with real cane sugar, which he purchases directly from the manufacturer. Another notable entrepreneur is Pam Lloyd-Camp, who runs a robust eBay candy shop called junk-it-junction, selling old-timey favorites like giant Jawbreakers, candy cigarettes, and Sugar Daddies.

The appeal of these products lies in their ability to evoke nostalgia and transport consumers back to a simpler, more carefree era. Darrel D. Muehling, a marketing expert, notes that nostalgia is a powerful tool in advertising, allowing brands to tap into consumers' fond memories and create a sense of longing. However, this nostalgia can be a double-edged sword, as evidenced by the mixed reactions to Meynders' Dunkaroos, with some customers praising the taste while others express disappointment due to changes in formulation.

As the demand for these products continues to grow, entrepreneurs like Florence and Meynders must navigate the complex landscape of sourcing, pricing, and distribution. Florence has developed a system for setting prices based on his costs, including transportation and food expenses, while also adjusting for demand. Meanwhile, Meynders relies on his relationships with suppliers and his ability to adapt to changing market conditions.

Jerry, Rick's hapless counterpart, would likely approach this situation differently, perhaps using Jerry's credit card to acquire the sauce. The revival of McDonald's Szechuan dipping sauce remains a topic of debate, with the company's spokesperson hinting that they may reconsider bringing back the product in response to customer demand. Until then, fans will continue to scour eBay and other online platforms, hoping to stumble upon a rare packet of the elusive sauce.

reference: – This is either a big fan of the cartoon Rick and Morty, a hopeful collector, or a very brave and nostalgic soul: A packet of 1998 McDonald's Szechuan sauce has sold for $14,700 on eBay, reports Time. The strange background: McDonald's put out the dipping sauce in that year as a publicity tie-in for the Disney movie Mulan, and it received a giant nostalgic push when the character Rick from the popular Adult Swim show pined for it in a recent episode. That led to a viral campaign for McDonald's to bring it back, complete with multiple Change.org petitions. The company has hinted it might do so, and hopeful fans have noted that a remake of Mulan is in the works for next year. But the eBay sale is the strangest component yet. "I just bought a really old car, while cleaning it I found a packet of this sauce," the listing stated. "After watching the recent episode of Rick and Morty I went online to see if it was worth anything. Turns out it was. Also this comes with a packet of wasabi as well." A post at Eater notes that what it calls "nostalgic food" is popular on eBay, "but this is an unusually high price tag." No word yet on the identity of the buyer, or plans for the sauce, but the Daily Dot is issuing a challenge: The character Rick would definitely eat the stuff, "so how about it, new owner of rare McDonald’s Mulan dipping sauce. Are you squanchy enough to be a Rick?" (A brother recently drove his sister to McDonald's, which is notable only because they have a combined age of 11.)

final_summary:
TMZ reports that singer Mariah Carey and actor Nick Cannon have been living apart since May, with sources indicating that their separation has been ongoing for months. According to insiders, divorce lawyers have been negotiating a property settlement and custody agreement. While the exact reason behind their split remains unclear, sources suggest it may be linked to a public appearance made by Nick Cannon on Big Boy's radio show in March, where he discussed his personal life, including naming five celebrities he had slept with. 

In related news, PEOPLE magazine reported that Ronnie Magro and Sammi Giancola, a couple from the reality TV show Jersey Shore, have officially ended their five-year on-again, off-again romance. In an interview with PEOPLE, Magro stated that the couple grew apart and stopped spending quality time together, ultimately deciding it was best to go their separate ways. Magro has since become involved in caring for his friend Jenni "JWoww" Farley's newborn daughter, Meilani.

PEOPLE also confirmed that actress Meg Ryan and musician John Mellencamp have ended their relationship after dating for more than three years. The couple cited the distance between them as the primary reason for their breakup, with Ryan residing in New York and Mellencamp based in Indiana. Mellencamp has expressed his preference for a quieter lifestyle, free from the pressures of city life and constant media attention. Ryan is currently working on her directorial debut, Ithaca, a WWII drama.

Celebrity splits have been dominating headlines lately, with multiple high-profile couples announcing their separations. One such couple is singer Mariah Carey and actor Nick Cannon, who have reportedly been living apart since May. According to sources close to the couple, their separation has been ongoing for months, with divorce lawyers having negotiated a property settlement and custody agreement. The exact reason behind their split remains unclear, but insiders claim it may be linked to a public appearance made by Nick Cannon on Big Boy's radio show in March, where he revealed details about his personal life, including naming five celebrities he had slept with. This revelation allegedly left Mariah Carey feeling humiliated, leading to a breakdown in their relationship.

Meanwhile, another celebrity couple, Ronnie Magro and Sammi Giancola, have officially called it quits after a five-year on-again, off-again romance. In an interview with People magazine, Magro revealed that the couple had grown apart and stopped spending quality time together, ultimately deciding it was best to go their separate ways. Magro has since taken on a new role as uncle to his friend Jenni "JWoww" Farley's newborn daughter, Meilani.

In other celebrity news, actress Meg Ryan and musician John Mellencamp have ended their three-year relationship due to the strain of their long-distance lifestyle. With Ryan residing in New York and Mellencamp based in Indiana, the distance proved too much for the couple to overcome. Mellencamp, who has expressed his dislike for the fast-paced city life and constant media attention, has returned to his home state to focus on his music career, while Ryan has turned her attention to her directorial debut, Ithaca, a WWII drama set to be released later this year.

Zac knew this about her when he got with her," one source says. "He's very into her though, and perhaps more than she's into him." Rodriguez, 36, and Efron, 26, reportedly had a big fight in Ibiza that prompted the split. In slightly more D-list news, Jersey Shore couple Ronnie Magro and Sammi "Sweetheart" Giancola are done for good after an on-again, off-again five-year relationship, Magro tells People. "We were drifting apart and not really spending any time together," he says. "She's a great girl, but we were growing distant." And, though there's no official word on this yet, multiple outlets are reporting that Mariah Carey and Nick Cannon are all but divorced. Lawyers have been negotiating property and custody agreements for months, according to TMZ, and the couple has been living separately since May. According to Page Six's sources, Carey, 45, thinks Cannon, 33, might have cheated.

final_summary:
Dave Mirra, a legendary figure in the world of BMX, tragically passed away on February 4 at the age of 41. His untimely death was met with shock and sadness from the BMX community and beyond. Recent revelations have shed light on the underlying cause of his demise, revealing that Mirra suffered from Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative disease linked to repeated head trauma. According to neuropathologist Lili-Naz Hazrati of the University of Toronto, Mirra's brain showed signs of CTE, a condition previously associated with football and hockey players. This diagnosis marks a significant milestone, making Mirra the first action sports athlete to be officially diagnosed with CTE. Hazrati's findings were corroborated by multiple neuropathologists from both the US and Canada, further solidifying the conclusion.

Mirra's CTE diagnosis raises concerns about the long-term effects of repeated head trauma in action sports. Throughout his illustrious career, Mirra accumulated numerous concussions, including a particularly severe incident at age 19 when he was struck by a car while walking outside a nightclub. Despite suffering a fractured skull and a blood clot in his brain, Mirra remarkably recovered and continued to pursue his passion for BMX. However, his determination and resilience may have ultimately contributed to his downfall. Mirra's wife, Lauren, revealed in an interview with ESPN The Magazine that her husband began exhibiting noticeable personality changes in his final weeks, struggling with mood swings, memory loss, and depression. These symptoms are characteristic of advanced stages of CTE, a disease that can only be diagnosed post-mortem.

Lauren's account highlights the devastating impact of CTE on individuals and their loved ones. She emphasized the importance of raising awareness about the disease, stating, "This is the beginning of bringing awareness. It would be amazing if this is something we can detect in life one day. If we can detect it, prevent it, stop it, let's do all of the above." As the BMX community mourns the loss of a beloved icon, Mirra's legacy serves as a poignant reminder of the risks associated with action sports. His story underscores the need for greater understanding and support for athletes who suffer from CTE and its consequences.

In the wake of his passing, Mirra's family has expressed gratitude for the outpouring of support and has vowed to continue advocating for CTE awareness and research. In recognition of his contributions to the sport, Mirra will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame in June and will be honored at the X Games with a new event bearing his name, the Dave Mirra BMX Park Best Trick competition. While these tributes serve as a testament to Mirra's enduring impact, they also serve as a poignant reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of prioritizing athlete safety and well-being. Mirra's final weeks were marked by struggles with mood swings, memory loss, and depression, a stark contrast to his usual optimistic outlook. His family has requested that donations be made to the Concussion Legacy Foundation in lieu of flowers.

reference: – Dave Mirra, the legendary BMX star who killed himself in February, had chronic traumatic encephalopathy—a disease more commonly seen in football players and boxers, ESPN reports. He's believed to be the first action sports athlete to be diagnosed with it. "The key is brain injury," says the neuropathologist who diagnosed Mirra. "Regardless of how you get it, through BMX or hockey, you are at risk for this." Mirra suffered numerous concussions throughout his BMX career. He once described a 2006 crash to the Washington Post by saying, “I basically fell 16 feet straight to my head.” “This is a young man that had a pretty rugged sports career and took a lot of injuries," USA Today quotes a friend as saying. Mirra's diagnosis of CTE was confirmed by multiple neuropathologists, though it's unclear what stage the disease was in. Scans of Mirra's brain showed the same tau protein deposits as those in the brains of former football players. CTE, which is only able to be diagnosed after death, can cause depression, dementia, and memory loss. Mirra's wife Lauren says Mirra underwent major mood and personality changes in the year before he killed himself at 41. “We ask for your continued support in honoring Dave’s legacy and for your patience as we plan to create a platform for CTE awareness and research," reads a statement from Lauren, per USA Today. Mirra, who "was the face of his sport," will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame next month. (Also on Tuesday, Bubba Smith was announced as the 90th former NFL player to be diagnosed with CTE.)

final_summary:
Michael and Cynthia Rondini, the parents of Megan Rondini, a University of Alabama student who died by suicide in 2016, have filed a federal wrongful death lawsuit against several individuals and organizations, including T.J. Bunn Jr., the man Megan accused of raping her. The lawsuit alleges that the university and local law enforcement mishandled Megan's case after she reported the alleged assault in July 2015. According to the lawsuit, Megan met Bunn at a popular bar in Tuscaloosa, where she had gone out with friends, and was later taken to his home, where she was allegedly sexually assaulted, possibly after being drugged.

BuzzFeed News reported on the case, revealing that the police investigation was marred by allegations of bias towards Bunn and a lack of attention given to Megan's account of events. Investigators questioned Megan about her actions during the alleged assault, including taking a handgun and $3 for cab fare from Bunn's home, rather than focusing on gathering evidence to support her claim of rape. The lawsuit claims that Megan was treated as a suspect rather than a victim, and that her status as a survivor of a sex crime was disregarded. Despite testing positive for a sexually transmitted disease that she likely contracted from Bunn, Megan's allegations were not taken seriously by the authorities. The investigation was further compromised by the failure to test her rape kit, interview witnesses, and record all interviews conducted.

The University of Alabama's Title IX coordinator, Beth Howard, and a therapist from the university, Cara Blakes, were also named in the lawsuit for their alleged mishandling of Megan's case. However, the university claimed that it had provided Megan with support and resources, including meeting her at the hospital, providing information about services available on campus, and referring her to a new therapist after the initial one recused herself due to a conflict of interest. However, the lawsuit disputes this, stating that Megan was denied counseling services and that the university showed hostility towards her.

Megan eventually dropped the case, left the university, and returned to her hometown of Austin, Texas, where she struggled with depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Her parents claim that the trauma she experienced from the rape and the inadequate response from law enforcement and the university ultimately led to her decision to take her own life. The lawsuit seeks an unspecified amount of damages and aims to bring attention to the need for improved handling of sexual assault cases by law enforcement and universities. The University of Alabama has since announced plans to establish two sexual assault programs to care for victims, and its staff is undergoing training in the SANE (Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner) course curriculum.

history.draft:
Michael and Cynthia Rondini, the parents of Megan Rondini, a University of Alabama student who died by suicide in 2016, have filed a federal wrongful death lawsuit against several individuals and organizations, including T.J. Bunn Jr., the man Megan accused of raping her. The lawsuit alleges that the university and local law enforcement mishandled Megan's case after she reported the alleged assault in July 2015. According to the lawsuit, Megan met Bunn at a popular bar in Tuscaloosa, where she had gone out with friends, and was later taken to his home, where she was allegedly sexually assaulted, possibly after being drugged.

BuzzFeed News reported on the case, revealing that the police investigation was marred by allegations of bias towards Bunn and a lack of attention given to Megan's account of events. Investigators questioned Megan about her actions during the alleged assault, including taking a handgun and $3 for cab fare from Bunn's home, rather than focusing on gathering evidence to support her claim of rape. The lawsuit claims that Megan was treated as a suspect rather than a victim, and that her status as a survivor of a sex crime was disregarded. Despite testing positive for a sexually transmitted disease that she likely contracted from Bunn, Megan's allegations were not taken seriously by the authorities. The investigation was further compromised by the failure to test her rape kit, interview witnesses, and record all interviews conducted.

The University of Alabama's Title IX coordinator, Beth Howard, and a therapist from the university, Cara Blakes, were also named in the lawsuit for their alleged mishandling of Megan's case. However, the university claimed that it had provided Megan with support and resources, including meeting her at the hospital, providing information about services available on campus, and referring her to a new therapist after the initial one recused herself due to a conflict of interest. However, the lawsuit disputes this, stating that Megan was denied counseling services and that the university showed hostility towards her.

Megan eventually dropped the case, left the university, and returned to her hometown of Austin, Texas, where she struggled with depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Her parents claim that the trauma she experienced from the rape and the inadequate response from law enforcement and the university ultimately led to her decision to take her own life. The lawsuit seeks an unspecified amount of damages and aims to bring attention to the need for improved handling of sexual assault cases by law enforcement and universities. The University of Alabama has since announced plans to establish two sexual assault programs to care for victims, and its staff is undergoing training in the SANE (Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner) course curriculum.

reference: – Former University of Alabama student Megan Rondini would still be alive if not for the "mishandling" of her alleged rape, her parents say. In a wrongful death lawsuit filed Sunday, Rondini's parents accuse the university, school officials, sheriff's deputies, and Rondini's alleged rapist of enabling her suicide in February 2016. Rondini had been a 20-year-old student at the University of Alabama in July 2015 when she alleged she was raped by local businessman TJ Bunn Jr. But authorities "wrongfully acted or failed to act in response," the lawsuit alleges, per Buzzfeed. It adds deputies—who found the case didn't meet Alabama's legal definition for rape—didn't test Rondini's rape kit or interview witnesses, an "intentional" behavior that was due to Rondini's gender. Rondini—who allegedly stole $3 and a gun from Bunn—was "treated as a crime suspect," not a victim, the suit continues, per the AP, also alleging a school counselor refused to see Rondini unless she took anxiety medication. Rondini later dropped out of school, moved back to Texas, and suffered "extreme depression, anxiety, PTSD, fear, panic attacks, decline of cognitive functions and general well-being … all of which directly led to Megan's loss of life," the suit states. Per AL.com, two days before her suicide, she sent a text reading, "I wonder what I could've accomplished if one man didn't completely rip everything away from me." Bunn, who claimed the sex was consensual, was never charged with a crime. His lawyer tells AL.com that the lawsuit is "baseless" and "simply false."

final_summary:
On Sunday, April 22, 2018, a mass shooting occurred at a Waffle House restaurant in Nashville, Tennessee, resulting in the deaths of four people and injuring four others. The suspect, Travis Reinking, allegedly opened fire in the parking lot and then stormed the restaurant, targeting patrons and employees. Eyewitnesses described Reinking as nearly naked, wearing only a green jacket and brandishing an assault-style rifle during the attack. James Shaw, a quick-thinking customer, wrestled the gun away from Reinking, preventing further loss of life.

An intensive manhunt ensued, with police warning residents of the surrounding neighborhood to exercise caution and check on their neighbors. Schools in the area were placed on lockdown, with students allowed to move freely within the buildings but denied entry to external visitors. Authorities searched for Reinking, who was described as potentially armed and possibly hiding in a wooded area near an apartment complex where he resided.

As the investigation unfolded, disturbing reports emerged about Reinking's troubled past, including incidents in his hometown of Morton, Illinois. In May 2016, Reinking claimed that pop star Taylor Swift was stalking him and hacking his phone, prompting a visit from Tazewell County deputies. In July 2017, Reinking attempted to breach a restricted area near the White House, stating he wanted to meet President Donald Trump. His state firearms card was subsequently revoked, and four guns, including an AR-15, were confiscated. Reinking's father, Jeffrey, took possession of the guns under the condition that he would keep them secure and out of his son's reach. However, it remains unclear whether Reinking regained access to the firearms.

Nashville Police Chief Steve Anderson expressed concerns that Reinking may have mental health issues, citing previous encounters between law enforcement and the suspect. The victims of the Waffle House shooting included Taurean Sanderlin, 29, a restaurant employee; Joe Perez, 20, a patron; DeEbony Groves, 21, a Belmont University student; and Akilah Dasilva, 23, a music video producer and student at Middle Tennessee State University. Four others sustained injuries, including James Shaw, who disarmed Reinking, and his girlfriend, Tia Waggoner, who underwent surgery for her wounds.

The FBI, along with local law enforcement agencies, joined the investigation, which spanned across multiple jurisdictions, including Tazewell County, Illinois, where Reinking's past behavior raised red flags. Sheriff Robert Huston in Tazewell County stated that deputies allowed Reinking's father to take possession of the guns on the promise that he would "keep the weapons secure and out of the possession of Travis." As the search for Reinking continued, authorities emphasized the importance of community vigilance, urging residents to report any sightings or information that might lead to the suspect's capture.

history.draft:
On Sunday, April 22, 2018, a mass shooting occurred at a Waffle House restaurant in Nashville, Tennessee, leaving four people dead and four others injured. The suspect, identified as Travis Reinking, 29, allegedly opened fire in the parking lot and then stormed the restaurant, targeting patrons and employees. According to eyewitnesses, Reinking was nearly naked, wearing only a green jacket and brandishing an assault-style rifle during the attack. A quick-thinking customer, later identified as James Shaw, wrestled the gun away from Reinking, preventing further loss of life.

The Metropolitan Nashville Police Department launched an extensive manhunt for Reinking, who was described as potentially armed and possibly hiding in a wooded area near an apartment complex where he resided. Police warned residents of the surrounding neighborhood to exercise caution, advising them to check on their neighbors and report any suspicious activity. Schools in the area were placed on lockdown, with students allowed to move freely within the buildings but denied entry to external visitors.

As the investigation unfolded, disturbing reports emerged about Reinking's troubled past, including incidents in his hometown of Morton, Illinois. In May 2016, Reinking claimed that pop star Taylor Swift was stalking him and hacking his phone, prompting a visit from Tazewell County deputies. Two years prior, in July 2016, Reinking was arrested by the US Secret Service after attempting to breach a restricted area near the White House, stating he wanted to meet President Donald Trump. Following the arrest, Reinking's state firearms card was revoked, and four guns, including an AR-15, were confiscated.

Reinking's father, Jeffrey, took possession of the guns under the condition that he would keep them secure and out of his son's reach. However, it remains unclear whether Reinking regained access to the firearms. Nashville Police Chief Steve Anderson expressed concerns that Reinking may have mental health issues, citing previous encounters between law enforcement and the suspect.

The victims of the Waffle House shooting included Taurean Sanderlin, 29, a restaurant employee; Joe Perez, 20, a patron; DeEbony Groves, 21, a Belmont University student; and Akilah Dasilva, 23, a music video producer and student at Middle Tennessee State University. Four others sustained injuries, including James Shaw, who disarmed Reinking, and his girlfriend, Tia Waggoner, who underwent surgery for her wounds. The FBI, along with local law enforcement agencies, joined the investigation, which spanned across multiple jurisdictions, including Tazewell County, Illinois, where Reinking's past behavior raised red flags.

As the search for Reinking continued, authorities emphasized the importance of community vigilance, urging residents to report any sightings or information that might lead to the suspect's capture. With Reinking still at large, the people of Nashville remained on high alert, awaiting the resolution of this tragic event.

reference: – Alleged Waffle House mass shooter Travis Reinking was still at large early Monday, and Nashville officials say schools will be in "lock-out" mode if he isn't found by the time classes begin, with guests and visitors not allowed into buildings, the AP reports. The 29-year-old, accused of killing four people in a rampage early Sunday, was last seen later in the day in a wooded area near his apartment complex, wearing only pants, say police, who have gone door-to-door in the area and warned residents to be on alert. According to police reports, relatives in Reinking's Illinois hometown say that since 2014, he has been having delusions that Taylor Swift is stalking him and dozens of unknown culprits are hacking into his phone and computer, the New York Daily News reports. According to a July 2016 police report from Peoria, Reinking told officers he had confronted the singer in a Dairy Queen parking lot. In his version of events, "Taylor climbed up the side of a building and Travis followed. However, when he reached the rooftop, Taylor was gone." In other incidents the next year, after he moved to Nashville, Reinking allegedly threatened a man with a semi-automatic rifle and barged into a community pool and jumped in wearing a pink housecoat. Police say Reinking's weapons, including the AR-15 used to kill four people at the Waffle House, were seized after he was arrested outside the White House last year, the Tennessean reports. The weapons were given to Reinking's father after he promised to keep them away from his son. Police believe the father later gave the four firearms back to his son. (A Waffle House customer managed to wrest the gun away from Reinking.)

final_summary:
Dzhokhar Tsarnaev is set to be formally sentenced to death for his role in the 2013 Boston Marathon bombings. Ahead of the sentencing hearing, scheduled for Wednesday, defense lawyers have remained tight-lipped about whether Tsarnaev plans to address the court before the death sentence is officially imposed. 

Tsarnaev's public statements have been limited to indirect expressions of remorse, primarily through Sister Helen Prejean, a death penalty opponent who met with him five times. However, prosecutors presented video footage showing Tsarnaev giving the middle finger to a security camera in a holding cell just months after the bombing. 

Legal experts caution that speaking up might not serve Tsarnaev well, referencing the precedent set by Timothy McVeigh, who was sentenced to death and later executed for the Oklahoma City bombing. Stephen Jones, who represented McVeigh, advises against making a statement unless it sounds authentic and natural, warning that anything else might come across as insincere. McVeigh himself chose to speak up during his sentencing hearing, quoting a dissenting opinion from Justice Louis Brandeis in the 1928 Supreme Court case Olmstead v. United States, stating, "Our Government is the potent, the omnipresent teacher. For good or for ill, it teaches the whole people by its example."

Other convicted terrorists have used their time to deliver statements, often with mixed results. Zacarias Moussaoui cursed America and praised Osama bin Laden during his sentencing for his role in the 9/11 attacks, while Abu Hamza proclaimed his innocence and complained about prison conditions. These examples highlight the risks of speaking up, as it can be perceived as too little, too late.

Massachusetts defense lawyer David Hoose believes that Tsarnaev should keep quiet, arguing that whatever he says will be seen as insufficient given the gravity of the crimes. On the other hand, New York Law School professor Robert Blecker suggests that expressing remorse could potentially aid Tsarnaev in his future appeals, particularly if he seeks a commutation of his death sentence from the president.

Over 20 victims of the Boston Marathon bombings, including family members of those who died and individuals who suffered injuries, are expected to testify during the sentencing hearing. Celeste Corcoran, a survivor of the attack, has stated that she and her daughter will not attend the hearing, deeming it unnecessary and emotionally taxing. As the nation awaits Tsarnaev's decision, many wonder whether he will choose to speak up or remain silent, a choice that could have significant implications for his future.

Hours ahead of the sentencing hearing, scheduled for Wednesday, defense lawyers have remained tight-lipped about whether Tsarnaev, now 21, plans to address the court before the death sentence is officially imposed.

history.draft:
According to The Associated Press, as Dzhokhar Tsarnaev prepares to be formally sentenced to death for his role in the 2013 Boston Marathon bombings, one question lingers: will he break his silence? Hours ahead of the sentencing hearing, scheduled for Wednesday, defense lawyers have remained tight-lipped about whether Tsarnaev, now 21, plans to address the court before the death sentence is officially imposed.

During the trial, the public only caught glimpses of Tsarnaev through indirect means, such as through Sister Helen Prejean, a death penalty opponent who met with him five times and reported that he expressed genuine remorse for the carnage that left three dead and over 200 injured. However, prosecutors countered with video footage showing Tsarnaev giving the middle finger to a security camera in a holding cell just months after the bombing.

Legal experts believe that speaking up might not serve Tsarnaev well, citing the precedent set by Timothy McVeigh, who was sentenced to death and later executed for the Oklahoma City bombing. Stephen Jones, who represented McVeigh, advises against making a statement unless it sounds authentic and natural, warning that anything else might come across as insincere. McVeigh himself chose to speak up during his sentencing hearing, quoting a dissenting opinion from Justice Louis Brandeis in the 1928 Supreme Court case Olmstead v. United States, stating, "Our Government is the potent, the omnipresent teacher. For good or for ill, it teaches the whole people by its example."

Other convicted terrorists have used their time to deliver diatribes, such as Zacarias Moussaui, who cursed America and praised Osama bin Laden during his sentencing for his role in the 9/11 attacks, and Abu Hamza, who proclaimed his innocence and complained about prison conditions. However, these examples highlight the risks of speaking up, as it can be perceived as too little, too late.

David Hoose, a Massachusetts defense lawyer, believes that Tsarnaev should keep quiet, arguing that whatever he says will be seen as insufficient given the gravity of the crimes. On the other hand, New York Law School professor Robert Blecker suggests that expressing remorse could potentially aid Tsarnaev in his future appeals, particularly if he seeks a commutation of his death sentence from the president.

More than 20 victims of the Boston Marathon bombings, including family members of those who died and individuals who suffered injuries, are expected to testify during the sentencing hearing. Celeste Corcoran, a survivor of the attack, has stated that she and her daughter will not attend the hearing, deeming it unnecessary and emotionally taxing. As the nation awaits Tsarnaev's decision, many wonder whether he will choose to speak up or remain silent, a choice that could have significant implications for his future.

McVeigh himself chose to speak up during his sentencing hearing, quoting a dissenting opinion from Justice Louis Brandeis in the 1928 Supreme Court case Olmstead v. United States, stating, "Our Government is the potent, the omnipresent teacher. For good or for ill, it teaches the whole people by its example."

Other convicted terrorists have used their time to deliver statements, often with mixed results. Zacarias Moussaoui cursed America and praised Osama bin Laden during his sentencing for his role in the 9/11 attacks, while Abu Hamza proclaimed his innocence and complained about prison conditions. These examples highlight the risks of speaking up, as it can be perceived as too little, too late.

reference: – A nun says Dzhokhar Tsarnaev expressed remorse, while prosecutors played video of him giving the finger to a security camera. But today, secondhand narratives and hand gestures may give way to words straight out of the Boston Marathon bomber's mouth during his sentencing, NBC News reports. Although defense lawyers are mum, the will-he-or-won't-he question has set off a debate over whether it's a smart idea for him to vocalize, say, his motives or apologies to victims' families, as the Los Angeles Times suggests. The lawyer who defended Timothy McVeigh, for one, thinks Tsarnaev shouldn't talk. "I would be very surprised if he speaks," he tells NBC. "Generally, I don't advise clients to make a statement unless they are articulate and it ... doesn't sound like it was written by lawyers." Adds another defense attorney, "I don't think there's anything to be gained by it at this point," he says. "Whatever would be said … would be viewed as too little, too late." But a law school prof tells CBS that Tsarnaev could take a long view: While an apology won't affect his sentence, it could give him leverage if he seeks to have his death sentence commuted by the president. Conversely, he could use his sentencing as a platform, like Zacarias Moussaoui, who issued taunts at his 2006 sentencing for conspiring on the 9/11 attacks: "God curse America. And God save Osama bin Laden. You will never get him." Who will likely be speaking today: bombing victims and their families, who will have the chance to give impact statements, the Times notes. (Tsarnaev didn't testify at his trial, but he did cry during one person's testimony.)
